\boxed{M_{\text{single-loop planar}} = 2L - 1}
$$

where $L$ is the number of **independent loops**. For a four-bar, $L = 1$, so $M = 2(1) - 1 = 1$ ✓. For a Watt or Stephenson six-bar, $L = 2$, so $M = 2(2) - 1 = 3$ (these have multiple DOF and require multiple inputs).

**Step 7 — Cross-Validation with Kutzbach**
For simple mechanisms like the four-bar, the **Kutzbach criterion (M ≥ 0)** is a necessary condition for motion. A negative value indicates a **locked structure** with redundant constraints. The Kutzbach inequality is therefore often written as:

$$
M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \geq 0
$$

for the chain to be capable of motion, and $M = 1$ specifically for a useful single-input mechanism.

### 3.2 Derivation of Independent Loops ($L$)

**Starting Premise:** A kinematic chain with $n$ moving links and $j$ single-DOF joints.

**Step 1 — Each joint connects exactly 2 links.** The number of "link ends" participating in joints is $2j$ (because each joint involves 2 links).

**Step 2 — Subtract the "tree" structure.** A tree graph with $n$ nodes has exactly $n-1$ edges. Any additional edge creates a loop.

**Step 3 — The number of loops is therefore:**

$$
L = j - (n-1) = j - n + 1
$$

$$
\boxed{L = j - n + 1}
$$

**Step 4 — Single-Loop Verification**
For a four-bar: $j = 4$ joints, $n = 3$ moving links.

$$
L = 4 - 3 + 1 = 2 \quad \text{(appears wrong)}
$$

Re-evaluating: The fixed frame is **also** a link in the loop-counting sense. With $n_{\text{total}} = 4$ (including frame), and joints $j = 4$:

$$
L = j - n_{\text{total}} + 1 = 4 - 4 + 1 = 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

This is the **correct interpretation** — include the fixed frame as a "link" for loop counting.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: DOF of Common Mechanisms

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is the **most-frequently-tested table** in KTU ESE questions on Module 1.

| Sl. No. | Mechanism | $n$ (moving) | $j_1$ (1-DOF joints) | $j_2$ (2-DOF joints) | $L$ (loops) | Mobility $M$ | Valid? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Four-bar chain | 3 | 4 R | 0 | 1 | $3(2) - 8 = -2$ | Locked (use loop formula: $M = 2L - 1 = 1$) |
| 2 | Slider-crank | 3 | 3R + 1P | 0 | 1 | $3(2) - 2(4) = -2$ | Locked (loop: $M = 1$) |
| 3 | Double slider-crank | 3 | 2R + 2P | 0 | 1 | $3(2) - 8 = -2$ | Locked (loop: $M = 1$) |
| 4 | Six-bar Watt | 5 | 6 | 0 | 2 | $3(4) - 12 = 0$ | Structure (loop: $M = 2(2) - 1 = 3$) |
| 5 | Six-bar Stephenson | 5 | 6 | 0 | 2 | $3(4) - 12 = 0$ | Structure (loop: $M = 3$) |
| 6 | Cam-follower (with roller) | 2 | 2 (cam pivot + follower slider) | 1 (cam-roller contact) | 1 | $3(1) - 2(2) - 1 = -2$ | (loop: $M = 2$) |
| 7 | Geneva mechanism | 4 | 4 | 1 | 1 | $3(3) - 8 - 1 = 0$ | Indexed motion |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** The Kutzbach equation $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ gives **incorrect** or **negative** values for simple single-loop planar mechanisms because it does not account for **special geometric configurations** (passive DOF, redundant constraints, special geometry). For planar single-loop chains, the **correct** formula is $M = 2L - 1$ (Gruebler's planar loop formula). Kutzbach's equation is a **necessary condition** (no motion if $M < 0$), not a **sufficient condition** for motion.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A mechanism has 6 moving links, 7 revolute pairs, 1 prismatic pair, and 1 cam pair (with roller follower). Determine whether it is a mechanism, structure, or locked chain.

**Solution:**

$$
n = 6, \quad j_1 = 7(\text{R}) + 1(\text{P}) = 8, \quad j_2 = 1 \text{ (cam pair)}
$$

$$
M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 = 3(6-1) - 2(8) - 1 = 15 - 16 - 1 = -2
$$

Since $M < 0$, the chain is **locked** — there are too many constraints for the number of links. To make it a mechanism ($M=1$), we must either **add a link** or **remove two single-DOF joints**.

**Adjusted design:** Remove 1 revolute joint → $j_1 = 7$, $j_2 = 1$:

$$
M = 3(5) - 2(7) - 1 = 15 - 14 - 1 = 0 \quad \text{(Structure)}
$$

Remove **two** revolute joints → $j_1 = 6$:

$$
M = 15 - 12 - 1 = 2 \quad \text{(Two inputs needed)}
$$

To get a single-input mechanism, we need $M = 1$:

$$
3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 = 1
$$

With $n = 6, j_2 = 1$: $15 - 2j_1 - 1 = 1 \Rightarrow j_1 = 6.5$ — non-integer, so impossible. Hence the design needs modification by changing a joint type or removing a link.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Kinematic Chain Classification

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **classification hierarchy** used to identify and analyze any kinematic chain. This is a block-level functional diagram, not a physical shape diagram, because the actual physical drawings of mechanisms (which can be hand-sketched in the exam) are not well-suited to Mermaid rendering.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Any Mechanical System] --> B[Identify Links n]
    B --> C[Identify Joints j]
    C --> D{Contact Type}
    D -->|Surface Contact| E[Lower Pair]
    D -->|Point/Line Contact| F[Higher Pair]
    E --> G{Motion Type}
    G -->|Pure Rotation| H[Revolute Pair R - 1 DOF]
    G -->|Pure Translation| I[Prismatic Pair P - 1 DOF]
    G -->|Rotation plus Translation| J[Cylindrical Pair - 2 DOF]
    G -->|3 Rotations| K[Spherical Pair - 3 DOF]
    F --> L{Contact Geometry}
    L -->|Cam plus Follower| M[Cam Pair - 2 DOF]
    L -->|Gear Teeth Mesh| N[Gear Pair - 1 DOF]
    L -->|Ball on Race| O[Rolling Pair - 1 DOF]
    H --> P[Apply Kutzbach Criterion]
    I --> P
    J --> P
    K --> P
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Compute Mobility M]
    Q --> R{M Value}
    R -->|M equals 1| S[SINGLE INPUT MECHANISM]
    R -->|M greater than 1| T[MULTI INPUT MECHANISM]
    R -->|M equals 0| U[STRUCTURE No Motion]
    R -->|M less than 0| V[LOCKED CHAIN Redundant]
    S --> W[Classify Chain Topology]
    T --> W
    W --> X1[Four Bar Chain]
    W --> X2[Slider Crank Chain]
    W --> X3[Double Slider Crank]
    W --> X4[Six Bar Watt]
    W --> X5[Six Bar Stephenson]
    W --> X6[Cam Follower System]
    X1 --> Y[Apply Inversion Theorem]
    X2 --> Y
    X3 --> Y
    X4 --> Y
    X5 --> Y
    X6 --> Y
    Y --> Z[Final Mechanism Identification]
```

### 4.2 Schematic of a Four-Bar Chain (Logical Block Form)

The following diagram shows the **logical structure** of a four-bar kinematic chain with four revolute pairs. In KTU exams, students are expected to draw the **actual physical schematic** by hand; the block diagram below is for **classification understanding only**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Link 1: Fixed Frame] -->|Revolute Pair O2| B[Link 2: Crank Input]
    A -->|Revolute Pair O4| D[Link 4: Rocker Output]
    B -->|Revolute Pair A| C[Link 3: Coupler]
    C -->|Revolute Pair B| D
    A -.->|Fixed Pivot| A
    B -->|Rotates about O2| B
    D -->|Oscillates about O4| D
    C -->|General Plane Motion| C
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Link 1 (frame)** is the fixed reference. It has two pivot joints (O2 and O4).
- **Link 2 (crank)** rotates fully about O2 if Grashof's law permits ($S + L \leq P + Q$).
- **Link 4 (rocker)** oscillates between two angular limits.
- **Link 3 (coupler)** undergoes **general plane motion** (both translation and rotation).
- All four joints are **lower pairs (revolute)** — surface contact via pins.

### 4.3 Schematic of a Slider-Crank Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Link 1: Fixed Frame Cylinder] -->|Revolute O| B[Link 2: Crank]
    A -->|Prismatic Joint| C[Link 3: Slider Piston]
    B -->|Revolute A| C
    A -.->|Cylinder Bore Guide| C
    B -.->|Rotates about O| B
    C -.->|Translates Linearly| C
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Link 1 (cylinder block)** is fixed. It provides the **prismatic pair** to the slider and the **revolute pair** (crank pivot) to the crank.
- **Link 2 (crank)** rotates about the fixed pivot O.
- **Link 3 (slider/piston)** translates linearly in the cylinder, connected to the crank by a pin joint.
- This is the **fundamental IC engine** topology — converting rotary motion to linear reciprocation.

### 4.4 Schematic of Kinematic Pair Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PAIRS[Kinematic Pairs] --> A[By Contact Type]
    PAIRS --> B[By Relative Motion]
    A --> A1[Lower Pair - Area Contact]
    A --> A2[Higher Pair - Point or Line Contact]
    A1 --> A1a[Sliding Type]
    A1 --> A1b[Rolling Type]
    A1 --> A1c[Screw Type]
    A2 --> A2a[Cam Pair]
    A2 --> A2b[Gear Pair]
    A2 --> A2c[Ball Bearing]
    B --> B1[1 DOF Pairs]
    B --> B2[2 DOF Pairs]
    B --> B3[3 DOF Pairs]
    B1 --> B1a[Revolute R]
    B1 --> B1b[Prismatic P]
    B1 --> B1c[Helical S]
    B1 --> B1d[Rolling]
    B2 --> B2a[Cylindrical]
    B2 --> B2b[Planar]
    B2 --> B2c[Cam with Flat Follower]
    B3 --> B3a[Spherical Ball and Socket]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]**
**Define the following: (i) Kinematic link, (ii) Kinematic pair, (iii) Kinematic chain. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Kinematic Link:** A kinematic link is a **resistant body** (rigid body under ideal conditions) or an assembly of resistant bodies, having **relative motion** with respect to other links. *Example:* The connecting rod of an IC engine — it has relative motion with the piston and the crank.

**(ii) Kinematic Pair:** A kinematic pair is a **joint between two kinematic links** that allows **constrained relative motion** between them. *Example:* A revolute pair formed by the piston pin and the small end of the connecting rod.

**(iii) Kinematic Chain:** A kinematic chain is an **assembled system of kinematic links connected by kinematic pairs** such that **definite relative motion** is possible. When one link is fixed, it becomes a **mechanism**. *Example:* The piston–connecting rod–crank assembly of a reciprocating engine.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]**
**Differentiate between a lower pair and a higher pair with two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Lower Pair | Higher Pair |
|---|---|---|
| **Contact Nature** | Surface contact (area contact) | Point or line contact |
| **Load Capacity** | High (full surface load distribution) | Low (concentrated stress) |
| **Lubrication** | Easy — oil film supported | Difficult — boundary lubrication |
| **Slip** | Less prone to slip | More prone to slip |
| **Examples** | (1) Piston in cylinder (Prismatic), (2) Shaft in journal bearing (Revolute) | (1) Cam-follower, (2) Gear teeth in mesh |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout (Common Mark-Loss Areas):**
> 1. **Confusing $n$ (moving links) with total links** — *Always* exclude the fixed frame from $n$. Forgetting this gives the wrong DOF.
> 2. **Sign errors in Kutzbach equation** — The formula is $3(n-1)$, not $3n - 1$. Off-by-one errors cost 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Failing to distinguish joint DOF (1-DOF vs 2-DOF)** — A cam pair is $j_2$, not $j_1$. Mixing them yields wrong answers.
> 4. **Not drawing the kinematic diagram** — KTU examiners *expect* a labeled diagram in any 7+ mark problem. A solution without a diagram loses at least 2 marks even if the math is correct.
> 5. **Forgetting the unit of mobility** — Mobility is **dimensionless**; do not write "radians" or "mm".

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a) (7 Marks)** *What are the different types of kinematic links? Classify them with suitable examples. Explain the terms "binary link," "ternary link," and "quaternary link" with neat sketches.*

**(b) (7 Marks)** *A four-bar chain has links of lengths 30 mm, 60 mm, 80 mm, and 100 mm. Classify the chain using Grashof's law and identify which link can be the crank.*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Solution:**

**Definition of Kinematic Link:**
A kinematic link is a **resistant body** (or rigid body) that has **relative motion** with respect to other bodies, connected by kinematic pairs.

**Types of Links (based on number of joints):**

**(1) Binary Link — 2 Joints:**
- A link that has **two kinematic pairs** connecting it to other links.
- *Examples:* Connecting rod (joints with piston pin and crank pin), crank, slider, rocker, lever.

**Sketch of a binary link:**
```
   O-----A-----B
   ^            ^
   |            |
 Joint 1      Joint 2
   |            |
 Connects     Connects
   to          to
 Link X       Link Y
```

**(2) Ternary Link — 3 Joints:**
- A link that has **three kinematic pairs** connecting it to other links.
- The link itself is **rigid** (e.g., a triangular plate), so the three sub-members do not move relative to each other.
- *Examples:* Triangular plate in a four-bar chain, bell-crank lever with 3 pivot points, the coupler of certain six-bar mechanisms.

**Sketch of a ternary link:**
```
        A
       / \
      /   \
     /     \
    B-------C
    ^   ^   ^
    |   |   |
   Jnt1 Jnt2 Jnt3
```

**(3) Quaternary Link — 4 Joints:**
- A link that has **four kinematic pairs** connecting it to other links.
- *Examples:* The fixed frame in a four-bar chain has 2 joints, but in a six-bar Watt chain, the frame has 3 joints. The crosshead in a slider-crank mechanism acts as a quaternary link when additional guides are present.
- Most commonly, the **base/frame** of complex mechanisms.

**Key Point:** A ternary or quaternary link is **counted as one link** in mobility equations because the relative motion between its constituent members is **zero** (they form a single rigid body).

**[7 marks — Complete]**

---

**(b) Solution:**

**Given:** Link lengths: $a = 30$ mm, $b = 60$ mm, $c = 80$ mm, $d = 100$ mm.

**Grashof's Law for Four-Bar Chains:**

$$
S + L \leq P + Q
$$

where $S$ = shortest link, $L$ = longest link, $P$ and $Q$ = the other two links.

**Identify the links:**

$$
S = 30 \text{ mm}, \quad L = 100 \text{ mm}, \quad P = 60 \text{ mm}, \quad Q = 80 \text{ mm}
$$

**Compute LHS and RHS:**

$$
S + L = 30 + 100 = 130 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
P + Q = 60 + 80 = 140 \text{ mm}
$$

**Check Grashof's Inequality:**

$$
130 \leq 140 \quad \checkmark
$$

The chain **satisfies Grashof's law** — it is a **Grashof chain**.

**Determine the type based on the shortest link's position:**

- The **shortest link (30 mm)** is adjacent to the **fixed frame** (assumed position of the 30 mm link in a standard four-bar).
- **Result:** The link adjacent to the frame that is the shortest can make **complete rotation** — it is the **crank**.
- The link opposite the shortest (the 100 mm link) will be the **rocker** (oscillates).
- The remaining two links (60 mm and 80 mm) are the **coupler** and the other **side link**.

**Conclusion:** The 30 mm link is the **crank** (full 360° rotation), and the 100 mm link is the **rocker** (oscillation). The mechanism is a **crank-rocker mechanism** (commonly used in shaper machines and beam engines).

**[7 marks — Complete]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]

**(a) (7 Marks)** *State and explain Kutzbach's criterion for determining the degrees of freedom of a planar mechanism. Why is it considered only a necessary condition and not a sufficient one?*

**(b) (7 Marks)** *A planar mechanism consists of 8 links (including the frame) connected by 10 lower pairs and 1 higher pair. Determine the number of degrees of freedom. Is it a mechanism, structure, or locked chain?*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Solution:**

**Statement of Kutzbach's Criterion (Planar):**

> *The mobility (degrees of freedom) of a planar mechanism is given by:*
>
> $$M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$
>
> *where $n$ is the number of moving links, $j_1$ is the number of single-DOF joints, and $j_2$ is the number of two-DOF joints.*

**Explanation of Each Term:**

**(1) $3(n-1)$ — Unconstrained DOF:**
- Each of the $n$ moving links would have 3 DOF in a plane (2 translations, 1 rotation) if it were free.
- Subtracting 3 accounts for the fixed frame's own DOF (which we do not count).

**(2) $2j_1$ — Single-DOF Joint Constraint:**
- Each single-DOF joint (Revolute R, Prismatic P) eliminates **2 of the 3** planar DOF of the two connected links, because it allows only 1 relative motion.

**(3) $j_2$ — Two-DOF Joint Constraint:**
- Each two-DOF joint (e.g., cam pair in 2-D, planar pair) eliminates only **1** of the 3 DOF, because it allows 2 relative motions.

**Why Kutzbach is Necessary but NOT Sufficient:**

Kutzbach's criterion gives the **theoretical mobility** assuming **all joints are independent and no special geometry exists**. However, in practice:

1. **Special Geometry (Passive DOF):** Some links may have **zero velocity at a particular instant** (e.g., the instantaneous center of a four-bar link lying on its own axis). This creates **passive DOF** that Kutzbach does not detect.

2. **Redundant Constraints:** Identical constraints from multiple joints can be **mathematically redundant** (e.g., a parallelogram four-bar), but Kutzbach counts each joint separately.

3. **Identical Links in Special Configuration:** A diamond four-bar has special positions where it gains an extra DOF (change-point) not predicted by Kutzbach.

**Conclusion:** $M \geq 0$ is **necessary** (no motion if $M < 0$), but $M = 1$ is **not sufficient** to guarantee that the chain is a useful mechanism. **Geometric analysis is always required.**

**[7 marks — Complete]**

---

**(b) Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Total links = 8 (including fixed frame)
- Lower pairs = 10 (all 1-DOF joints: R or P)
- Higher pair = 1 (assume cam pair, which is a 2-DOF joint in 2-D)
- Planar mechanism

**Step 1 — Find number of moving links:**

$$
n = \text{Total links} - 1 \text{ (fixed frame)} = 8 - 1 = 7
$$

**Step 2 — Classify the joints:**

- Lower pairs: 10 single-DOF joints → $j_1 = 10$
- Higher pair (cam): 1 two-DOF joint → $j_2 = 1$

**Step 3 — Apply Kutzbach's Equation:**

$$
M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

$$
M = 3(7-1) - 2(10) - 1
$$

$$
M = 3(6) - 20 - 1
$$

$$
M = 18 - 21 = -3
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the Result:**

Since $M = -3 < 0$, the chain is **over-constrained** and forms a **LOCKED CHAIN** (or a rigid structure with redundant constraints). It has **no relative motion possible**.

**Step 5 — Modification for a Mechanism:**

To convert this into a single-input mechanism ($M = 1$), we need to add $\Delta M = 1 - (-3) = 4$ DOF. This can be done by:
- **Removing 2 single-DOF joints** (each adds +2 DOF): $M = -3 + 4 = 1$ ✓
- **OR removing 1 single-DOF and 1 two-DOF joint** (adds +3, not enough)
- **OR adding 1 link and 2 single-DOF joints** (adds +1 DOF net): $M = -3 + 3 - 2 = -2$ (still locked)

**Conclusion:** The original design is a **locked chain**. By removing 2 lower pairs, it becomes a single-input mechanism.

**[7 marks — Complete]**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **The 20 Golden Points for Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision:**

- [x] **Kinematic link** = rigid body with relative motion. Classify by **number of joints**: Binary (2), Ternary (3), Quaternary (4).
- [x] **Kinematic pair** = joint between two links. **Lower pair** = area contact. **Higher pair** = point/line contact.
- [x] **Revolute (R) = 1 DOF** (rotation). **Prismatic (P) = 1 DOF** (translation). Both are 1-DOF joints ($j_1$).
- [x] **Cylindrical pair = 2 DOF**. **Spherical pair = 3 DOF**. **Planar pair = 3 DOF** (in 3-D).
- [x] **Kinematic chain** = links + pairs. **Mechanism** = chain with one link **fixed**.
- [x] **Kutzbach (Planar):** $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ — **necessary** condition only.
- [x] **Grashof's Law:** $S + L \leq P + Q$ for at least one link to fully rotate.
- [x] **Crank-rocker** = shortest link adjacent to frame, satisfies Grashof.
- [x] **Double-crank** = shortest link is the **frame** (frame rotates? — no, in 4-bar the frame is fixed; double-crank is when shortest link is the ground link and opposite links rotate fully).
- [x] **Double-rocker (Grashof)** = shortest link is the **coupler**; no link rotates fully.
- [x] **Non-Grashof** = $S + L > P + Q$ → triple-rocker, no full rotation.
- [x] **Loops:** $L = j - n_{\text{total}} + 1$. Four-bar has $L = 1$. Six-bar has $L = 2$.
- [x] **Structure:** $M = 0$ — no motion. **Mechanism:** $M = 1$ — single input. **Locked:** $M < 0$.
- [x] **Four-bar chain** = 4 R-joints, 1 DOF (mechanism).
- [x] **Slider-crank** = 3R + 1P, 1 DOF.
- [x] **Double slider-crank** = 2R + 2P, 1 DOF.
- [x] **Cam-follower with roller** = 2-DOF joint between cam and roller; the roller rotates passively.
- [x] **Inversions** = fixing different links of a kinematic chain gives different mechanisms. For a 4-bar chain, there are **3 useful inversions** (4 - 1 = 3, since one link must remain fixed).
- [x] **Higher pairs** are essential in **gears, cams, and bearings**, and are typically **less durable** but **more accurate** in motion transmission.
- [x] **KTU exam tip:** Always draw the **kinematic diagram** with **labeled links and joints** before applying Kutzbach — it earns easy 2 marks and prevents counting errors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinematic Inversions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT412 - KINEMATICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Kinematic Inversions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kinematic Inversion** is the systematic method of obtaining **new mechanisms** with different motion characteristics by **fixing (grounding) different links** of an original kinematic chain, one at a time, while keeping the relative dimensions of the links unchanged.

A kinematic chain is said to be **inverted** when the relative motions of its links are changed by fixing different links as the frame (ground). The number of possible inversions of a kinematic chain is equal to the **number of links** present in the chain.

### Mathematical Statement of Inversions
If a kinematic chain consists of $n$ links, then the number of distinct inversions possible is given by:

$$N_{inversions} = n$$

However, **one of these inversions is considered trivial** (the original configuration), leaving $(n-1)$ **useful inversions** that yield practically distinct mechanisms.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Origami Link" Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a kinematic chain like a chain of paper clips linked together.** If you pin (fix) the *first* clip to a board, the second clip swings around it. If instead you pin the *middle* clip, the entire configuration behaves completely differently — even though the clips themselves haven't changed.

This is exactly what happens in **kinematic inversion**:
- The **links (shape and size)** stay the same.
- The **ground link (frame) changes**.
- The **resulting motion of the output link changes completely**.

> [!TIP]
> **The art of a Mechanical Engineer:** Many practical machines (engines, shapers, quick-return mechanisms) are born from this single clever trick — picking the *right link* to fix.

## 1.3 Why Inversions Matter in KTU Context

In KTU 2024 scheme examinations, kinematic inversions form the **backbone of Module 1** because:

1. They form the **basis of all real-world machines** (IC engines, shaping machines, pumps, etc.)
2. They are the **direct application of degrees of freedom (DOF)** concepts.
3. They frequently appear in **Part B (14-mark)** questions involving velocity/acceleration analysis.

---

## 1.4 Fundamental Terminology Used in Inversions

| Term | Meaning | KTU Standard Notation |
|------|---------|------------------------|
| Frame (Fixed Link) | The link that is grounded/held stationary | Link 1 (typically) |
| Crank | A link making complete rotation | $r$ |
| Slotted Lever / Oscillating Arm | A link oscillating about a fixed pivot | $L$ |
| Slider | A link reciprocating along a straight path | — |
| Connecting Rod (Coupler) | Link connecting crank and slider/lever | $l$ |
| Whitworth Disc | Special rotating disc in quick-return mechanisms | — |
| Quick Return Ratio (QRR) | Ratio of time of cutting to time of return stroke | $Q$ |

---

## 1.5 The Three Parent Chains Used in Inversions

All practical inversions in KTU syllabus are derived from only **three parent chains**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Foundational Chains:**
> 1. **Four-Bar Chain (Quadric Chain)** — 4 links, all revolute
> 2. **Single Slider Crank Chain** — 4 links, 3 revolute + 1 prismatic
> 3. **Double Slider Crank Chain** — 4 links, 2 revolute + 2 prismatic

All practical mechanisms (beam engine, Whitworth, crank-shaper, elliptical trammel, Scotch yoke, etc.) are **inversions of these three parent chains**.

---

## 1.6 KTU 2024 Highlight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECPT412, Module 1):** Students must be able to *draw, identify, and explain* at least the following inversions:
> - **Four-bar chain:** Beam engine, Coupling rod of locomotive, Watt's straight-line, Pantograph
> - **Single slider crank:** Pendulum pump / Bull engine, Oscillating cylinder engine, Crank & slotted lever, Whitworth quick-return
> - **Double slider crank:** Elliptical trammel, Scotch yoke, Oldham's coupling

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Inversions of the Four-Bar Chain

The four-bar chain has **4 links**. Fixing each link in turn gives **4 inversions**, but the original configuration (where one link is a fixed pivot pair) is considered the **standard four-bar linkage**.

### 2.1.1 First Inversion — Beam Engine Mechanism (Watt's Beam Engine)
- **Fixed link:** Ground link (the cylinder pivot-base line)
- **Application:** Original Watt steam engine
- **Mechanism:** A beam oscillates about a central pivot. One end of the beam is connected to the piston rod, the other to the connecting rod which drives the crank-flywheel.
- **Output:** Continuous rotation of the crank from reciprocating piston motion.

### 2.1.2 Second Inversion — Coupling Rod of Locomotive
- **Fixed link:** The **coupling rod (long link)**
- **Application:** Steam/diesel locomotives
- **Mechanism:** Two cranks of equal length rotate at the same speed. They are connected by the fixed coupling rod. The wheels are constrained to move in perfect synchrony.
- **Purpose:** Couples multiple driving wheels so they rotate as a unit.

### 2.1.3 Third Inversion — Watt's Straight-Line Mechanism
- **Fixed link:** A non-adjacent short link
- **Application:** Historically used to guide pistons in approximately straight line
- **Mechanism:** A point on the coupler traces an approximate straight line (figure-8 or lemniscate).

### 2.1.4 Fourth Inversion — Pantograph
- **Fixed link:** A non-adjacent link at one end
- **Application:** Used for copying drawings, letters, signatures at scaled-up or scaled-down sizes
- **Mechanism:** A four-bar pantograph linkage that maintains similarity of shape as it traces a path.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick (KTU exam):** "**B-C-W-P**" — **B**eam, **C**oupling rod, **W**att's straight line, **P**antograph.

---

## 2.2 Inversions of the Single Slider Crank Chain

The single slider crank chain has **4 links**. One of them is a slider (prismatic pair). The **useful inversions** are:

### 2.2.1 First Inversion — Pendulum Pump (Bull Engine)
- **Fixed link:** The **crank**
- **Mechanism:** The slider (piston) reciprocates as the connecting rod oscillates. Used in oil pumps, water pumps.
- **Output:** Reciprocating motion of slider.

### 2.2.2 Second Inversion — Oscillating Cylinder Engine
- **Fixed link:** The **slider (piston)**
- **Application:** Some early steam engines
- **Mechanism:** The cylinder oscillates about a pivot while the piston reciprocates within it.

### 2.2.3 Third Inversion — Crank and Slotted Lever (Quick Return Mechanism)
- **Fixed link:** The **slider**
- **Application:** **Shaping machines, slotting machines**
- **Mechanism:** A slotted lever oscillates about a fixed pivot. A crank pin engages the slot. The crank rotates uniformly, but the slotted lever oscillates with **slow forward stroke** and **fast return stroke**.
- **Quick Return Ratio (QRR):**

$$Q = \frac{T_{cutting}}{T_{return}} = \frac{\pi + 2\alpha}{\pi - 2\alpha}$$

where $\alpha$ is the angle between the axis of the slot (lever) at the extreme positions and the perpendicular to the line of stroke. $\alpha$ is called the **angle of advance**.

### 2.2.4 Fourth Inversion — Whitworth Quick Return Mechanism
- **Fixed link:** The **small link (coupling) between the crank and the slotted lever**
- **Application:** High-speed shaping and planing machines
- **Mechanism:** A small crank (driven by a large gear/spur) engages a slotted lever. The driving gear rotates uniformly; the slotted lever oscillates.
- **QRR:**

$$Q = \frac{180^\circ + 2\beta}{180^\circ - 2\beta}$$

where $2\beta$ is the **angle of advance** (the angle through which the driving crank rotates during the return stroke).

---

## 2.3 Inversions of the Double Slider Crank Chain

The double slider crank chain has **4 links** with **2 prismatic pairs** and **2 revolute pairs**.

### 2.3.1 First Inversion — Elliptical Trammel
- **Mechanism:** Two sliders move along two perpendicular straight guides. A rigid frame (coupler) connects them. Any point on the coupler (not on the slider axes) traces an **ellipse**.
- **Application:** Used to draw ellipses; historically used to cut elliptical gears and elliptical holes.

### 2.3.2 Second Inversion — Scotch Yoke
- **Mechanism:** A pin on a rotating crank engages a slot in a sliding yoke. The yoke reciprocates with simple harmonic motion.
- **Application:** Used in some punch presses and valve actuators where SHM is desired.

### 2.3.3 Third Inversion — Oldham's Coupling
- **Application:** Connects two parallel but **slightly misaligned shafts**.
- **Mechanism:** A central disc with two perpendicular tongues engages slots on two flanges attached to the misaligned shafts. As shafts rotate, the central disc accommodates the misalignment by sliding within the slots.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas for Module 1 (PECPT412):**

| # | Mechanism / Concept | Key Formula | Description |
|---|---------------------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Crank & Slotted Lever QRR** | $Q = \dfrac{\pi + 2\alpha}{\pi - 2\alpha}$ | $\alpha$ = angle of advance |
| 2 | **Whitworth QRR** | $Q = \dfrac{180^\circ + 2\beta}{180^\circ - 2\beta}$ | $2\beta$ = angle of advance |
| 3 | **Length of stroke in C&S Lever** | $L_s = (r_1 + r_2) - (r_1 - r_2) = 2r_2$ | Stroke = $2 \times$ length of slotted lever beyond crank center |
| 4 | **Slider velocity (C&S Lever)** | $v_s = \omega \cdot r \cdot \cos\theta \cdot \dfrac{\cos\alpha}{\cos^2\phi}$ | Complex — see derivation in Section 3 |
| 5 | **Slider acceleration (C&S Lever)** | $a_s = \omega^2 \cdot r \cdot \left[\dfrac{\cos\alpha}{\cos\phi} + \dfrac{\sin^2\alpha \tan\phi}{\cos^2\phi}\right]$ | For KTU advanced problems |
| 6 | **Whitworth — angle of advance relation** | $2\beta = \dfrac{(Q-1)}{(Q+1)} \times 180^\circ$ | Useful for design problems |
| 7 | **Number of inversions** | $N = n$ (number of links) | For an $n$-link chain |
| 8 | **Whitworth design relation** | $\tan\beta = \dfrac{R}{L}$ | $R$ = driving crank radius, $L$ = driving gear center distance |
| 9 | **C&S Lever — $\tan\phi$ relation** | $\tan\phi = \dfrac{r \sin\theta}{L - r\cos\theta}$ | Slot angle $\phi$ with vertical |
| 10 | **Elliptical Trammel equation** | $\dfrac{x^2}{a^2} + \dfrac{y^2}{b^2} = 1$ | Traced by a point on coupler |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where do these inversions live in industry?**
> - **Crank & Slotted Lever:** Found in **shaper machines** (HMT, BFW) for metal cutting. The slow forward stroke allows proper cutting; the fast return saves cycle time.
> - **Whitworth Quick Return:** Used in **high-speed shaping machines** and **planers** where cycle time is critical.
> - **Oldham's Coupling:** Used in **motor-generator sets** and **CNC machines** to absorb parallel misalignment between shafts.
> - **Pantograph:** Used in **plotters, EDM machines, and engraving systems**.
> - **Elliptical Trammel:** Used in **optical instruments** and **gear-cutting machines**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Quick Return Ratio for Crank & Slotted Lever Mechanism

### 3.1.1 Setup and Geometry

Consider a crank and slotted lever mechanism used in a shaping machine:
- $O_1$ = Center of the crank (fixed pivot of the slotted lever)
- $O_2$ = Center of the crank (the crank rotates about this)
- $r$ = Length of the crank
- $L$ = Distance between $O_1$ and $O_2$ (center distance)
- $\alpha$ = **Angle of advance** (angle between the line of stroke and the lever position at the extreme ends)

### 3.1.2 Identification of Forward and Return Strokes

The crank rotates at **uniform angular velocity** $\omega$. As the crank rotates:

- The **forward (cutting) stroke** corresponds to the crank rotating from one extreme position to the other through an angle of $(180^\circ + 2\alpha)$ in degrees, or $(\pi + 2\alpha)$ in radians.
- The **return stroke** corresponds to the crank rotating through an angle of $(180^\circ - 2\alpha)$ in degrees, or $(\pi - 2\alpha)$ in radians.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the asymmetry?** Because the extreme positions of the lever are NOT perpendicular to the line of stroke — they are tilted by $\pm \alpha$. So the crank must rotate MORE than 180° to complete the cutting stroke and LESS than 180° to complete the return stroke.

### 3.1.3 Time of Cutting and Return Strokes

Since the crank rotates at constant $\omega$:

$$T_{cutting} = \frac{\pi + 2\alpha}{\omega}$$

$$T_{return} = \frac{\pi - 2\alpha}{\omega}$$

### 3.1.4 Quick Return Ratio (QRR)

By definition, QRR is the ratio of time of cutting to time of return:

$$Q = \frac{T_{cutting}}{T_{return}}$$

Substituting:

$$Q = \frac{\dfrac{\pi + 2\alpha}{\omega}}{\dfrac{\pi - 2\alpha}{\omega}}$$

The $\omega$ cancels out:

$$Q = \frac{\pi + 2\alpha}{\pi - 2\alpha}$$

### 3.1.5 Final Result

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi + 2\alpha}{\pi - 2\alpha}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** When $\alpha = 0$, $Q = 1$ (no quick return — uniform motion). As $\alpha$ increases, $Q$ increases, meaning the cutting stroke takes even longer compared to return.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Whitworth Quick Return Mechanism — QRR and Design Relation

### 3.2.1 Setup

The Whitworth quick return mechanism has:
- A **driving crank** of length $R$ rotating about center $O$
- A **driven slotted lever** oscillating about a fixed pivot $O_1$
- Distance between $O$ and $O_1$ = $L$ (center distance)

### 3.2.2 Geometry at Extreme Position

At the **extreme position of the slotted lever**, the crank and the lever are aligned (collinear). The extreme positions of the lever are symmetric about the vertical line $O_1O$.

From the geometry of the right triangle formed at the extreme position:

$$\tan\beta = \frac{R}{L}$$

where $2\beta$ is the **total angle of advance** (the angle through which the driving crank rotates during the return stroke).

### 3.2.3 Quick Return Ratio

In one complete cycle, the crank rotates $360^\circ$. The return stroke corresponds to $2\beta$ rotation, and the cutting stroke to $(360^\circ - 2\beta)$.

$$T_{return} \propto 2\beta, \quad T_{cutting} \propto (360^\circ - 2\beta)$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{360^\circ - 2\beta}{2\beta} = \frac{180^\circ - \beta}{\beta}}$$

Or equivalently (using full circle convention):

$$Q = \frac{180^\circ + \beta}{180^\circ - \beta}$$

depending on the textbook's definition of $2\beta$ (whether it represents the return angle alone or the cutting angle).

### 3.2.4 Inverse Design Formula

If $Q$ is given and $2\beta$ needs to be found:

$$2\beta = \frac{(Q - 1)}{(Q + 1)} \times 180^\circ$$

This is useful in **design problems** where a specific QRR is required.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Length of Stroke in Crank & Slotted Lever

### 3.3.1 Setup

Let:
- $O_1$ = fixed pivot of slotted lever
- $O_2$ = crank center
- $L$ = $O_1 O_2$ distance
- $r$ = crank length
- $R$ = length of slotted lever from $O_1$ to the line of stroke

### 3.3.2 Extreme Positions

At the extreme positions, the crank aligns with the slotted lever (the line $O_1$-crank pin is along the lever). The slider reaches the **innermost** and **outermost** positions on the line of stroke.

- **Outer extreme:** $r_1 = R + r$
- **Inner extreme:** $r_2 = R - r$

### 3.3.3 Length of Stroke

$$L_s = r_1 - r_2 = (R + r) - (R - r) = 2r$$

$$\boxed{L_s = 2r}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The length of the stroke depends ONLY on the crank length $r$, not on the center distance $L$ or the lever length $R$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Velocity of Slider in Crank & Slotted Lever

### 3.4.1 Instantaneous Center Method (KTU Preferred Approach)

Let:
- $O_1$ = fixed pivot of slotted lever
- $O_2$ = crank center
- $A$ = crank pin (revolute joint between crank and slot)
- $\theta$ = crank angle from line of stroke
- $\phi$ = angle of slot with the line perpendicular to line of stroke

### 3.4.2 Geometric Relation for $\tan\phi$

The position of $A$ relative to $O_1$:

$$x = L - r\cos\theta, \quad y = r\sin\theta$$

$$\tan\phi = \frac{y}{x} = \frac{r\sin\theta}{L - r\cos\theta}$$

### 3.4.3 Velocity of Point A (Crank Pin)

The crank rotates at $\omega$. The velocity of $A$ is **perpendicular to $O_2 A$**:

$$v_A = \omega \cdot r$$

This velocity makes an angle $\theta$ with the line of stroke (or with $O_1 O_2$).

### 3.4.4 Component Along the Slot

Only the component of $v_A$ **along the slot** drives the slotted lever. The angle between $v_A$ and the slot direction is $(\theta - \phi)$.

The angular velocity of the slotted lever:

$$\omega_L = \frac{v_A \cos(\theta - \phi)}{O_1 A}$$

The velocity of the slider (along the line of stroke) is the horizontal component of the velocity of the slot point aligned with the slider position.

### 3.4.5 Final Velocity Expression

For a comprehensive derivation (often quoted in KTU textbooks):

$$v_{slider} = \omega \cdot r \cdot \cos\theta \cdot \left[\frac{\cos(\theta - \phi)}{\cos\phi}\right]$$

Simplified (commonly used form in KTU problems):

$$\boxed{v_{slider} = \omega \cdot r \cdot \left[\cos\theta + \frac{\sin\theta \tan\phi}{1}\right] \cdot \frac{1}{\cos\phi}}$$

Or the **standard textbook result**:

$$v_{slider} = \omega \cdot r \cdot \left[\cos\theta + \frac{\sin\theta \cdot r\sin\theta}{L - r\cos\theta}\right] \cdot \frac{\cos\alpha}{\cos^2\phi}$$

(where $\alpha$ is the angle of advance at the extreme position)

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem)

### **Problem:**
A crank and slotted lever mechanism used in a shaping machine has:
- Crank length $r = 150$ mm
- Distance between centers $L = 400$ mm
- Crank speed $N = 120$ rpm
- Angle of advance $\alpha = 30^\circ$

**Find:**
1. Quick Return Ratio (QRR)
2. Length of stroke
3. Maximum velocity of the slider (during cutting stroke)
4. Time of cutting and return strokes

### **Solution:**

#### Part 1: Quick Return Ratio

$$Q = \frac{\pi + 2\alpha}{\pi - 2\alpha} = \frac{\pi + \frac{2\pi}{6}}{\pi - \frac{2\pi}{6}}$$

Wait, $\alpha = 30^\circ = \pi/6$ rad. Converting to radians:

$$2\alpha = 2 \times \frac{\pi}{6} = \frac{\pi}{3} \text{ rad}$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi + \pi/3}{\pi - \pi/3} = \frac{4\pi/3}{2\pi/3} = 2$$

$$\boxed{Q = 2}$$

#### Part 2: Length of Stroke

$$L_s = 2r = 2 \times 150 = 300 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{L_s = 300 \text{ mm}}$$

#### Part 3: Angular Velocity of Crank

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 120}{60} = 4\pi \text{ rad/s}$$

#### Part 4: Maximum Slider Velocity (approximate, using sin-component)

The maximum velocity of the slider in C&S lever is approximately:

$$v_{max} \approx \omega \cdot r \cdot \left(1 + \frac{r}{L}\right)$$

$$v_{max} \approx 4\pi \times 0.15 \times \left(1 + \frac{0.15}{0.4}\right)$$

$$v_{max} = 1.885 \times 1.375 = 2.592 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v_{max} \approx 2.59 \text{ m/s}}$$

#### Part 5: Time Calculations

Time per revolution of crank:

$$T_{rev} = \frac{60}{N} = \frac{60}{120} = 0.5 \text{ s}$$

Cutting angle = $\pi + 2\alpha = \pi + \pi/3 = 4\pi/3$ rad

$$T_{cutting} = T_{rev} \times \frac{4\pi/3}{2\pi} = 0.5 \times \frac{2}{3} = 0.333 \text{ s}$$

$$T_{return} = T_{rev} \times \frac{2\pi/3}{2\pi} = 0.5 \times \frac{1}{3} = 0.167 \text{ s}$$

Verification: $T_{cutting} + T_{return} = 0.333 + 0.167 = 0.5$ s ✓

---

## 3.6 Comparison Matrix — All Quick Return Mechanisms

| Parameter | Crank & Slotted Lever | Whitworth Quick Return | Bull Engine |
|-----------|------------------------|------------------------|-------------|
| **Fixed link** | Slider | Small link | Crank |
| **QRR formula** | $Q = \dfrac{\pi + 2\alpha}{\pi - 2\alpha}$ | $Q = \dfrac{180 + 2\beta}{180 - 2\beta}$ | $Q = \dfrac{\pi + 2\alpha}{\pi - 2\alpha}$ |
| **Stroke length** | $2r$ | $2R$ (slot radius) | $2r$ |
| **Application** | Shaper, slotter | High-speed shaper | Pumps |
| **Smoothness** | Moderate | Smoother (longer arm) | Less smooth |
| **KTU Weightage** | High | High | Moderate |

---

## 3.7 Derivation Summary Box (KTU Quick-Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Derivation Steps to Memorize:**
> 1. **QRR (C&S Lever):** $Q = (\pi + 2\alpha)/(\pi - 2\alpha)$ — derived from $T = \theta/\omega$
> 2. **Stroke:** $L_s = 2r$ — derived from extreme position analysis
> 3. **Angle of advance** $\alpha$ comes from geometry: $\sin\alpha = r/L$
> 4. **Whitworth $2\beta$ design:** $\tan\beta = R/L$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define kinematic inversion. State the number of useful inversions possible for a four-bar kinematic chain.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

**Definition (2 marks):** A kinematic chain is said to be inverted when the relative motions of the links are changed by fixing (grounding) different links, one at a time, while keeping the relative link dimensions unchanged. This process of obtaining new mechanisms is called **kinematic inversion**.

**Number of useful inversions (1 mark):** A four-bar chain has **4 links**, hence **4 inversions** are possible. Since one of them is the original (standard) configuration, there are **3 useful inversions** in practice.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2023]`
**List the inversions of the single slider crank chain and state one practical application of each.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

| Inversion | Fixed Link | Application |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| 1. Pendulum Pump (Bull Engine) | Crank | Water pumps, oil pumps |
| 2. Oscillating Cylinder Engine | Slider | Early steam engines |
| 3. Crank & Slotted Lever | Connecting rod | Shaping machines, slotters |
| 4. Whitworth Quick Return | Smallest link | High-speed shaping machines |

(1.5 marks for the list + 1.5 marks for applications)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the inversions of a **four-bar chain** with neat sketches. State one practical application for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **crank and slotted lever mechanism** used in a shaper has a crank of length **150 mm** and a center distance of **450 mm**. The crank rotates at **80 rpm**.
  - (i) Find the **angle of advance** $\alpha$. 
  - (ii) Calculate the **Quick Return Ratio**. 
  - (iii) Determine the **time of cutting stroke** and **time of return stroke**.
  - **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The four-bar chain has 4 links: ground (1), input crank (2), coupler (3), and output crank (4). Each link can be fixed.

1. **Beam Engine (Watt's Engine) — Ground link fixed (1.75 marks):**
   The beam oscillates about a central pivot. The piston rod connects to one end of the beam; the connecting rod and crank at the other end drive the flywheel. Used in **Watt's steam engine**.

2. **Coupling Rod of Locomotive — Coupling rod fixed (1.75 marks):**
   The long coupling rod is fixed. Two cranks of equal length on the driving wheels are connected to it. The wheels rotate at identical speeds, ensuring synchronous motion. Used in **steam locomotives**.

3. **Watt's Straight-Line Mechanism — A non-adjacent short link fixed (1.75 marks):**
   A point on the coupler traces an approximate straight line. Used in **piston guiding** in early steam engines (approximate straight-line motion).

4. **Pantograph — A non-adjacent link fixed (1.75 marks):**
   Used for **copying/enlarging/reducing drawings** while maintaining geometric similarity.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $r = 150$ mm $= 0.15$ m, $L = 450$ mm $= 0.45$ m, $N = 80$ rpm.

**(i) Angle of advance $\alpha$ (2 marks):**

From the geometry at the extreme position of the slotted lever, the crank and lever are aligned, and the crank makes an angle $\alpha$ with the line of stroke:

$$\sin\alpha = \frac{r}{L} = \frac{150}{450} = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$\alpha = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) = 19.47^\circ$$

**(ii) Quick Return Ratio (2 marks):**

$$Q = \frac{\pi + 2\alpha}{\pi - 2\alpha} = \frac{\pi + 2(19.47^\circ \times \pi/180)}{\pi - 2(19.47^\circ \times \pi/180)}$$

Converting $\alpha = 19.47^\circ$ to rad: $\alpha = 0.3398$ rad

$$Q = \frac{\pi + 2(0.3398)}{\pi - 2(0.3398)} = \frac{3.1416 + 0.6796}{3.1416 - 0.6796} = \frac{3.8212}{2.4620}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 1.552}$$

**(iii) Time of cutting and return strokes (3 marks):**

Angular velocity of crank:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 80}{60} = 8.378 \text{ rad/s}$$

Time per revolution:

$$T_{rev} = \frac{60}{N} = \frac{60}{80} = 0.75 \text{ s}$$

Cutting angle $= \pi + 2\alpha = 3.8212$ rad

Return angle $= \pi - 2\alpha = 2.4620$ rad

Time of cutting stroke:

$$T_{cutting} = T_{rev} \times \frac{\pi + 2\alpha}{2\pi} = 0.75 \times \frac{3.8212}{2\pi} = 0.75 \times 0.608$$

$$\boxed{T_{cutting} = 0.456 \text{ s}}$$

Time of return stroke:

$$T_{return} = T_{rev} \times \frac{\pi - 2\alpha}{2\pi} = 0.75 \times \frac{2.4620}{2\pi} = 0.75 \times 0.392$$

$$\boxed{T_{return} = 0.294 \text{ s}}$$

**Verification:** $T_{cutting} + T_{return} = 0.456 + 0.294 = 0.75$ s $= T_{rev}$ ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` — **Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Whitworth quick return mechanism**. Derive the expression for its **Quick Return Ratio**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **Whitworth quick return mechanism** has a driving crank of radius **75 mm** and a center distance of **300 mm**. If the crank rotates at **100 rpm**, determine:
  - (i) The **angle of advance** $2\beta$.
  - (ii) The **Quick Return Ratio**.
  - (iii) The **length of stroke** of the slotted lever.
  - **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Whitworth Quick Return Mechanism (4 marks):**

The Whitworth quick return mechanism is the **fourth inversion** of the single slider crank chain, where the **smallest link (coupler)** between the crank pin and the slotted lever is fixed.

**Components:**
- A **driving crank** of radius $R$ rotates at uniform angular velocity.
- A **slotted lever** of length $L$ from the fixed pivot oscillates about its pivot.
- The driving crank is keyed to a small spur gear, which meshes with a larger gear fixed to the slotted lever.
- The driving crank and the slotted lever are connected via a **pin engaging the slot**.

**Working (3 marks):**
- As the small driving gear rotates uniformly, it drives the large gear on the slotted lever through gear meshing.
- The slotted lever oscillates, with the angular velocity **changing continuously**.
- At the extreme positions, the crank and the lever are aligned.
- The **return stroke** is fast (when the crank is near the dead-center of the lever) and the **cutting stroke** is slow.

**Derivation of QRR (3 marks, included in 7):**

At the extreme position, the crank and lever are collinear. Let $2\beta$ be the angle through which the driving crank rotates during the **return stroke**.

From the right triangle geometry:

$$\tan\beta = \frac{R}{L}$$

In one complete revolution ($360^\circ$) of the driving crank:
- Cutting stroke corresponds to $360^\circ - 2\beta$
- Return stroke corresponds to $2\beta$

Quick Return Ratio:

$$Q = \frac{T_{cutting}}{T_{return}} = \frac{360^\circ - 2\beta}{2\beta} = \frac{180^\circ - \beta}{\beta}$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{180^\circ + 2\beta}{180^\circ - 2\beta}}$$

(equivalent form depending on whether $2\beta$ is the return angle or a parameter)

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $R = 75$ mm $= 0.075$ m, $L = 300$ mm $= 0.3$ m, $N = 100$ rpm.

**(i) Angle of advance $2\beta$ (2.5 marks):**

$$\tan\beta = \frac{R}{L} = \frac{75}{300} = 0.25$$

$$\beta = \tan^{-1}(0.25) = 14.04^\circ$$

$$\boxed{2\beta = 28.07^\circ}$$

**(ii) Quick Return Ratio (2.5 marks):**

Using $Q = \dfrac{180^\circ - \beta}{\beta}$ (with $2\beta$ as the return angle parameter):

$$Q = \frac{180^\circ - 14.04^\circ}{14.04^\circ} = \frac{165.96^\circ}{14.04^\circ} = 11.82$$

Or using the alternative form $Q = \dfrac{180^\circ + 2\beta}{180^\circ - 2\beta}$:

$$Q = \frac{180 + 28.07}{180 - 28.07} = \frac{208.07}{151.93} = 1.369$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 1.37 \text{ (using standard form)}}$$

**(iii) Length of stroke (2 marks):**

The length of the stroke is twice the crank radius (for Whitworth):

$$L_s = 2R = 2 \times 75 = 150 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{L_s = 150 \text{ mm}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion:** Always convert $\alpha$ from degrees to radians (or use $\pi$ factor) when using the formula $Q = (\pi + 2\alpha)/(\pi - 2\alpha)$. Mixing units is a **2-mark penalty**.
> 2. **QRR definition:** Some books define $Q$ as $T_{cutting}/T_{return}$, others as the inverse. **Read the question carefully** — the KTU convention is $T_{cutting}/T_{return} > 1$.
> 3. **Inversion identification:** Students often confuse "Crank & Slotted Lever" with "Whitworth". The key difference: in **Whitworth**, the driving link is a **crank on a separate gear train**, not a direct crank. The small link is fixed.
> 4. **Stroke formula:** Don't write $L_s = 2R$ for C&S Lever when $R$ is the lever length — the formula is $L_s = 2r$ where $r$ is the **crank length**, not the lever.
> 5. **Missing sketches:** In 7-mark questions, **always include a labeled diagram**. A 14-mark question without sketches loses up to **3 marks**.
> 6. **Sign of $\alpha$:** $\alpha$ is always taken as **positive** in the magnitude. Don't carry forward negative signs from $\sin^{-1}$.

---

## 5.3 Bonus Practice Questions

### **Q3.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**State and explain the difference between a kinematic chain and a kinematic pair with two examples each.** *(3 marks)*

#### Brief Answer:
A **kinematic pair** is a joint between two links that permits **relative motion** (e.g., revolute pair, prismatic pair). A **kinematic chain** is a **series of kinematic pairs** connected in such a way that the **relative motion between the first and last link is completely constrained** (e.g., four-bar chain, slider crank mechanism).

**Examples of pairs:** Revolute (hinge), Prismatic (slider), Screw, Cam, Gear, Ball.
**Examples of chains:** Four-bar chain, Single slider crank, Double slider crank.

---

### **Q4.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**Explain the working of a Scotch yoke mechanism. Where is it used?** *(3 marks)*

#### Brief Answer:
A **Scotch yoke** is an inversion of the double slider crank chain where a **pin on a rotating crank** engages a **slot in a sliding yoke**. The yoke moves with **simple harmonic motion** (SHM) as the crank rotates uniformly.

**Applications:** Punch presses, valve actuators in some engines, and applications requiring pure SHM reciprocation.

---

### **Q5.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2022]`
**What is the difference between a machine and a mechanism? Define a structure.** *(3 marks)*

#### Brief Answer:
- **Mechanism:** A kinematic chain with **one link fixed** (frame) that transmits motion in a predetermined manner. (e.g., four-bar linkage, slider crank)
- **Machine:** A mechanism (or combination) that **transmits force and modifies motion** to do useful work. (e.g., IC engine, shaper, lathe)
- **Structure:** A kinematic chain with **no relative motion** between links — it transmits only force. (e.g., a truss, a building frame)

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this hierarchy:** Structure ⊂ Mechanism ⊂ Machine (each successive concept adds capability.)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Kinematic Inversions (PECPT412, Module 1)**

### **Core Concepts:**
- ☐ Inversion = changing the **fixed link** of a kinematic chain
- ☐ Number of inversions of an $n$-link chain = $n$
- ☐ Three parent chains: Four-bar, Single slider crank, Double slider crank
- ☐ Inversions of single slider crank are the most important (QRR applications)

### **Four-Bar Chain Inversions (Mnemonic: B-C-W-P):**
- ☐ **B**eam engine (ground fixed)
- ☐ **C**oupling rod of locomotive (coupling rod fixed)
- ☐ **W**att's straight line (short link fixed)
- ☐ **P**antograph (other non-adjacent link fixed)

### **Single Slider Crank Inversions:**
- ☐ **Bull engine** (crank fixed) — used in pumps
- ☐ **Oscillating cylinder engine** (slider fixed)
- ☐ **Crank & Slotted Lever** (connecting rod fixed) — shaper/slotter
- ☐ **Whitworth Quick Return** (smallest link fixed) — high-speed shaper

### **Double Slider Crank Inversions:**
- ☐ **Elliptical Trammel** — two perpendicular sliders + coupler
- ☐ **Scotch Yoke** — pin in slot, gives SHM
- ☐ **Oldham's Coupling** — for parallel shaft misalignment

### **Key Formulas:**
- ☐ QRR (C&S Lever): $Q = \dfrac{\pi + 2\alpha}{\pi - 2\alpha}$
- ☐ QRR (Whitworth): $Q = \dfrac{180^\circ + 2\beta}{180^\circ - 2\beta}$
- ☐ Stroke length: $L_s = 2r$
- ☐ Angle of advance (C&S): $\sin\alpha = r/L$
- ☐ Angle of advance (Whitworth): $\tan\beta = R/L$

### **Exam Strategy:**
- ☐ Always draw a **labeled sketch** for 7+ mark questions
- ☐ State the **fixed link** explicitly when identifying an inversion
- ☐ Quote the **QRR formula** with the variable meaning clearly
- ☐ Verify with **boundary conditions**: $Q = 1$ when $\alpha = 0$ (uniform motion)
- ☐ Convert angles properly (degrees vs. radians) in numeric problems
- ☐ Use **standard notation**: $r$ = crank, $L$ = center distance, $\alpha$ = angle of advance

### **Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
- ☐ Confusing $\alpha$ (in C&S Lever) with $\beta$ (in Whitworth) — they are different angles
- ☐ Writing $L_s = 2R$ in C&S Lever when it should be $L_s = 2r$
- ☐ Forgetting that **the slotted lever pivots**, not the slider, in C&S Lever
- ☐ Mixing up the **fixed link** in Whitworth vs. C&S Lever

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equivalent Linkages, Kinematic Analysis of Planar Mechanisms: Mobility Analysis and Range of Movement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT412 - KINEMATICS OF MACHINICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Equivalent Linkages, Kinematic Analysis of Planar Mechanisms: Mobility Analysis and Range of Movement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Equivalent Linkages?

An **equivalent linkage** is a simplified four-bar (or simpler) kinematic chain that replicates the input-output motion behavior of a complex multi-link planar mechanism at two specific points of interest (the *tracer points* or *pivots*). It captures only the **essential functional motion** of the original mechanism, stripping away geometric complexity.

**Formal KTU Definition (Module 1 Syllabus):**
> An *equivalent linkage* of a multi-link planar mechanism is a four-bar chain whose instantaneous angular displacements, velocities, and accelerations of its input and output links exactly match those of the original mechanism at any given configuration, when measured at the tracer/coupler points of interest.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Think of a complex analog wristwatch with 30+ gears, springs, and levers. Yet, what the *user* perceives is just the hour-hand and minute-hand motion. A watchmaker, when studying only the *visible functional motion*, can replace the entire gear train with a single conceptual linkage that produces the same hour-hand and minute-hand rotation. That conceptual replacement is the **equivalent linkage** — a black box that mimics external behavior without replicating internal complexity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Equivalent Linkages Matter in KTU 2024:**
> - Synthesis of mechanisms (Burmester theory) is built on this concept.
> - Multi-loop mechanisms (like the Watt, Stephenson, and Klann six-bar linkages) reduce to equivalent four-bars for analysis.
> - Saves enormous computational effort in design.

### 1.3 Kinematic Analysis of Planar Mechanisms

**Kinematic Analysis** is the study of motion (position, velocity, acceleration) of a mechanism *without* considering the forces producing that motion. For planar mechanisms, this is done at three progressive levels:

| Level | Quantity Determined | KTU Cognitive Level |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Position Analysis** | Displacement of each link at a given configuration | Apply (L3) |
| **Velocity Analysis** | Linear and angular velocities (first time derivative) | Apply (L3) |
| **Acceleration Analysis** | Linear and angular accelerations (second time derivative) | Analyze (L4) |

### 1.4 Mobility Analysis

**Mobility** (or **Degrees of Freedom**, DOF) is the number of independent parameters required to completely define the configuration of a mechanism in space.

$$M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

where:
- $n$ = total number of rigid links (including the fixed frame/ground)
- $j_1$ = number of lower pairs (1 DOF joints: revolute, prismatic, etc.)
- $j_2$ = number of higher pairs (2 DOF joints: cam-follower, gear mesh, etc.)

### 1.5 Range of Movement (ROM)

The **Range of Movement** of a mechanism is the bounded angular/linear interval over which an output link, a coupler point, or a rigid body (the *movable output*) can travel during one complete cycle of the input driver.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ROM is classified (per KTU 2024 syllabus) into:
> 1. **Function Generation** — links (or points) follow prescribed relative angular/linear motions.
> 2. **Path Generation** — a point traces a prescribed curve in space.
> 3. **Motion Generation** — a rigid body (link) assumes prescribed finite positions in succession.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mobility Analysis: Theory Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Count Links ($n$)**
Include *every* rigid body, including the ground link (treated as a single fixed link, not multiple).

**Step 2 — Identify Joints**
- **Lower pair (1 DOF):** Revolute (R), Prismatic (P), Screw (helical - rare in planar), Cylindrical (special in planar reduces to R), Universal (special in planar reduces to R).
- **Higher pair (2 DOF):** Cam-follower contact, gear meshing (for non-conjugate analysis), ball on a flat surface.

**Step 3 — Apply the Criterion**

For a planar mechanism with only lower pairs and no redundant DOF:

$$M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \quad \text{(Gruebler's Criterion)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Case (Kutzbach's Modification):**
> If the mechanism has multiple loops and *no passive DOF* (no idler rollers etc.), Gruebler's equation is sufficient. If there are *passive links* (zero DOF that nevertheless move passively — e.g., a roller follower), use:
> $$M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2 + F_p$$
> where $F_p$ = number of idle/passive DOFs.

**Step 4 — Interpret the Result**
- $M = 1$: **Mechanism** (deterministic motion for one input).
- $M = 0$: **Structure** (rigid, no motion possible).
- $M = 2$ or more: **Multiple DOF** — needs more inputs to be deterministic.
- $M < 0$: **Pre-loaded structure** — kinematically indeterminate (requires elasticity analysis).

### 2.2 Range of Movement: Theory Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Identify the Output Element**
- Could be a coupler point (path generation).
- Could be a coupler link's angle (motion generation).
- Could be the output link's swing angle (function generation).

**Step 2 — Identify the Limit Positions**
- **Limit positions** occur when the output link's angular velocity $\omega_3 = 0$ but the input link continues to move. These are *extreme positions*.
- **Dead/Toggle positions** are special limits where the mechanism loses control (output link can move but input cannot drive it further).

**Step 3 — Apply Grashof's Law (for Four-Bar ROM)**

$$\text{Let } S = \text{shortest link}, \quad L = \text{longest link}, \quad P, Q = \text{other two links}$$

$$S + L \leq P + Q$$

- If $S + L < P + Q$ and $S$ = **ground link** → *Crank-rocker* (full continuous rotation of input, oscillation of output).
- If $S + L < P + Q$ and $S$ = **side link adjacent to ground** → *Double-crank (Drag-link)* (both pivoted links rotate fully).
- If $S + L < P + Q$ and $S$ = **opposite/coupler link** → *Double-rocker (Watt)* (no link rotates fully).
- If $S + L = P + Q$ → *Change-point mechanism* (special case, toggle in some configurations).
- If $S + L > P + Q$ → **No continuous rotation** possible; *Triple-rocker* behavior.

### 2.3 Equivalent Linkage Theory

For an $n$-link mechanism, the equivalent four-bar linkage is found by:
1. **Selecting two tracer points** on the coupler (e.g., pin joints of a multi-loop chain).
2. **Computing the relative motion** (Burmester circle-point/center-point theory).
3. **Synthesizing the equivalent four-bar** that has the same instantaneous kinematics at those two points.

For a six-bar Stephenson or Watt chain, the *equivalent four-bar* approximation considers only the dominant loop and neglects higher-order corrections for first-cut analysis.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | KTU Marks Weightage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | Gruebler's mobility criterion for planar mechanism | 3–4 marks |
| 2 | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 + F_p$ | Kutzbach's modified form with passive DOFs | 3–4 marks |
| 3 | $S + L \leq P + Q$ | Grashof's inequality (range of movement test) | 3–4 marks |
| 4 | $\omega_3 = 0$ at limit positions | Defines extremes of output link motion | 2 marks |
| 5 | $M = 0$ | Structure (kinematically locked) | 2 marks |
| 6 | $M = 1$ | Valid mechanism (deterministic) | 2 marks |
| 7 | $\theta_{ROM} = \theta_{max} - \theta_{min}$ | Output link swing range (radians) | 3 marks |
| 8 | Four-bar + dyad $\approx$ six-bar | Equivalent linkage rule (functional substitution) | 2 marks |
| 9 | $\dot{\theta_3} = 0 \implies \tan(\phi) = \frac{\sin(\alpha)}{\cos(\alpha) - k}$ | Toggle condition (with appropriate substitutions) | 4–5 marks |
| 10 | Burmester point/circle locus | Defines equivalent four-bar geometric locus | 5 marks |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** The single most-asked formula in Part A mobility questions is the Gruebler equation. In Part B, it is typically combined with a *numerical count* question (e.g., "Find DOF of a 6-bar with given joints").

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robot manipulator design:** Equivalent four-bars replace complex parallelograms to simplify servo sizing.
- **Automotive suspension:** Multi-link suspensions reduce to an equivalent four-bar for ride-handling analysis.
- **Compliant mechanisms:** Mobility analysis predicts the number of independent deformation modes.
- **Foldable structures (space deployables):** Mobility must be exactly 1 for predictable deployment.
- **Biomechanics:** Human joints are modeled as multi-link equivalent chains for gait analysis.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation of Gruebler's Mobility Equation

**Starting Premise:**
Consider a planar mechanism composed of $n$ rigid links, including the ground. Before any joints are added, every link in 2D space can undergo $3$ independent motions: 2 translations (along $x$ and $y$) and 1 rotation about its own center. Therefore, the unconstrained system has:

$$\text{Total DOFs} = 3n$$

**Step 1 — Apply the Ground Constraint**
The ground link is fixed; it has 0 DOF. So we subtract 3 DOFs:

$$\text{Remaining DOFs} = 3n - 3 = 3(n-1)$$

**Step 2 — Apply Joint Constraints**
Each **lower pair** (1-DOF joint) removes 2 relative motions between the joined links (in 2D, a lower pair removes 2 of the 3 relative DOFs). Each **higher pair** (2-DOF joint) removes only 1 relative motion.

So total DOFs removed:

$$\text{DOFs removed} = 2 j_1 + j_2$$

**Step 3 — Net Mobility**
Combine both:

$$M = 3(n-1) - 2 j_1 - j_2$$

**Step 4 — Verification (Sanity Check):**
- A **simple four-bar** (n = 4, j₁ = 4, j₂ = 0):
$$M = 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0 = 9 - 8 = 1 \quad \checkmark$$
- A **rigid triangle on ground** (n = 3, j₁ = 3, j₂ = 0):
$$M = 3(3-1) - 2(3) - 0 = 6 - 6 = 0 \quad \checkmark$$
- A **five-bar linkage** (n = 5, j₁ = 5, j₂ = 0):
$$M = 3(5-1) - 2(5) - 0 = 12 - 10 = 2 \quad \checkmark$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Toggle/Limit Position Condition for a Four-Bar

Let a four-bar ABCD with input link 2 rotate at constant $\omega_2$. The output link 4's velocity is:

$$\omega_4 = \omega_2 \cdot \frac{\ell_2 \sin(\theta_3 - \theta_2)}{\ell_4 \sin(\theta_3 - \theta_4)}$$

**Toggle/Limit Position Condition:**

$$\omega_4 = 0 \implies \sin(\theta_3 - \theta_2) = 0 \implies \theta_3 - \theta_2 = 0 \text{ or } \pi$$

That is, **links 2 and 3 are collinear** (either extended or overlapped).

**Geometrically:**
- **Extended collinearity:** Output link 4 is at its **maximum** angular position.
- **Overlapped collinearity:** Output link 4 is at its **minimum** angular position.

The **Range of Movement** of the output link is:

$$\theta_{ROM} = \theta_{4,\max} - \theta_{4,\min} = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{a^2 + d^2 - (b-c)^2}{2ad}\right) - \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{a^2 + d^2 - (b+c)^2}{2ad}\right)$$

where $a, b, c, d$ are link lengths: $a$ = input crank, $b$ = coupler, $c$ = output rocker, $d$ = ground.

### 3.3 Equivalent Linkage Reduction: Six-Bar to Four-Bar

**Starting Mechanism:** A Stephenson III six-bar has six binary links and seven lower-pair joints.

**Mobility of original:**
$$M_{orig} = 3(6-1) - 2(7) = 15 - 14 = 1$$

**Reduction Principle:**
The Stephenson III contains a *binary dyad* (two links + one pin between them) attached to a four-bar primary loop. The equivalent four-bar is formed by:

1. **Identifying the primary four-bar** (the larger loop).
2. **Freezing the secondary dyad** at one configuration — this fixes a reference for analysis.
3. **Mapping the two coupler points of the original** to two points on the equivalent four-bar's coupler.

The resulting four-bar has link lengths:

$$\ell_{i,eq} = f(\ell_1, \ell_2, \ell_3, \ell_4, \ell_5, \ell_6, \theta_{input})$$

where $f$ is a nonlinear function derived from the loop-closure equations of the original six-bar.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024, the expected level is *conceptual* — the candidate should be able to **describe** the procedure and **sketch** the equivalent four-bar for a given six-bar, not derive the closed-form $f$.

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Mobility Analysis)

**Problem:** A planar mechanism consists of 7 links, 9 revolute joints, and 1 cam (higher) pair. The mechanism has 1 passive roller. Find mobility.

**Given:**
- $n = 7$
- $j_1 = 9$
- $j_2 = 1$
- $F_p = 1$ (passive roller DOF)

**Solution — Step 1: Apply Kutzbach's Modified Equation:**

$$M = 3(n-1) - 2 j_1 - j_2 + F_p$$

**Step 2: Substitute values:**

$$M = 3(7-1) - 2(9) - 1 + 1$$

$$M = 3(6) - 18 - 1 + 1$$

$$M = 18 - 18 = 0$$

**Step 3: Interpretation:**
- $M = 0$ → **Structure, not a mechanism.**
- This means the cam-follower with 1 passive roller is over-constrained or under-driven.

**Step 4: Engineering Insight:**
The passive roller is meant to remove the *higher pair* (point contact) and replace it with a *lower pair* (rolling contact). It removes 1 DOF of relative slip but *adds 1 passive DOF* (rotation of the roller), so net mobility is unchanged. The result $M = 0$ means the original problem is a **locked structure** — the candidate should re-examine the joint count.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mobility Analysis Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Identify Mechanism] --> B[Count Total Rigid Links n including ground]
    B --> C[Identify All Joints]
    C --> D{Joint Type?}
    D -- Lower Pair R P --> E[j1 Count Increment]
    D -- Higher Pair Cam Gear --> F[j2 Count Increment]
    E --> G[Check for Passive Rollers]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Passive DOFs Present?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Apply Kutzbach Modified: M = 3 n minus 1 minus 2 j1 minus j2 plus Fp]
    H -- No --> J[Apply Gruebler: M = 3 n minus 1 minus 2 j1 minus j2]
    I --> K{Interpret M}
    J --> K
    K -- M equals 1 --> L[VALID MECHANISM]
    K -- M equals 0 --> M[STRUCTURE LOCKED]
    K -- M greater than 1 --> N[MULTI DOF SYSTEM]
    K -- M less than 0 --> O[PRELOADED INDETERMINATE]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Kinematic Analysis Levels

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[POSITION ANALYSIS] --> V[VELOCITY ANALYSIS]
    V --> ACC[ACCELERATION ANALYSIS]
    P --> P1[Input Angular Position theta2]
    P1 --> P2[Solve Loop Closure Equations]
    P2 --> P3[Output Position theta3 theta4]
    V --> V1[Input Angular Velocity omega2]
    V1 --> V2[Differentiate Loop Closure]
    V2 --> V3[Output Velocity omega3 omega4]
    ACC --> A1[Input Angular Acceleration alpha2]
    A1 --> A2[Second Differentiate]
    A2 --> A3[Output Acceleration alpha3 alpha4]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Equivalent Linkage Reduction Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Original Multi Link Mechanism] --> S2[Identify Tracer Points on Coupler]
    S2 --> S3[Compute Relative Angular Motion at Tracers]
    S3 --> S4[Apply Burmester Circle Point Theory]
    S4 --> S5{Equivalent Four Bar Exists?}
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Construct Equivalent Four Bar]
    S5 -- No --> S7[Use Gruebler Check on Original]
    S6 --> S8[Validate Kinematic Match]
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9[Use Equivalent Linkage for Design]
```

### 4.4 Range of Movement Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Range of Movement ROM] --> R1[Function Generation]
    R --> R2[Path Generation]
    R --> R3[Motion Generation]
    R1 --> R11[Output Link Angle follows Prescribed Function of Input]
    R2 --> R21[Coupler Point traces Prescribed Curve]
    R3 --> R31[Coupler Link assumes Prescribed Finite Positions]
    R1 --> CHK[Apply Grashof Law for Four Bar]
    CHK --> T1{S plus L versus P plus Q}
    T1 -- S plus L less than P plus Q --> G1[Full or Partial Rotation]
    T1 -- S plus L greater than P plus Q --> G2[Triple Rocker only Oscillation]
```

### 4.5 Four-Bar Toggle Position Diagram (Schematic)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph EXT[EXTENDED COLLINEARITY: THETA4 MAX]
        A1[Ground Pivot A] --- L2A[Link 2 Vertical Up]
        L2A --- C1[Collinear Point with Link 3]
        C1 --- L3A[Link 3 Extended]
        C1 --- L4A[Link 4 at Max Angle]
        A1 --- D1[Ground Pivot D]
        D1 --- L4A
    end
    subgraph OVR[OVERLAPPED COLLINEARITY: THETA4 MIN]
        A2[Ground Pivot A] --- L2B[Link 2 Vertical Up]
        L2B --- C2[Overlap Point]
        C2 --- L3B[Link 3 Overlapped]
        C2 --- L4B[Link 4 at Min Angle]
        A2 --- D2[Ground Pivot D]
        D2 --- L4B
    end
```
