## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Number of Instantaneous Centers in a Mechanism

Consider a mechanism with $n$ moving links. Each link can form an instant center with every other link. We must count the number of *distinct unordered pairs* of links.

The number of ways to choose 2 links out of $n$ links is given by the standard combination formula:

$$N = \binom{n}{2} = \frac{n!}{2!(n-2)!}$$

Expanding the factorials:

$$N = \frac{n \times (n-1) \times (n-2)!}{2 \times 1 \times (n-2)!}$$

Cancelling $(n-2)!$ from numerator and denominator:

$$N = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$$

This is the **fundamental counting equation** for ICs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example (KTU 2024 Style):** For a six-bar Stephenson mechanism, $n = 6$ moving links, so $N = \dfrac{6 \times 5}{2} = 15$ instantaneous centers.

### 3.2 Derivation: The Aronhold-Kennedy Theorem

Consider three links — link 1 (frame), link 2, and link 3 — with instantaneous centers $I_{12}, I_{13}$, and $I_{23}$.

**Step 1:** Since link 1 is the fixed frame, the motion of link 2 relative to link 1 is pure rotation about the point $I_{12}$. Therefore, link 2 may be considered momentarily as a rigid body rotating about $I_{12}$.

**Step 2:** Similarly, the motion of link 3 relative to link 1 is pure rotation about $I_{13}$. Thus link 3 is also a rigid body rotating about $I_{13}$ at that instant.

**Step 3:** Now consider the relative motion of link 2 and link 3. The relative motion of link 2 with respect to link 3 must also be pure rotation — about some point $I_{23}$ that is fixed in both link 2 and link 3 at that instant.

**Step 4:** The position of $I_{23}$ must satisfy **two** geometric conditions:
- It lies on the rigid body of link 2.
- It lies on the rigid body of link 3.

**Step 5:** Since link 2 is rotating about $I_{12}$ as observed from link 1, the velocity of any point on link 2 is **perpendicular to the line joining that point to $I_{12}$**. For $I_{23}$ to have zero velocity in link 2's own frame, the line of rotation $I_{12} \to I_{23}$ must align with the kinematic constraint.

**Step 6:** By analogous reasoning for link 3 rotating about $I_{13}$, the point $I_{23}$ must lie on the line joining $I_{12}$ and $I_{13}$.

**Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{I_{12}, \ I_{13}, \ I_{23} \ \text{are collinear}}$$

This is the **Aronhold-Kennedy Theorem**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Velocity of a Point on a Link Using its Instantaneous Center

Let a rigid link be undergoing planar motion. At an instant, the link rotates about its instantaneous center $I$ with angular velocity $\omega$. Let there be a point $P$ on the link at a perpendicular distance $r$ from $I$.

**Step 1:** The instantaneous center, by definition, has zero velocity. So $V_I = 0$.

**Step 2:** Every other point on the rigid link rotates about $I$ at that instant. The angular velocity of the link is the same for all points on it.

**Step 3:** Therefore, the linear velocity of point $P$ is given by the standard rigid body rotation formula:

$$V_P = \omega \cdot r$$

where $r$ is the distance from $I$ to $P$.

**Step 4:** The direction of $V_P$ is perpendicular to the line $I P$, in the sense of the rotation.

**Step 5 (Extension):** For a four-bar mechanism, the velocity of any point on the coupler (link 3) can be calculated if we know $\omega_3$ and the distance from that point to $I_{13}$ (or $I_{23}$).

### 3.4 Derivation: Angular Velocity Ratio Between Two Links

Consider a four-bar mechanism with links 1 (frame), 2, 3, and 4. We want to find $\omega_2 / \omega_3$.

**Step 1:** Consider the relative motion of link 2 with respect to link 3. By definition, this rotation occurs about the instantaneous center $I_{23}$.

**Step 2:** The velocity of any point $A$ on link 2, as observed from link 3, is:

$$V_{A/3} = (\omega_2 - \omega_3) \cdot I_{23} A$$

**Step 3:** Similarly, for point $A$ on link 2 observed from link 1 (the frame):

$$V_A = \omega_2 \cdot I_{12} A$$

**Step 4:** Equating the two velocity expressions for point $A$:

$$(\omega_2 - \omega_3) \cdot I_{23} A = \omega_2 \cdot I_{12} A$$

**Step 5:** Solving for the ratio:

$$\frac{\omega_2}{\omega_3} = \frac{I_{23} A}{I_{12} A - I_{23} A} = \frac{I_{12} I_{23}}{I_{13} I_{23}} \cdot \frac{I_{13} A}{I_{12} A}$$

After algebraic simplification, for a known geometric construction:

$$\frac{\omega_2}{\omega_3} = \frac{I_{13} I_{23}}{I_{12} I_{23}}$$

This is the famous **angular velocity ratio formula** using IC distances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** This ratio formula is the basis of nearly every velocity analysis problem you will face in the board exam. Practice drawing a four-bar linkage and identifying $I_{13}$ and $I_{23}$ to apply this relation.

### 3.5 Velocity Magnitude Calculation — Analytical Walkthrough

Suppose a four-bar mechanism is in the configuration shown in the KTU 2024 board problem. Let link 2 (input crank) rotate at $\omega_2 = 10 \text{ rad/s}$ and the link lengths be $a = 50 \text{ mm}, \ b = 100 \text{ mm}, \ c = 80 \text{ mm}, \ d = 120 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 1:** Velocity of point $A$ (joint between link 1 and link 2):

$$V_A = \omega_2 \cdot a = 10 \times 0.050 = 0.5 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2:** Velocity of point $B$ (joint between link 3 and link 4) is unknown — call it $V_B$.

**Step 3:** Locate $I_{13}$ using Kennedy's theorem (intersection of lines through known ICs). Measure the distance $I_{13} A$ and $I_{13} B$ from the scaled diagram.

**Step 4:** Since the entire coupler (link 3) rotates about $I_{13}$:

$$V_A = \omega_3 \cdot I_{13} A \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_3 = \frac{V_A}{I_{13} A}$$

**Step 5:** Compute $V_B$:

$$V_B = \omega_3 \cdot I_{13} B = \frac{V_A \cdot I_{13} B}{I_{13} A}$$

This gives the velocity of the output point without solving the vector loop — purely by geometry.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Classification of Instantaneous Centers

The following Mermaid block diagram classifies the three major types of ICs and how they are located in any planar mechanism.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Instantaneous Centers in Planar Mechanisms"] --> B1["Primary ICs"]
    A0 --> B2["Secondary ICs"]
    A0 --> B3["Ternary ICs"]
    B1 --> C1["Located at actual pin joints between two links"]
    B1 --> C2["Count equals number of binary joints j in mechanism"]
    B2 --> D1["Located between non-adjacent links"]
    B2 --> D2["Found using Aronhold-Kennedy Theorem"]
    B2 --> D3["Three ICs of any three links must be collinear"]
    B3 --> E1["Three or more links share a common pivot point"]
    B3 --> E2["Single point serves as multiple primary and relative ICs"]
    C1 --> F1["Example I12 at crank pivot in four-bar"]
    C2 --> F2["Example I34 at rocker pivot in four-bar"]
    D2 --> G1["Line through two known ICs intersects third line at the new IC"]
    D3 --> G2["Constraint: I12, I13, I23 are collinear"]
    E1 --> H1["Example: Compound gear on a single shaft"]
    E2 --> H2["Example: Multiple levers on one fulcrum"]
```

### 4.2 Four-Bar Mechanism: Functional Architecture of All 6 ICs

A four-bar linkage has $n = 4$ links, so the total number of ICs is $N = \dfrac{4 \times 3}{2} = 6$. The following block diagram shows the architecture of how all six are identified.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1["Link 1 - Fixed Frame"] --> IC12["I12 - Pin joint A"]
    L2["Link 2 - Input Crank"] --> IC12
    L2 --> IC23["I23 - Pin joint B between crank and coupler"]
    L3["Link 3 - Coupler"] --> IC23
    L3 --> IC34["I34 - Pin joint C between coupler and rocker"]
    L4["Link 4 - Output Rocker"] --> IC34
    L4 --> IC14["I14 - Pin joint D - fixed pivot of rocker"]
    L1 --> IC14
    L1 -.-> IC13["I13 - Secondary IC between frame and coupler"]
    L3 -.-> IC13
    L2 -.-> IC24["I24 - Secondary IC between crank and rocker"]
    L4 -.-> IC24
    IC12 -.->|"Kennedy line L1"| IC13
    IC14 -.->|"Kennedy line L2"| IC13
    IC12 -.->|"Kennedy line L3"| IC24
    IC34 -.->|"Kennedy line L4"| IC24
    IC13 -.->|"Intersect of L1 and L2"| IC13marker["IC13 Located"]
    IC24 -.->|"Intersect of L3 and L4"| IC24marker["IC24 Located"]
```

### 4.3 Procedure Flowchart: Locating ICs in a Six-Bar Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Step 1: Identify all links and number them 1 to n"]
    S2["Step 2: Mark all primary ICs at the pin joints"]
    S3["Step 3: Identify ternary links and assign shared ICs"]
    S4["Step 4: Construct Kennedy lines for any three links"]
    S5["Step 5: Locate secondary ICs at line intersections"]
    S6["Step 6: Tabulate all ICs in a matrix format"]
    S7["Step 7: Verify using Carnots Theorem if needed"]
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7
```

### 4.4 Velocity Analysis Workflow Using the IC Method

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1["Input: Angular velocity of driver link omega2"]
    V2["Step A: Compute velocity of pin joint A using V_A equals omega2 times length of link 2"]
    V3["Step B: Locate I13 via Kennedy lines"]
    V4["Step C: Compute angular velocity of coupler omega3 equals V_A divided by distance I13 to A"]
    V5["Step D: Compute velocity of pin joint B using V_B equals omega3 times distance I13 to B"]
    V6["Step E: Compute angular velocity of output link omega4 equals V_B divided by length of link 4"]
    V1 --> V2 --> V3 --> V4 --> V5 --> V6
    V6 --> V7["Output: Complete velocity field of the mechanism at that instant"]
```

### 4.5 Conceptual Diagram: Space Centrode and Body Centrode

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M0["Motion of a Rigid Body in a Plane"] --> M1["Space Centrode"]
    M0 --> M2["Body Centrode"]
    M1 --> M1A["Locus of IC in the fixed frame over one full cycle"]
    M1 --> M1B["Also called Fixed Centrode"]
    M2 --> M2A["Locus of IC as observed on the moving link itself"]
    M2 --> M2B["Also called Moving Centrode"]
    M1A --> M3["Pure rolling contact between the two centrodes at every instant"]
    M2A --> M3
    M3 --> M4["Engineering Use: Design of conjugate cam profiles, gear tooth profiles, path generators"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define an Instantaneous Center. How is the number of instantaneous centers in a mechanism with $n$ links determined?**

**Model Answer:**
An Instantaneous Center (IC) is a point about which a rigid body (link) is considered to be rotating at a given instant. It is the point on or about the link that has zero linear velocity at that instant.

The number of ICs in a mechanism with $n$ moving links is given by:

$$N = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$$

This is because each IC is a mutual property of a pair of links, and the number of distinct pairs from $n$ links is the combination $\binom{n}{2}$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q2. State the Aronhold-Kennedy theorem of three centers in line. Mention its significance.**

**Model Answer:**
**Statement:** When three rigid bodies have plane relative motion, the three instantaneous centers of the three pairs of bodies must lie on a single straight line (or be coincident).

**Significance:**
1. It is used to **locate the relative (secondary) instantaneous centers** in a mechanism.
2. By drawing straight lines through pairs of known primary ICs, the intersection point of two such lines gives the location of a secondary IC.
3. It provides a **graphical means** of finding ICs without solving the velocity equations analytically.
4. It forms the foundation of the **velocity polygon / IC method** of mechanism analysis.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 1 | 14 Marks]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** A four-bar mechanism $ABCD$ has links of lengths $AB = 100 \text{ mm}, \ BC = 200 \text{ mm}, \ CD = 150 \text{ mm}, \ AD = 250 \text{ mm}$. The crank $AB$ rotates uniformly at $\omega_{AB} = 20 \text{ rad/s}$ in the clockwise direction. Find the velocity of the midpoint of the coupler $BC$ using the Instantaneous Center method. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Locate all six instantaneous centers of the four-bar mechanism $ABCD$ and explain the procedure using Kennedy's theorem. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Velocity of Midpoint of Coupler:**

**Step 1:** Draw the configuration diagram to scale.

**Step 2:** Calculate the velocity of point $B$ (pin joint between link 2 and link 3):

$$V_B = \omega_{AB} \times AB = 20 \times 0.100 = 2.0 \text{ m/s}$$

The direction of $V_B$ is perpendicular to $AB$, in the sense of rotation.

**Step 3:** Locate $I_{13}$ (relative IC between frame 1 and coupler 3) using Kennedy's theorem:
- Line 1: Extend $AB$ and $CD$ — they meet at $I_{14}$ (already the fixed pivot $D$).
- Line 2: The line through $I_{12}$ and $I_{14}$ intersects the line through $I_{23}$ and $I_{34}$ at the point $I_{13}$.

**Step 4:** Measure $I_{13} B$ and $I_{13} M$ (where $M$ is the midpoint of $BC$) from the scaled diagram.

Suppose (from the diagram) $I_{13} B = 250 \text{ mm}$ and $I_{13} M = 220 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 5:** Calculate the angular velocity of the coupler:

$$\omega_3 = \frac{V_B}{I_{13} B} = \frac{2.0}{0.250} = 8.0 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 6:** Calculate the velocity of the midpoint $M$:

$$V_M = \omega_3 \times I_{13} M = 8.0 \times 0.220 = 1.76 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{V_M = 1.76 \text{ m/s}, \ \text{perpendicular to } I_{13} M}$$

---

**Part (b) — Location of All Six ICs:**

For a four-bar mechanism with $n = 4$ links:

$$N = \frac{4 \times 3}{2} = 6 \text{ ICs}$$

The six ICs are: $I_{12}, I_{13}, I_{14}, I_{23}, I_{24}, I_{34}$.

**Step 1: Locate the four primary ICs at the pin joints:**
- $I_{12}$ at point $A$ (pivot of input crank).
- $I_{23}$ at point $B$ (pivot between crank and coupler).
- $I_{34}$ at point $C$ (pivot between coupler and rocker).
- $I_{14}$ at point $D$ (pivot of output rocker on the frame).

**Step 2: Locate the two secondary ICs using Kennedy's theorem:**

To find $I_{13}$ (between frame 1 and coupler 3):
- Consider the three links 1, 2, 3 — by Kennedy's theorem, $I_{12}, I_{13}, I_{23}$ are collinear.
- Since $I_{12}$ and $I_{23}$ are known (at $A$ and $B$ respectively), draw line $AB$ and extend it.
- Consider another triple — links 1, 3, 4 — by Kennedy's theorem, $I_{13}, I_{14}, I_{34}$ are collinear.
- Since $I_{14}$ and $I_{34}$ are known (at $D$ and $C$ respectively), draw line $DC$ and extend it.
- The intersection of lines $AB$ and $DC$ gives the point $I_{13}$.

To find $I_{24}$ (between crank 2 and rocker 4):
- Triple 1, 2, 4 gives the collinearity of $I_{12}, I_{24}, I_{14}$ — draw line $AD$.
- Triple 2, 3, 4 gives the collinearity of $I_{23}, I_{24}, I_{34}$ — draw line $BC$.
- The intersection of $AD$ and $BC$ gives $I_{24}$.

**Step 3: Verification using Carnot's Theorem:**

For the four-bar mechanism:

$$I_{12}I_{14} \cdot I_{23}I_{34} = (I_{12}I_{13} \cdot I_{23}I_{14}) + (I_{14}I_{13} \cdot I_{23}I_{34})$$

Substitute the measured values from the scaled diagram to verify.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not forget the extension:** When lines $AB$ and $DC$ do not intersect within the physical mechanism, the ICs may lie **outside** the physical link. Always extend the lines.
> - **Direction of velocity:** The velocity is **perpendicular to the line joining the point to the IC** — not parallel. Many students lose 1-2 marks by getting the direction wrong.
> - **Scale conversion:** Remember to convert mm from the diagram to meters for velocity calculations in SI units. $100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**(a)** A slider-crank mechanism has crank length $r = 60 \text{ mm}$ and connecting rod length $l = 200 \text{ mm}$. The crank rotates at $N = 300 \text{ rpm}$ clockwise. Using the Instantaneous Center method, determine the velocity of the slider and the angular velocity of the connecting rod when the crank is at $45°$ from the inner dead center. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **Space Centrode** and **Body Centrode** of a four-bar mechanism. Discuss the concept of rolling and sliding of the centrodes. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Velocity of Slider Using IC Method:**

**Given:**
- Crank length $r = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.060 \text{ m}$.
- Connecting rod length $l = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.200 \text{ m}$.
- Crank speed $N = 300 \text{ rpm}$.
- Crank angle $\theta = 45°$ from IDC.

**Step 1:** Angular velocity of the crank:

$$\omega_2 = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 300}{60} = 31.416 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2:** Velocity of the crank pin $B$:

$$V_B = \omega_2 \times r = 31.416 \times 0.060 = 1.885 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3:** Locate the instantaneous centers for the slider-crank mechanism.

Number of links $n = 4$ (frame, crank, connecting rod, slider), so $N = 6$ ICs.

The primary ICs are:
- $I_{12}$ at the crank center $O$.
- $I_{23}$ at the crank pin $B$.
- $I_{34}$ at the slider (line of stroke).
- $I_{14}$ at infinity (along the slider's line of motion).

**Step 4:** Locate the relative ICs:
- $I_{13}$ is found by Kennedy's theorem from links 1, 2, 3 — intersection of the line through $I_{12}$ and $I_{23}$ (i.e., line $OB$) with the line of stroke of the slider.
- $I_{24}$ is found from links 1, 2, 4 — $I_{14}$ is at infinity along the line of stroke, so $I_{12} I_{24}$ is parallel to the line of stroke. Combined with $I_{23} I_{34}$ collinear (which gives the line through $B$ parallel to the stroke), the intersection gives $I_{24}$.

**Step 5:** From the geometry, $I_{13} B = \dfrac{l}{\cos\phi}$ where $\phi$ is the connecting rod angle.

Using the slider-crank equation:

$$\sin\phi = \frac{r \sin\theta}{l} = \frac{60 \times \sin 45°}{200} = \frac{60 \times 0.7071}{200} = 0.2121$$

$$\phi = 12.25°$$

**Step 6:** Distance $I_{13} B$:

$$I_{13} B = \frac{l}{\cos\phi} = \frac{200}{\cos 12.25°} = \frac{200}{0.9771} = 204.7 \text{ mm} = 0.2047 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7:** Distance $I_{13} C$ (from IC to slider point $C$):

The perpendicular distance from $I_{13}$ to the line of stroke equals the vertical height of $I_{13}$ above the stroke line, which can be computed from the geometry:

$$I_{13} C = \frac{r \sin(\theta + \phi)}{\cos\phi}$$

Substituting the values:

$$I_{13} C = \frac{60 \times \sin(45° + 12.25°)}{\cos 12.25°} = \frac{60 \times \sin 57.25°}{0.9771} = \frac{60 \times 0.841}{0.9771} = 51.6 \text{ mm} = 0.0516 \text{ m}$$

**Step 8:** Angular velocity of the connecting rod:

$$\omega_3 = \frac{V_B}{I_{13} B} = \frac{1.885}{0.2047} = 9.21 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 9:** Velocity of the slider:

$$V_C = \omega_3 \times I_{13} C = 9.21 \times 0.0516 = 0.475 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{V_C = 0.475 \text{ m/s}, \quad \omega_3 = 9.21 \text{ rad/s}}$$

---

**Part (b) — Space Centrode and Body Centrode:**

**Space Centrode (or Fixed Centrode):**
The space centrode is the locus of the instantaneous center of rotation in the **fixed reference frame** as the mechanism moves through its cycle. It is fixed in space and does not move with the link.

**Body Centrode (or Moving Centrode):**
The body centrode is the locus of the instantaneous center of rotation as observed **on the moving link itself**. It moves with the link.

**Sketch Description:**

Imagine the coupler of a four-bar mechanism. As the mechanism cycles, the IC of the coupler (relative to the frame) traces a curve in the fixed plane — this is the space centrode. The same IC, when marked on the coupler itself, traces a different curve on the coupler — this is the body centrode.

**Rolling and Sliding:**
The two centrodes are in **pure rolling contact** at every instant. The instantaneous center of rotation is the point of contact between the two centrodes at that instant.

> If the two centrodes were rigidly attached and rolled on each other without slipping, the coupler would have the same motion as in the four-bar mechanism — this is the **kinematic equivalence** principle used in **conjugate cam design** and **gear tooth generation**.

The rolling is generally **pure rolling** (no slipping) at the IC, but away from the IC, the centrodes may exhibit both rolling and sliding components — the motion is a **general plane motion**.

**Engineering Application:**
- In **gear design**, the involute tooth profile is generated by ensuring the body centrode of one gear rolls on the space centrode of the mating gear.
- In **cam design**, the conjugate cam profile is derived by rolling the body centrode on the space centrode to obtain the equivalent mechanism.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls in Slider-Crank Problems:**
> - The IC $I_{14}$ of a slider-crank mechanism lies at **infinity** (perpendicular to the line of stroke) — forgetting this leads to a totally wrong location of $I_{24}$ and $I_{13}$.
> - The slider's velocity is **along the line of stroke** — students often mistakenly mark it perpendicular to $I_{13} C$. The correct direction is the constraint direction (line of stroke), and the IC method gives the *magnitude*; the *direction* is dictated by the slider's physical constraint.
> - Always convert rpm to rad/s using $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ before any calculation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read 5 Minutes Before the Exam**

- **Number of ICs Formula:** $N = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ — memorize this and be ready to apply it for any $n$ (4, 5, 6, 7 bar mechanisms).
- **Aronhold-Kennedy Theorem:** $I_{12}, I_{13}, I_{23}$ are collinear for any three links 1, 2, 3. This is your primary tool for finding **secondary** ICs.
- **Primary vs Secondary ICs:** Primary = at actual pin joints. Secondary = located using Kennedy's theorem.
- **Ternary ICs:** Occur when three or more links share a common pivot. They are simultaneously primary for one pair and secondary for others.
- **Velocity Formula:** $V_A = \omega \times r_{A \to IC}$ — use this with the link's angular velocity and the perpendicular distance from the IC to point $A$.
- **Angular Velocity Ratio:** $\dfrac{\omega_2}{\omega_3} = \dfrac{I_{13} I_{23}}{I_{12} I_{23}}$ — the foundation of every velocity analysis.
- **Centroles:** Space centrode (fixed) and body centrode (moving) — they roll on each other at the IC.
- **Carnot's Theorem:** Used as a cross-check for the IC distances in a four-bar mechanism: $I_{12}I_{14} \cdot I_{23}I_{34} = I_{12}I_{13} \cdot I_{23}I_{14} + I_{14}I_{13} \cdot I_{23}I_{34}$.
- **Unit Conversion:** Always work in SI (meters, rad/s) — convert mm and rpm before substituting.
- **Drawing the Diagram to Scale:** The IC method is a **graphical** technique. The accuracy of your answer depends on the precision of the scaled drawing.
- **Direction of Velocity:** Always **perpendicular** to the line from the IC to the point — in the sense of rotation.
- **Slider-Crank Special Case:** $I_{14}$ lies at infinity (perpendicular to the line of stroke). This is unique to the slider-crank mechanism.
- **KTU Board Pattern:** Expect a 7-mark problem asking you to locate all ICs and a 7-mark problem asking for velocity of a specific point. Practice both.
- **Common Errors:** Forgetting to extend lines, ignoring the slider-crank infinity IC, missing the unit conversion, mixing up the rotation sense of velocity vectors.
- **Real-World Use:** Automotive steering, robot manipulators, cam design, gear train analysis, and printing press mechanisms all rely on the IC method.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aronhold-Kennedy Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT412 - KINEMATICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Aronhold-Kennedy Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Aronhold-Kennedy Theorem: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

The **Aronhold-Kennedy Theorem** is a foundational theorem in the kinematics of mechanisms that governs the behaviour of **instant centers of rotation** (also called *instantaneous centers*, *centrodes*, or *instant poles*). The theorem states:

> **"When three or more rigid links move relative to one another in a plane, their respective instant centers lie on a single straight line, known as the *Kennedy line* or *Aronhold line*."**

This theorem is one of three classical theorems in instant center analysis, and it forms the mathematical backbone of the graphical velocity analysis method for planar mechanisms such as the **four-bar linkage**, **slider-crank mechanism**, and **Whitworth quick-return mechanism**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Aronhold-Kennedy Theorem is the gateway concept for solving Part B (14-mark) problems on velocity analysis using the instant center method. Mastery of this theorem is **non-negotiable** for ESE (End Semester Examination).

## Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are watching three spinning merry-go-rounds on a playground from above:

- Each merry-go-round (representing a **rigid link**) spins about a point that is *not fixed* in space — it shifts every instant.
- However, if you draw a **straight line** connecting the centres of any two merry-go-rounds, the third merry-go-round's spinning centre will **always lie on this same line** at that instant.

This is precisely what the Aronhold-Kennedy Theorem asserts. It gives us a **ruler-and-compass-free** way to locate the instantaneous axis of rotation for any link in a complex mechanism.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics:**
> - The theorem applies strictly to **planar (2D) mechanisms**.
> - For spatial mechanisms, a separate theorem (using instantaneous screws) applies.
> - The instant center of a link pair is also called **centrode** or **pole of relative motion**.

---

## Why is this Theorem Important?

1. **Velocity Determination:** Once instant centers are known, the velocity of any point on any link can be found using only the magnitude of one known velocity.
2. **Mechanical Efficiency:** No need for vector loop equations or relative velocity polygons for every link.
3. **Graphical Simplicity:** It transforms a complex vector problem into a ruler-and-protractor geometry exercise.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## The Three Theorems of Instant Centers

The Aronhold-Kennedy Theorem is actually one component of a broader set of three theorems on instant centers, each discovered sequentially by **Aronhold (1872)** and **Kennedy (1886)**.

### Theorem 1 — Primary (Trivial) Instant Centers

> Every pin joint between two links is itself an instant center for those two links.

- These are **immediately obvious** by inspection.
- Number of primary instant centers = **(n − 1)**, where *n* is the total number of links.

### Theorem 2 — Existence of Instant Center Between Any Two Links

> Any two links in a mechanism, whether connected directly by a pin joint or not, possess an instant center. It can be found by using the Aronhold-Kennedy Theorem (Theorem 3) repeatedly.

- This guarantees that the **total number of possible instant centers** is finite and countable.

### Theorem 3 — Aronhold-Kennedy Theorem (The Main Theorem)

> **Any three links moving relatively to one another have three instant centers that lie on a straight line.**

- This is the heart of the instant center method.
- It allows us to **graphically locate** secondary instant centers using only primary ones.

## Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

Let us denote the instant center between link *i* and link *j* as **I_{ij}**.

- **Step 1 — Identification:** Identify all primary (pin-joint) instant centers by inspecting the mechanism diagram.
- **Step 2 — Count:** Calculate the total number of instant centers required using the formula $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$.
- **Step 3 — Locate:** For each unknown secondary instant center, identify three links (commonly involving two primary centers) and apply Kennedy's theorem to draw the straight line.
- **Step 4 — Intersect:** The intersection of two such straight lines locates the required instant center uniquely.
- **Step 5 — Apply:** Use the located instant centers in the velocity equation $\dfrac{v_B}{v_A} = \dfrac{\text{distance from } I \text{ to } B}{\text{distance from } I \text{ to } A}$.

## KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula / Symbol | Boundary / Unit | Application |
|---------|-----------------|-----------------|-------------|
| Total Instant Centers | $N = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | n = number of links | Counting all possible instant centers |
| Primary (Trivial) Centers | $P = n - 1$ | At pin joints | Direct inspection |
| Secondary Centers | $S = N - P = \dfrac{(n-1)(n-2)}{2}$ | Found by Kennedy lines | Require graphical construction |
| Velocity Ratio | $\dfrac{v_B}{v_A} = \dfrac{IB}{IA}$ | Pure ratio (no units) | Velocity at point B |
| Angular Velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{v_A}{IA}$ | rad/s | Angular velocity of link |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Instant center analysis is used in the design of IC engine valve mechanisms, quick-return mechanisms, sewing machine drives, and robotic arm linkages.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1 — Total Number of Instant Centers

Consider a planar mechanism with **n** rigid links.

**Statement to Prove:** The total number of instant centers is $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$.

### Proof (Combinatorial Argument)

**Step 1:** An instant center exists for **every pair** of links in a relative motion system. This follows directly from Theorem 2.

**Step 2:** The number of *ordered* pairs of links chosen from n links is given by the permutation formula:

$$
\text{Ordered Pairs} = {}^nP_2 = n \times (n-1)
$$

**Step 3:** However, an instant center between link *i* and link *j* is **identical** to the instant center between link *j* and link *i*. In other words, $I_{ij} \equiv I_{ji}$.

**Step 4:** Hence, the count of *distinct* (unordered) pairs is the combination formula:

$$
\text{Distinct Pairs} = {}^nC_2 = \dfrac{n!}{2!(n-2)!}
$$

**Step 5:** Simplifying the factorial expression:

$$
\begin{aligned}
{}^nC_2 &= \dfrac{n \cdot (n-1) \cdot (n-2)!}{2 \cdot 1 \cdot (n-2)!} \\
&= \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Conclusion:

$$
\boxed{N = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}}
$$

**Each distinct pair corresponds to exactly one instant center.** Hence proved.

---

## Derivation 2 — Number of Secondary Instant Centers

The number of secondary centers is the total minus the primary ones:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= N - P \\
&= \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2} - (n-1) \\
&= (n-1)\left[\dfrac{n}{2} - 1\right] \\
&= \dfrac{(n-1)(n-2)}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{S = \dfrac{(n-1)(n-2)}{2}}
$$

---

## Verification Table for Common Mechanisms

| Mechanism Type | n (links) | N (total) | P (primary) | S (secondary) |
|----------------|-----------|-----------|-------------|---------------|
| Four-bar linkage | 4 | 6 | 3 | 3 |
| Slider-crank | 4 | 6 | 3 | 3 |
| Five-bar linkage | 5 | 10 | 4 | 6 |
| Six-bar Stephenson | 6 | 15 | 5 | 10 |
| Seven-bar Watt | 7 | 21 | 6 | 15 |

---

## Proof of Kennedy's Theorem (Geometric Argument)

**Setup:** Consider three links 1, 2, and 3 moving in a plane. Let:
- $I_{12}$ = instant center of links 1 and 2.
- $I_{23}$ = instant center of links 2 and 3.
- $I_{13}$ = instant center of links 1 and 3.

**Geometric Observation:**

Consider a coincident point **P** on links 1 and 2. Its velocity is:

$$
\vec{v}_P^{(1)} = \vec{v}_P^{(2)} \quad \text{(since they share the point)}
$$

Similarly, point **Q** on links 2 and 3 has:

$$
\vec{v}_Q^{(2)} = \vec{v}_Q^{(3)}
$$

**Velocity of any point on link 2 about $I_{12}$:**

A point on link 2 located at distance $r$ from $I_{12}$ has velocity perpendicular to the line joining it to $I_{12}$, with magnitude $\omega_2 \cdot r$.

**Combining both equations**, the only geometry consistent with both constraints is that **$I_{12}$, $I_{23}$, and $I_{13}$ are collinear**.

Hence:

$$
\boxed{I_{12}, \, I_{23}, \, I_{13} \text{ are collinear.}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This collinearity is precisely what allows us to "draw a straight line through two known instant centers to find the third" — the practical engineer's rule.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1 — Concept Map of Instant Center Theorems

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Instant Center Theory"] --> B["Theorem 1: Primary Centers"]
    A --> C["Theorem 2: Existence"]
    A --> D["Theorem 3: Aronhold-Kennedy"]
    B --> E["Located at Pin Joints"]
    B --> F["Count equals n minus 1"]
    C --> G["Every link pair has an IC"]
    C --> H["Total equals n(n-1)/2"]
    D --> I["Three ICs are collinear"]
    D --> J["Enables graphical location"]
    E --> K["Direct Inspection"]
    F --> K
    G --> L["Combinatorial Counting"]
    H --> L
    I --> M["Kennedy Line Construction"]
    J --> M
    K --> N["Solve for Velocities"]
    L --> N
    M --> N
```

## Diagram 2 — Functional Flow of Velocity Analysis Using Aronhold-Kennedy Theorem

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1["Step 1: Identify n links"] --> S2["Step 2: Calculate N total ICs"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Locate Primary ICs by inspection"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Choose triples of links"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Apply Kennedy collinearity"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6: Draw Kennedy lines"]
    S6 --> S7["Step 7: Locate Secondary ICs at intersections"]
    S7 --> S8["Step 8: Compute velocities using IC distances"]
```

## Diagram 3 — Block Architecture of a Four-Bar Instant Center Layout

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1["Link 1: Ground Frame"] -->|IC at pivot A| L2
    L2["Link 2: Input Crank"] -->|IC at pivot B| L3
    L3["Link 3: Coupler"] -->|IC at pivot C| L4
    L4["Link 4: Output Rocker"] --> L1
    L2 -.->|Secondary IC 13| L3
    L2 -.->|Secondary IC 14| L4
    L3 -.->|Secondary IC 24| L4
    L1 -.->|Fixed Pin| L4
    L1 -.->|Fixed Pin| L2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of Diagram 3:** In a four-bar mechanism, there are 3 primary (solid arrows) and 3 secondary (dotted arrows) instant centers, totaling **N = 6** as per the formula.

## Diagram 4 — Kennedys Theorem Visualization

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["I12 on Line L1"] --> B["I23 on Line L1"]
    B --> C["I13 on Line L1"]
    C --> D["Common Straight Line (Kennedy Line)"]
```

> This illustrates the collinearity rule: the three instant centers for any three relatively moving links share a single straight line at every instant.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: State the Aronhold-Kennedy Theorem for instant centers of a mechanism.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Aronhold-Kennedy Theorem states that *when three links of a mechanism move relative to one another in a plane, their three instant centers lie on a single straight line*.

This theorem provides the geometric principle for locating the secondary instant centers graphically using primary (pin-joint) centers. By drawing straight lines through known primary centers involving common links, the intersection of two such Kennedy lines yields the required secondary instant center, enabling rapid velocity analysis.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Definition (2 marks): Three links, collinear, planar motion.
- Application mention (1 mark): Used to locate secondary ICs.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: A mechanism has 6 links. Calculate the total number, primary, and secondary instant centers.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Given n = 6 links.

**Total Instant Centers:**

$$
N = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2} = \dfrac{6 \times 5}{2} = 15
$$

**Primary Instant Centers (at pin joints):**

$$
P = n - 1 = 6 - 1 = 5
$$

**Secondary Instant Centers (to be located graphically):**

$$
S = N - P = 15 - 5 = 10
$$

**Mark Distribution:**
- Total (1 mark), Primary (1 mark), Secondary (1 mark).

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - May 2023] — Module 1, Choice 1

**In a four-bar mechanism ABCD with fixed link AD, link AB = 100 mm rotates at 60 rpm clockwise. The lengths are: AB = 100 mm, BC = 200 mm, CD = 150 mm, AD = 250 mm. Locate all instant centers and find the angular velocity of link CD.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Locate all six instant centers

**Solution:**

Number of links: n = 4.
Total instant centers: $N = \dfrac{4 \times 3}{2} = 6$.
Primary: $P = 3$ (at A, B, C).
Secondary: $S = 3$.

**Primary Instant Centers:**
- $I_{12}$ (Link 1 & 2) at pin **A**
- $I_{23}$ (Link 2 & 3) at pin **B**
- $I_{34}$ (Link 3 & 4) at pin **C**
- $I_{14}$ (Link 1 & 4) at pin **D**

**Locating Secondary Centers using Kennedy's Theorem:**

Consider links 1, 2, 4: $I_{12}$ (at A), $I_{14}$ (at D), $I_{24}$ are collinear.

Thus $I_{24}$ lies on line **AD extended**.

Consider links 2, 3, 4: $I_{23}$ (at B), $I_{34}$ (at C), $I_{24}$ are collinear.

Thus $I_{24}$ also lies on line **BC extended**.

Therefore, $I_{24}$ = intersection of lines AD and BC extended.

Consider links 1, 2, 3: $I_{12}$ (at A), $I_{23}$ (at B), $I_{13}$ are collinear.

Thus $I_{13}$ lies on line **AB extended**.

Consider links 1, 3, 4: $I_{34}$ (at C), $I_{14}$ (at D), $I_{13}$ are collinear.

Thus $I_{13}$ also lies on line **CD extended**.

Therefore, $I_{13}$ = intersection of lines AB and CD extended.

Consider links 1, 2, 4: already used.
Consider links 1, 3, 4: already used.
Consider links 2, 3, 4: $I_{23}$, $I_{34}$, $I_{24}$ collinear — confirmed.

The remaining secondary center $I_{14}$ is itself a primary at D.

**Final listing:**
- Primary: A ($I_{12}$), B ($I_{23}$), C ($I_{34}$), D ($I_{14}$)
- Secondary: $I_{13}$ (AB ∩ CD extended), $I_{24}$ (AD ∩ BC extended), plus the trivial $I_{14}$ at D.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Angular velocity of link CD

**Given:**
- $\omega_2 = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 60}{60} = 2\pi$ rad/s
- Velocity at B: $v_B = \omega_2 \times AB = 2\pi \times 0.1 = 0.2\pi$ m/s

Using instant center $I_{13}$ (between links 1 and 3):

$$
\dfrac{v_C}{v_B} = \dfrac{I_{13}C}{I_{13}B}
$$

Therefore:

$$
v_C = v_B \times \dfrac{I_{13}C}{I_{13}B}
$$

For link CD rotating about D:

$$
\omega_4 = \dfrac{v_C}{CD} = \dfrac{0.2\pi \times \dfrac{I_{13}C}{I_{13}B}}{0.15}
$$

Numerically, once $I_{13}$ is located graphically (using a scale drawing), the ratio $I_{13}C / I_{13}B$ can be measured.

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\omega_4 = \dfrac{v_B \cdot I_{13}C}{I_{13}B \cdot CD} \text{ rad/s}}
$$

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] — Module 1, Choice 2

**Explain the three theorems on instant centers with a suitable example of a slider-crank mechanism. The crank rotates at 300 rpm. Determine the velocity of the slider if crank length = 50 mm and connecting rod length = 200 mm.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: The Three Theorems

**Solution:**

**Theorem 1 — Primary Instant Centers:**

The instant centers located at the pin joints of a mechanism are called primary or trivial instant centers. Their number is $(n-1)$, where $n$ is the number of links.

In a slider-crank (4 links: ground, crank, connecting rod, slider), the primary centers are:
- $I_{12}$ at the crank pivot
- $I_{23}$ at the crank-pin (big-end)
- $I_{34}$ at the gudgeon pin (small-end)

**Theorem 2 — Existence of All Instant Centers:**

Any two links in a mechanism, even those not directly connected by a pin, possess a unique instant center. The total number of instant centers in any mechanism is:

$$
N = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}
$$

For a slider-crank: $N = \dfrac{4 \times 3}{2} = 6$.

**Theorem 3 — Aronhold-Kennedy Theorem:**

When three links move relatively, their three instant centers are collinear (lie on a single straight line).

For example, $I_{13}$, $I_{12}$, and $I_{23}$ lie on a straight line passing through the crank pivot and big-end pin.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Velocity of slider

**Given:**
- Crank speed: $N = 300$ rpm
- $\omega_2 = \dfrac{2\pi \times 300}{60} = 10\pi$ rad/s
- Crank length: $r = 50$ mm = $0.05$ m
- Connecting rod: $l = 200$ mm = $0.2$ m

**Velocity of crank pin B:**

$$
v_B = \omega_2 \times r = 10\pi \times 0.05 = 0.5\pi \text{ m/s}
$$

**Using instant center $I_{13}$:**

The location of $I_{13}$ is found by Kennedy's theorem. From similarity of triangles in the slider-crank:

$$
I_{13}B = \dfrac{l^2}{2r} \quad \text{(approximately, for short crank)}
$$

More accurately (using exact geometry):

The slider velocity from instant center analysis:

$$
v_C = v_B \times \dfrac{I_{13}C}{I_{13}B}
$$

For slider-crank, this simplifies to:

$$
v_C = \omega_2 \times r \left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{r \sin^2\theta}{l}\right)
$$

where $\theta$ is the crank angle from inner dead centre.

**At $\theta = 0$ (slider at extreme position):**

$$
v_C = 10\pi \times 0.05 \times 1 = 0.5\pi \approx 1.571 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{v_C \approx 1.571 \text{ m/s (maximum, at $\theta = 0$)} }
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** Mixing mm with m in velocity calculations is the #1 mark killer. Always express crank length in **metres** before computing velocity.
> 2. **Wrong identification of $I_{ij}$:** Students often swap the indices. Remember — $I_{12}$ is between link 1 and link 2, not link 1 and link 12.
> 3. **Missing collinearity check:** When locating a secondary center, you need **two Kennedy lines** intersecting. Drawing only one line does not pin down the center.
> 4. **Direction of rotation confusion:** Always indicate CW or CCW in the velocity diagram; marks are deducted for missing direction.
> 5. **Not drawing lines to scale:** Graphical accuracy directly affects your computed values in instant center problems.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Theorem 1 (Primary):** Pin joints themselves are instant centers; count = $(n-1)$.
- **Theorem 2 (Existence):** Every link pair has one instant center; total count = $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$.
- **Theorem 3 (Aronhold-Kennedy):** Three relatively moving links have three instant centers lying on a **straight line**.
- **Primary vs Secondary:** Primary = directly visible at pivots; Secondary = located by Kennedy lines.
- **Velocity Formula:** $v_B = \omega \cdot r_{IB}$ where $r_{IB}$ is the distance from instant center to point B.
- **Angular Velocity:** $\omega = \dfrac{v}{r}$ — apply to any point on a rotating link about its IC.
- **Planar Restriction:** The theorem strictly applies to **planar mechanisms**, not spatial ones.
- **Graphical Tool:** A scale drawing (1 cm = 50 mm typical) is essential for numerical answers.
- **Common Mechanisms:** Four-bar, slider-crank, and Whitworth are the most-frequently tested in KTU exams.
- **Counting Tip:** Always compute $N$, $P$, $S$ first before drawing; this prevents missing centers.
- **Collinearity Construction:** Draw line through 2 known ICs → intersects another Kennedy line → gives the new IC.
- **Answer Format:** KTU expects **free-body diagrams**, **scale dimensions**, and **clear mention of $\omega$ direction**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Velocity and Acceleration Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT412 - KINEMATICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Velocity and Acceleration Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Velocity Analysis — Formal Definition

**Velocity analysis** of a mechanism is the graphical or analytical procedure used to determine the linear and angular velocities of all the links and points in a mechanism for a given configuration and a known input angular velocity ($\omega$). It establishes *how fast* each part of a machine moves without considering the forces causing that motion.

Mathematically, the velocity of any point $B$ on a rigid link is related to another point $A$ on the same link by the **fundamental relative velocity equation**:

$$\vec{V}_B = \vec{V}_A + \vec{V}_{BA}$$

where $\vec{V}_{BA}$ is the velocity of $B$ with respect to $A$, directed **perpendicular** to the line $AB$ and having magnitude $\omega \cdot \vert AB \vert$ (where $\omega$ is the angular velocity of the link).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "perpendicular"?** For a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis through $A$, the point $B$ traces a circular arc around $A$. The instantaneous velocity is always tangent to that arc, hence perpendicular to the radius $AB$.

### 1.2 Acceleration Analysis — Formal Definition

**Acceleration analysis** determines the linear and angular accelerations of all the links in a mechanism for a given input motion. It is a necessary preliminary to force analysis, dynamic balancing, and vibration studies.

The fundamental **relative acceleration equation** is:

$$\vec{a}_B = \vec{a}_A + \vec{a}_{BA}^t + \vec{a}_{BA}^n$$

where:
- $\vec{a}_{BA}^t$ = tangential component of acceleration of $B$ w.r.t. $A$ = $\alpha \cdot \vert AB \vert$ (perpendicular to $AB$)
- $\vec{a}_{BA}^n$ = normal (centripetal) component of acceleration of $B$ w.r.t. $A$ = $\omega^2 \cdot \vert AB \vert$ (directed from $B$ towards $A$)
- $\alpha$ = angular acceleration of the link $AB$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coriolis Acceleration:** When a point slides on a link that is itself rotating, an additional component — the **Coriolis component** — appears:
> $$\vec{a}_c = 2\,\vec{\omega} \times \vec{V}_{rel}$$
> This component has a magnitude $a_c = 2\omega \cdot V_{rel}$ and is directed by rotating the relative velocity vector $V_{rel}$ by $90°$ in the sense of $\omega$.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English

Imagine a **spinning merry-go-round** in a playground. A child sitting on a horse at the rim is moving in a circle, so the child has a velocity tangent to the circle and an acceleration pointing toward the center (centripetal). Now imagine a second child **sliding outward** along a radial spoke of the merry-go-round while it spins. From a stationary observer on the ground, the sliding child's path is a spiral, and the child has an extra sideways "push" — that sideways push is the **Coriolis acceleration**. The kid on the horse corresponds to rigid-body rotation; the sliding kid corresponds to a slider on a rotating link. **Velocity and acceleration analysis** is the engineering method for tracking exactly these motions for every part of a machine.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

- Angular velocity unit: **rad/s** (SI) or **rpm** (N)
- Conversion: $\omega \,(\text{rad/s}) = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$
- Linear velocity unit: **m/s**
- Linear acceleration unit: **m/s²**
- Standard gravitational reference (only when required for gravity components): **$g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$**
- Standard crank length ranges in textbook problems: **$r = 25$ mm to $300$ mm**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Principles

A mechanism is composed of **rigid links** connected by **kinematic pairs**. The analysis is grounded in two foundational laws of rigid-body kinematics:

1. **The Rigid-Body Constraint (Fixed Distance):** The distance between any two points on a single rigid link is invariant. This produces the **perpendicularity rule** for velocities and the existence of only two acceleration components (tangential + normal) between two points of a rigid link.
2. **The Instantaneous Geometry Rule:** At any instant, the configuration of the mechanism is fully described by its position diagram (drawn to scale). All velocity and acceleration vectors are then constructed graphically using the relative-motion equations.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Procedure (Relative Velocity/Acceleration Method)

**Step 1 — Draw the configuration diagram** to a suitable scale at the given crank angle. Label all points (e.g., $O_2, O_4, A, B$).

**Step 2 — Choose the input angular velocity** $\omega_2$ (direction known from the driver). Identify the unknown angular velocities $\omega_3, \omega_4, \dots$ and unknown linear velocities to be found.

**Step 3 — Apply the relative velocity equation** to each moving link, link by link. For each equation, two unknowns must remain to be solvable by the **velocity polygon (triangle of velocities)**.

**Step 4 — Construct the velocity polygon** by:
- Drawing $\vec{o}\,a \parallel$ to $V_A$ with a chosen velocity scale.
- Drawing a line through $a$ perpendicular to $AB$ to locate $b$ (intersection of two such lines for two unknowns).

**Step 5 — Measure magnitudes** from the polygon using the velocity scale $k_v$ (m/s per mm), and **measure directions** of vectors to compute unknown angular velocities using $\omega = \dfrac{V}{\vert \text{link} \vert}$.

**Step 6 — For acceleration analysis**, apply the relative acceleration equation **at every joint**, marking four components for non-slider links: $\vec{a}_A$, $\vec{a}_{BA}^t$ ($\perp$ to $AB$), $\vec{a}_{BA}^n$ ($B \to A$), and the $\vec{a}_B$ vector of unknown direction.

**Step 7 — Construct the acceleration polygon** starting from a known pole $o'$. Two unknowns (magnitude of $a_B$ and $\alpha_3$) are typically left for the final closure, solved by drawing two locating lines and finding their intersection.

### 2.3 Kennedy's Theorem on Instantaneous Centers

> [!NOTE]
> **Kennedy's Theorem:** *Any three bodies having relative motion will have three instantaneous centers, and these three instantaneous centers lie on a single straight line.*

This is the foundation of the **Aronhold-Kennedy Theorem**, enabling rapid determination of velocities without solving vector equations. For a four-bar mechanism with 4 links, the number of instantaneous centers is:

$$N = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2} = \dfrac{4 \times 3}{2} = 6$$

### 2.4 Coriolis Component — Origin & Conditions

A Coriolis component of acceleration **only arises** when:
- A point is moving **along** (sliding on) a rotating link, **and**
- The two motions are **not parallel** to each other.

In an IC engine slider-crank, the slider moves along the cylinder axis, while the connecting rod rotates. The piston has Coriolis acceleration because of this sliding on a rotating link.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula reference** for velocity and acceleration analysis problems. Memorize the *direction* alongside the *magnitude* of every vector.

| # | Quantity | Vector Equation | Magnitude Formula | Direction |
|---|----------|-----------------|-------------------|-----------|
| 1 | Angular velocity conversion | — | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | rad/s |
| 2 | Linear velocity of $B$ on rotating link | $\vec{V}_B = \vec{V}_A + \vec{V}_{BA}$ | $V_B = V_A \pm \omega \cdot \vert AB \vert$ | $\vec{V}_{BA} \perp AB$ |
| 3 | Slider velocity (4-bar w/ slider) | $\vec{V}_C = \vec{V}_B + \vec{V}_{CB}$ | $V_C$ along slider axis | $\vec{V}_{CB} \perp BC$ |
| 4 | Instantaneous center velocity | — | $V_P = \omega \cdot \vert AP \vert$ | $\perp AP$ |
| 5 | Relative acceleration | $\vec{a}_B = \vec{a}_A + \vec{a}_{BA}^t + \vec{a}_{BA}^n$ | — | — |
| 6 | Tangential component | $\vec{a}_{BA}^t$ | $a_{BA}^t = \alpha \cdot \vert AB \vert$ | $\perp AB$ |
| 7 | Normal (centripetal) component | $\vec{a}_{BA}^n$ | $a_{BA}^n = \omega^2 \cdot \vert AB \vert$ | Along $B \to A$ |
| 8 | Coriolis component | $\vec{a}_c = 2\,\vec{\omega} \times \vec{V}_{rel}$ | $a_c = 2 \omega V_{rel}$ | Rotate $V_{rel}$ by $90°$ in sense of $\omega$ |
| 9 | Angular acceleration from polygon | — | $\alpha_3 = \dfrac{a_{BA}^t}{\vert AB \vert}$ | — |
| 10 | Number of instantaneous centers | — | $N = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | — |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Engine balancing and vibration analysis** in IC engines require slider-crank acceleration data.
- **Cam profile design** in high-speed automatics needs follower acceleration to avoid jump-impact (jounce).
- **Robot arm trajectory planning** uses the same 2D/3D acceleration equations (extended to spatial mechanisms).
- **Vehicle suspension and driveline** design uses four-bar acceleration results.
- **Printing and packaging machinery** uses velocity analysis to synchronize rollers.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Relative Velocity Equation

Let point $A$ and point $B$ be two points on a rigid link rotating about a fixed axis (or moving as part of a rigid body). We want to express $\vec{V}_B$ in terms of $\vec{V}_A$ and the relative motion of $B$ w.r.t. $A$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Position vector:} \quad & \vec{r}_B = \vec{r}_A + \vec{r}_{B/A} \\
\text{Differentiate w.r.t. time:} \quad & \dfrac{d\vec{r}_B}{dt} = \dfrac{d\vec{r}_A}{dt} + \dfrac{d\vec{r}_{B/A}}{dt} \\
\text{By definition of velocity:} \quad & \vec{V}_B = \vec{V}_A + \dfrac{d\vec{r}_{B/A}}{dt} \\
\text{Since } \vert \vec{r}_{B/A} \vert \text{ is constant (rigid body):} \quad & \dfrac{d\vec{r}_{B/A}}{dt} = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{B/A} \\
\text{Therefore:} \quad & \vec{V}_B = \vec{V}_A + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{B/A} \\
\text{Define:} \quad & \vec{V}_{B/A} = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{B/A} \\
\text{Final form:} \quad & \boxed{\vec{V}_B = \vec{V}_A + \vec{V}_{B/A}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic of each line:**
- Line 1: Position addition using vector triangle.
- Line 2: Differentiation because velocity is the time-derivative of position.
- Line 3: Substitution of the standard velocity definition.
- Line 4: Critical step — the rigid-body constraint $\vert \vec{r}_{B/A} \vert = $ const forces the derivative to be a pure rotation, which mathematically is a cross product with $\vec{\omega}$.
- Line 5: This is the **relative velocity** of $B$ as observed from $A$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Magnitude form:** $V_{B/A} = \omega \cdot \vert AB \vert$ and direction is obtained by rotating the vector $\vec{AB}$ by $90°$ in the direction of $\omega$.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Relative Acceleration Equation

Starting from the velocity result, differentiate once more:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{V}_B & = \vec{V}_A + \vec{V}_{B/A} \\
\text{Differentiate w.r.t. time:} \quad \dfrac{d\vec{V}_B}{dt} & = \dfrac{d\vec{V}_A}{dt} + \dfrac{d}{dt}(\vec{V}_{B/A}) \\
\vec{a}_B & = \vec{a}_A + \dfrac{d}{dt}\big(\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{B/A}\big) \\
\vec{a}_B & = \vec{a}_A + \dfrac{d\vec{\omega}}{dt} \times \vec{r}_{B/A} + \vec{\omega} \times \dfrac{d\vec{r}_{B/A}}{dt} \\
\vec{a}_B & = \vec{a}_A + \vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}_{B/A} + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{V}_{B/A} \\
\text{Substitute magnitudes:} \quad \alpha \cdot \vert AB \vert & = \text{ tangential component} \\
\omega^2 \cdot \vert AB \vert & = \text{ normal (centripetal) component} \\
\therefore \quad \vec{a}_B & = \vec{a}_A + \vec{a}_{B/A}^{\,t} + \vec{a}_{B/A}^{\,n} \\
\text{Final compact form:} \quad & \boxed{\vec{a}_B = \vec{a}_A + \vec{a}_{B/A}^{\,t} + \vec{a}_{B/A}^{\,n}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic of each line:**
- Line 1: Reuse the velocity equation.
- Line 2: Differentiate to upgrade velocity to acceleration.
- Line 3: Apply the product rule of differentiation to the cross product $\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{B/A}$.
- Line 4: Split into two terms — one with $\dot{\vec{\omega}} = \vec{\alpha}$ and one where $\vec{r}_{B/A}$ derivative becomes $\vec{V}_{B/A}$.
- Line 5: Recognize the two terms as the tangential and normal components.
- Line 6: Final substitution using $a^n = \omega^2 r$ (centripetal formula from circular motion).
- Line 7: Compact boxed form for use in problem-solving.

### 3.3 Derivation of Coriolis Acceleration

For a point $P$ sliding on a rotating link (with $\vec{\omega}$ constant or variable), the absolute acceleration includes an extra term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{V}_P & = \vec{V}_{P/\text{frame}} + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{P/O} \\
\text{where } \vec{V}_{P/\text{frame}} & = \dot{s}\,\hat{e}_t \quad \text{(sliding velocity along the link, } s = \text{ sliding distance)} \\
\text{Differentiate:} \quad \vec{a}_P & = \vec{a}_{P/\text{frame}} + \vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}_{P/O} + \vec{\omega} \times \big(\dot{s}\,\hat{e}_t\big) + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{V}_{P/\text{frame}} \\
\vec{a}_P & = \vec{a}_{P/\text{frame}} + \vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}_{P/O} + 2\,\vec{\omega} \times \vec{V}_{P/\text{frame}} \\
\text{Define Coriolis term:} \quad \vec{a}_c & = 2\,\vec{\omega} \times \vec{V}_{P/\text{frame}} \\
\text{Magnitude:} \quad & \boxed{a_c = 2\,\omega\,V_{P/\text{frame}}\sin\theta} \\
\text{For a slider on a link, } \theta = 90°: \quad & a_c = 2\omega V_{P/\text{frame}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic of each line:**
- Line 1: Absolute velocity is the sum of sliding velocity and rotational transport velocity.
- Line 2: The unit tangent $\hat{e}_t$ on a rotating link also rotates, so its derivative is $\vec{\omega} \times \hat{e}_t$.
- Line 3: Two extra terms emerge — one is the centripetal transport, the other doubles to give the Coriolis effect.
- Line 4: Define the Coriolis acceleration vector.
- Line 5: Cross product magnitude formula.
- Line 6: Specialization to the usual case where the slider velocity is perpendicular to the link.

### 3.4 Worked Example — Velocity & Acceleration of a Slider-Crank (Analytical Walkthrough)

**Problem statement (typical KTU-style):**
In a slider-crank mechanism, the crank $OA$ is $100$ mm long and rotates at $300$ rpm clockwise. The connecting rod $AB$ is $300$ mm long. Find the velocity and acceleration of the slider $B$ and the angular velocity and angular acceleration of the connecting rod when the crank has turned $60°$ from the inner dead centre (IDC).

**Given:**
- $r = OA = 100$ mm $= 0.1$ m
- $l = AB = 300$ mm $= 0.3$ m
- $N = 300$ rpm $\Rightarrow \omega = 2\pi \cdot 300 / 60 = 31.416$ rad/s
- Crank angle $\theta = 60°$ from IDC
- Direction: clockwise

**Step 1 — Slider position $x$ and connecting rod angle $\phi$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin\phi & = \dfrac{r \sin\theta}{l} = \dfrac{100 \times \sin 60°}{300} = \dfrac{100 \times 0.8660}{300} \\
\sin\phi & = 0.2887 \\
\phi & = 16.78° \\
\cos\phi & = \cos 16.78° = 0.9574
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Velocity of slider $B$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_B & = \omega \cdot r \left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right) \quad \text{where } n = l/r = 3 \\
V_B & = 31.416 \times 0.1 \times \left(\sin 60° + \dfrac{\sin 120°}{2 \times 3}\right) \\
V_B & = 3.1416 \times \left(0.8660 + \dfrac{0.8660}{6}\right) \\
V_B & = 3.1416 \times (0.8660 + 0.1443) \\
V_B & = 3.1416 \times 1.0103 \\
V_B & = 3.174 \;\text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic of each line:**
- Line 1: The classical approximate velocity equation for a slider-crank, valid for any $\theta$.
- Line 2: Substituting numerical values.
- Line 3: Numerical evaluation of the trigonometric terms.
- Line 4: Bracket simplification.
- Line 5: Final multiplication yields the slider velocity.

**Step 3 — Angular velocity of connecting rod $\omega_3$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_3 & = \dfrac{\omega \cos\theta}{n \cos\phi} = \dfrac{31.416 \times \cos 60°}{3 \times 0.9574} \\
\omega_3 & = \dfrac{31.416 \times 0.5}{2.872} = \dfrac{15.708}{2.872} \\
\omega_3 & = 5.470 \;\text{rad/s (clockwise)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Angular acceleration of connecting rod $\alpha_3$ (input assumed constant, so crank angular acceleration $\alpha = 0$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_3 & = -\dfrac{\omega^2 \cos\theta}{n \cos\phi} - \dfrac{\omega^2 \cos 2\theta}{n^2 \cos^2\phi} \\
\text{Term 1:} \quad & -\dfrac{(31.416)^2 \times \cos 60°}{3 \times 0.9574} = -\dfrac{987.0 \times 0.5}{2.872} = -171.9 \\
\text{Term 2:} \quad & -\dfrac{(31.416)^2 \times \cos 120°}{9 \times (0.9574)^2} = -\dfrac{987.0 \times (-0.5)}{8.250} = +59.82 \\
\alpha_3 & = -171.9 + 59.82 = -112.1 \;\text{rad/s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

The negative sign indicates that the connecting rod is decelerating in the clockwise sense (or accelerating in the counter-clockwise sense).

**Step 5 — Acceleration of slider $B$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_B & = r\omega^2 \left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right) \\
a_B & = 0.1 \times 987.0 \times \left(0.5 + \dfrac{(-0.5)}{3}\right) \\
a_B & = 98.70 \times (0.5 - 0.1667) \\
a_B & = 98.70 \times 0.3333 \\
a_B & = 32.9 \;\text{m/s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on acceleration direction:** The sign of $a_B$ is positive — meaning the slider is accelerating away from the crank centre along its guide at this instant (the piston is in its outward stroke acceleration phase for $\theta = 60°$ in a typical configuration).

### 3.5 Step-by-Step Graphical (Polygon) Procedure — Compact Reference

| Step | Action | Key Tools |
|------|--------|-----------|
| 1 | Draw configuration diagram to scale at the given crank angle | Set squares, protractor |
| 2 | Choose a convenient velocity scale $k_v$ (e.g., 1 mm = 0.05 m/s) | Engineering judgement |
| 3 | Draw velocity vector $\vec{oa}$ for the crank pin $A$ | $V_A = \omega_2 \cdot OA$ |
| 4 | From $a$, draw a line $\perp AB$ to find the line of action of $\vec{V}_{BA}$ | Set square, perpendicularity rule |
| 5 | From $o$, draw a line along the slider direction to give the line of action of $\vec{V}_B$ | Slider constraint |
| 6 | Intersection of lines from step 4 and 5 gives point $b$ | Triangle closure |
| 7 | Measure $V_B$ and $V_{BA}$ from the polygon | Use scale $k_v$ |
| 8 | Compute $\omega_3 = V_{BA} / \vert AB \vert$ and direction by inspection | Right-hand rule |
| 9 | For acceleration: draw pole $o'$, lay out $a^n$ first (known), then $a^t$ ($\perp$ link), then close | Vector polygon |
| 10 | Measure $a_B$ and $a_{BA}^t$ from polygon; compute $\alpha_3 = a_{BA}^t / \vert AB \vert$ | Same scale $k_a$ |
