# 📙 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the 2-D Rotation Matrix

We want a matrix $R(\theta)$ acting on a column vector $\vec{r} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}$ to produce a vector of the same length rotated by $\theta$ counter-clockwise.

**Step 1 — Component decomposition.**  
Let $\vec{r}$ make angle $\phi$ with the $x$-axis. Its components are:

$$x = r \cos\phi, \qquad y = r \sin\phi$$

**Step 2 — After rotation** (by $\theta$), the new angle becomes $\phi + \theta$. The new components are:

$$x' = r \cos(\phi + \theta) = r(\cos\phi\cos\theta - \sin\phi\sin\theta)$$
$$y' = r \sin(\phi + \theta) = r(\sin\phi\cos\theta + \cos\phi\sin\theta)$$

**Step 3 — Substitution using $x$ and $y$:**

$$x' = x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta$$
$$y' = x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta$$

**Step 4 — Write in matrix form:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\boxed{\,R(\theta) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\[4pt] \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}\,}$$

This is the canonical 2-D rotation matrix used throughout planar mechanism synthesis.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Pole of a Displacement (Two-Position Body Guidance)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pole (fixed-point center / instant center) of a displacement:**  
> The unique point in the plane that does *not* move when a rigid body undergoes a *general* planar displacement. For a *pure* translation, the pole is at infinity; for a *general* displacement (rotation + translation), the pole is a finite point.

### Setup

Let point $P$ in position 1 be at $\vec{P_1}$ and in position 2 be at $\vec{P_2}$. The general displacement is:

$$\vec{P_2} = R(\theta)\,\vec{P_1} + \vec{d}$$

We want a point $X$ such that $\vec{X_2} = \vec{X_1} = \vec{X}$. Substituting $X$ for $P$:

$$\vec{X} = R(\theta)\,\vec{X} + \vec{d}$$

Rearranging:

$$\bigl[I - R(\theta)\bigr]\,\vec{X} = \vec{d}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute $R(\theta)$ and expand:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 - \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & 1 - \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_x \\ X_y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} d_x \\ d_y \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Use the half-angle identities** $1 - \cos\theta = 2\sin^2(\theta/2)$ and $\sin\theta = 2\sin(\theta/2)\cos(\theta/2)$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 2\sin^2(\theta/2) & 2\sin(\theta/2)\cos(\theta/2) \\ -2\sin(\theta/2)\cos(\theta/2) & 2\sin^2(\theta/2) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_x \\ X_y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} d_x \\ d_y \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Divide through by $2\sin(\theta/2)$ (assuming $\theta \neq 0$):**

$$\begin{bmatrix} \sin(\theta/2) & \cos(\theta/2) \\ -\cos(\theta/2) & \sin(\theta/2) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_x \\ X_y \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{2\sin(\theta/2)} \begin{bmatrix} d_x \\ d_y \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Solve the 2×2 system.** Applying Cramer's rule with determinant $\sin^2(\theta/2) + \cos^2(\theta/2) = 1$:

$$X_x = \frac{1}{2\sin(\theta/2)}\Bigl[d_x \sin(\theta/2) - d_y \cos(\theta/2)\Bigr]$$

$$X_y = \frac{1}{2\sin(\theta/2)}\Bigl[d_x \cos(\theta/2) + d_y \sin(\theta/2)\Bigr]$$

**Step 5 — Final compact form (the pole coordinates):**

$$\boxed{\,\vec{X} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\sin(\theta/2)}\,\begin{bmatrix} d_x\sin(\theta/2) - d_y\cos(\theta/2) \\[4pt] d_x\cos(\theta/2) + d_y\sin(\theta/2) \end{bmatrix}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric interpretation:** The pole is the *intersection* of the perpendicular bisectors of the segments joining corresponding points of the body in positions 1 and 2. Equivalently, the pole is at distance $\lVert \vec{X} \rVert = \lVert \vec{d} \rVert / (2 \sin(\theta/2))$ from the displacement vector $\vec{d}$'s line of action.

---

## 3.3 Two-Position Synthesis for a 4-Bar Mechanism (Path Generation)

We wish to design a 4-bar linkage $A B C D$ such that a point $P$ on the coupler $BC$ passes through two prescribed points $P_1$ and $P_2$, *and* the coupler rotates by angle $\theta$ between these two positions.

### Step-by-step Solution (Analytical, Complex-Plane Method)

**Step 1 — Choose a reference frame.** Place the *fixed pivots* $A$ and $D$ to be determined, on a *common horizontal axis* (this is a degree of freedom we exploit). Let $A$ be at the origin and $D$ be at $(L, 0)$ where $L$ is the *ground link* length.

**Step 2 — Express coupler positions in complex form.**  
In position 1, the coupler point $P$ is at complex coordinate $z_{P_1}$.  
In position 2, the same point is at $z_{P_2}$, and the entire coupler has rotated by $\theta$.  
For an arbitrary point $Q$ on the coupler:

$$z_{Q_2} = e^{\,i\theta}\,z_{Q_1} + \Delta$$

where $\Delta$ is the translation vector of a chosen *reference point* of the body.

**Step 3 — Locate moving pivots $B$ and $C$.** Since $B$ lies on the coupler, we can write:

$$z_{B_2} = e^{\,i\theta}\,z_{B_1} + \Delta, \qquad z_{C_2} = e^{\,i\theta}\,z_{C_1} + \Delta$$

**Step 4 — Use the rigid link constraints** $\vert z_{B_k} - A \vert = \text{const} = r_2$ and $\vert z_{C_k} - D \vert = \text{const} = r_3$ for $k = 1, 2$. This yields **two circles** through which the moving pivots must pass.

**Step 5 — Intersection of circles.** The intersections of these circles give the *candidate* positions of $B$ and $C$. The mechanism is fully defined by the resulting $r_1, r_2, r_3, r_4$ link lengths.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two-position synthesis always has a *continuum* of solutions.** The "design space" is 2-D, parameterized by, e.g., the coupler length and the angular position of the tracer point $P$. This flexibility is exactly why 4-bar path-generation is so widely used in design.

---

## 3.4 Three-Position Synthesis for Rigid Body Guidance (Burmester Outline)

For a rigid body to occupy **three prescribed positions**, the four-bar mechanism that *carries* the body must have its moving pivots on the **circle-point circle (CPC)** and the corresponding fixed pivots on the **center-point circle (CenP)**.

**Step 1 — Compute the three displacements** $D_{12}, D_{23}, D_{13}$ between successive positions using complex-plane or matrix formulation.

**Step 2 — Compute the three poles** $P_{12}, P_{23}, P_{13}$ (the fixed-point centers) of these displacements using the formula derived in §3.2.

**Step 3 — Construct the perpendicular bisectors** of the segments joining pole $P_{12}$ to $P_{23}$ and $P_{23}$ to $P_{13}$. The **intersection of these bisectors is the center of the circle-point circle (CPC)**.

**Step 4 — The radius of the CPC** is the distance from the center to any of the three poles.

**Step 5 — Repeat** using the image poles (conjugate poles, obtained by reflecting each pole across the line joining the displacement) to construct the **center-point circle (CenP)**.

**Step 6 — Intersect CPC and CenP.** Each intersection point is a valid *moving pivot* $B$ or $C$. Choose the pairing that yields a physically realizable mechanism (link lengths positive, Grashof condition satisfied, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A 4-bar mechanism can in general satisfy *exactly* 3 rigid-body precision points. For *4* or *5* prescribed positions, one must either (a) use a 5-bar linkage, (b) accept an approximate solution via optimization, or (c) introduce redundant design parameters (e.g., the *dyadic* synthesis approach of Waldron & Kinzel).

---

## 3.5 Number of Precision Points a 4-Bar Can Match — Analytical Proof

A planar 4-bar has **3 link lengths** $r_1, r_2, r_3$ (taking $r_4$ as the ground link, *or* including $r_4$ gives 4 with 1 ground link fixed). With the ground link fixed, we have **3 design parameters** for a 4-bar.

| Task | Equations per precision point | Max precision points for 4-bar |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Function generation | 1 (a single relation $y = f(x)$) | **5** (Freudenstein equation) |
| Path generation | 2 (tracer-point $x$ and $y$ coordinates) | 9 in special cases, but generally limited by additional constraints |
| Body guidance | 3 (translation $d_x, d_y$, rotation $\theta$) | **3** (Burmester theory) |

For body guidance with 3 precision points, we have 3 × 3 = 9 equations and 4 free pivot-point coordinates (two moving × two positions) plus 1 rotation parameter = 5 unknowns. Wait — this *over-determines* the system. The resolution is that not all combinations of 4-bar solutions exist: the Burmester theory shows that the 9 equations are *not independent* and the pivot points are restricted to lie on the Burmester circles.

> [!NOTE]
> The mathematical reason is that the 9 closure equations share a *common* rotation matrix structure, reducing the independent constraints to the *Burmester circle intersections* — typically yielding 2, 4, or no real solutions.

# 📒 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams below are Mermaid block/flow diagrams representing the *conceptual architecture* of path generation and rigid body guidance. Physical drawings of mechanisms (4-bar sketches, tracer-point loci) are intentionally replaced by structured functional flow blocks per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE schematic policy.

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of Kinematic Synthesis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Desired Motion Specification] --> B{What is Prescribed?}
    B -- y equals f of x --> C[Function Generation]
    B -- A tracer point traces a curve --> D[Path Generation]
    B -- Full postures of a rigid body --> E[Rigid Body Guidance]
    C --> C1[Type Synthesis: Pick 4 bar or 6 bar]
    D --> D1[Type Synthesis: Pick 4 bar or 5 bar]
    E --> E1[Type Synthesis: Pick 4 bar or 5 bar]
    C1 --> C2[Number Synthesis: Verify DOF]
    D1 --> D2[Number Synthesis: Verify DOF]
    E1 --> E2[Number Synthesis: Verify DOF]
    C2 --> C3[Dimensional Synthesis: Freudenstein Equation]
    D2 --> D3[Dimensional Synthesis: Burmester Theory CPC and CenP]
    E2 --> E3[Dimensional Synthesis: Burmester Theory Circle Point and Center Point]
    C3 --> F[Mechanism Link Lengths]
    D3 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Validation: Animation, Manufacturability, Grashof Check]
```

## 4.2 Rigid Body Guidance Process — Block-Level Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputData[Prescribed Body Postures]
        P1[Position 1: Translation T1 Rotation Theta 1]
        P2[Position 2: Translation T2 Rotation Theta 2]
        P3[Position 3: Translation T3 Rotation Theta 3]
    end
    InputData --> ComputeDisp[Compute Displacement Operators D12 D23 D13]
    ComputeDisp --> ComputePoles[Find Poles P12 P23 P13 via Fixed Point Centers]
    ComputePoles --> Burmester[Construct Circle Point Circle CPC and Center Point Circle CenP]
    Burmester --> Intersect[Intersect CPC and CenP to get candidate pivots B and C]
    Intersect --> Select[Select physical solution: Positive links, Grashof, No interference]
    Select --> Output[4 Bar Link Lengths r1 r2 r3 r4]
```

## 4.3 Path Generation Process — Block-Level Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DesiredPath[Prescribed Coupler Curve e.g. Straight Line Teardrop Figure 8] --> ChooseMech{Mechanism Choice}
    ChooseMech -- 4 bar --> FourBar[4 Bar Coupler Curve Atlas Search]
    ChooseMech -- Slider crank --> SliderCrank[Slider Crank Tracer Point Analysis]
    ChooseMech -- 5 bar --> FiveBar[5 Bar Optimization Loop]
    FourBar --> BurmesterPath[Burmester Point Search: Match 2 to 3 Precision Points]
    SliderCrank --> BurmesterPath
    FiveBar --> OptStep[Numerical Optimization minimize error between desired and traced path]
    BurmesterPath --> Valid4[Validate: Animation, Check Grashof, Reach singularities]
    OptStep --> Valid5[Validate: Animation, Check constraints]
    Valid4 --> FinalOut[Final Link Lengths and Tracer Point Coordinates]
    Valid5 --> FinalOut
```

## 4.4 Burmester Circle Architecture — Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MovingFrame[Moving Plane of the Coupler]
        CPC[Circle Point Circle CPC: Locus of Moving Pivots B and C]
        IM[Image Poles IM12 IM23 IM13]
    end
    subgraph FixedFrame[Fixed Plane of the Ground Link]
        CENP[Center Point Circle CenP: Locus of Fixed Pivots A and D]
        RP[Real Poles P12 P23 P13]
    end
    MovingFrame --> Transform[Apply Inverse Displacement Operators D21 D32]
    Transform --> FixedFrame
    CPC --> IntersectB[Pick moving pivot B on CPC]
    CPC --> IntersectC[Pick moving pivot C on CPC]
    CENP --> IntersectB
    CENP --> IntersectC
    IntersectB --> Mech[Construct 4 bar mechanism]
    IntersectC --> Mech
```

## 4.5 Comparison of the Three Synthesis Tasks — Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FG[Function Generation]
        FG1[Input: Pairs of link angles]
        FG2[Tool: Freudenstein Equation]
        FG3[4 bar: Up to 5 precision points]
    end
    subgraph PG[Path Generation]
        PG1[Input: Sequence of tracer point coordinates]
        PG2[Tool: Coupler Curve Atlas and Burmester]
        PG3[4 bar: Up to 3 to 9 precision points]
    end
    subgraph BG[Rigid Body Guidance]
        BG1[Input: Poses of entire rigid body]
        BG2[Tool: Burmester CPC and CenP]
        BG3[4 bar: Up to 3 precision points]
    end
    FG --> ENG1[Engineering Output: Function Generator for Analog Computing]
    PG --> ENG2[Engineering Output: Walking Machine Coupler Shoe Cleaner]
    BG --> ENG3[Engineering Output: Windshield Wiper Dumper Bed Flap]
```

# 📕 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
> Differentiate clearly between **path generation** and **rigid body guidance** in mechanism synthesis. Give one practical example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Path Generation | Rigid Body Guidance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Specification** | A tracer point on the moving link must describe a *continuous prescribed curve* | The *entire rigid body* must occupy prescribed *finite positions* |
| **Orientation** | Not specified (coupler may take any orientation along the path) | Fully specified at each prescribed position |
| **Typical Example** | A walking-toy leg tracer point tracing a flat-footed path | A windshield wiper blade passing through a sequence of tilted postures |
| **Common engineering device** | Carpet cleaning mechanism, quick-return mechanism | Windshield wiper, dumper bed, suspension geometry |

*(1 mark each for the two definitions, 1 mark for examples — awarded as per KTU valuation key.)*

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
> What are the **three types of kinematic synthesis**? State the **maximum number of precision points** that a 4-bar mechanism can achieve for each type.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Type Synthesis** – Selection of mechanism type (4-bar, slider-crank, 5-bar…).  
2. **Number Synthesis** – Selection of link and joint counts (with DOF verification).  
3. **Dimensional Synthesis** – Determination of actual link proportions.

Maximum precision points for a 4-bar:

- **Function Generation:** Up to **5** precision points (via the Freudenstein equation).  
- **Path Generation:** Up to **9** in special symmetric cases, generally **3** in classical synthesis.  
- **Rigid Body Guidance:** Up to **3** precision points (Burmester theory).

*(1 mark for naming the three types, 1 mark for two of the maxima, 1 mark for the remaining maximum — partial credit allowed.)*

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### 🔹 Solution to Question B-A

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Displacement 1→2.** Translation vector is the difference in reference-point coordinates:  

$$\vec{d}_{12} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Rotation matrix (with $\theta_{12} = 30^{\circ}$, so $\cos 30^{\circ} = \sqrt{3}/2$ and $\sin 30^{\circ} = 1/2$):

$$R(30^{\circ}) = \begin{bmatrix} \sqrt{3}/2 & -1/2 \\ 1/2 & \sqrt{3}/2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Displacement 2→3.** Translation vector:

$$\vec{d}_{23} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Rotation matrix (with $\theta_{23} = 20^{\circ}$):

$$R(20^{\circ}) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos 20^{\circ} & -\sin 20^{\circ} \\ \sin 20^{\circ} & \cos 20^{\circ} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9397 & -0.3420 \\ 0.3420 & 0.9397 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute pole $P_{12}$.** Using the half-angle formula from §3.2 with $\theta = 30^{\circ}$, $\sin(\theta/2) = \sin 15^{\circ} \approx 0.2588$, $\cos(\theta/2) = \cos 15^{\circ} \approx 0.9659$:

$$X_x = \frac{1}{2(0.2588)}\bigl[2(0.2588) - 1(0.9659)\bigr] = \frac{0.5517 - 0.9659}{0.5176} \approx -0.800$$

$$X_y = \frac{1}{2(0.2588)}\bigl[2(0.9659) + 1(0.2588)\bigr] = \frac{1.9318 + 0.2588}{0.5176} \approx 4.231$$

So $P_{12} \approx (-0.800,\; 4.231)$.

**Step 4 — Compute pole $P_{23}$.** With $\theta = 20^{\circ}$, $\sin 10^{\circ} \approx 0.1736$, $\cos 10^{\circ} \approx 0.9848$:

$$X_x = \frac{1}{2(0.1736)}\bigl[2(0.1736) - (-1)(0.9848)\bigr] = \frac{0.3472 + 0.9848}{0.3472} \approx 3.837$$

$$X_y = \frac{1}{2(0.1736)}\bigl[2(0.9848) + (-1)(0.1736)\bigr] = \frac{1.9696 - 0.1736}{0.3472} \approx 5.175$$

So $P_{23} \approx (3.837,\; 5.175)$.

**Step 5 — Compute pole $P_{13}$** by composition of displacements:

$$R_{13} = R(20^{\circ}) \cdot R(30^{\circ}) = R(50^{\circ})$$

Total translation $\vec{d}_{13}$:

$$\vec{d}_{13} = R(20^{\circ}) \vec{d}_{12} + \vec{d}_{23} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9397 & -0.3420 \\ 0.3420 & 0.9397 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \begin{bmatrix} 1.8794 - 0.3420 \\ 0.6840 + 0.9397 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5374 \\ 1.6237 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3.5374 \\ 0.6237 \end{bmatrix}$$

Using half-angle ($\theta = 50^{\circ}$, $\sin 25^{\circ} \approx 0.4226$, $\cos 25^{\circ} \approx 0.9063$):

$$X_x = \frac{1}{2(0.4226)}\bigl[3.5374(0.4226) - 0.6237(0.9063)\bigr] \approx \frac{1.4949 - 0.5653}{0.8452} \approx 1.099$$

$$X_y = \frac{1}{2(0.4226)}\bigl[3.5374(0.9063) + 0.6237(0.4226)\bigr] \approx \frac{3.2061 + 0.2636}{0.8452} \approx 4.103$$

So $P_{13} \approx (1.099,\; 4.103)$.

*(5 marks for the matrices, 1 mark for each pole, total 7 marks.)*

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Construct perpendicular bisectors of $P_{12}P_{23}$ and $P_{23}P_{13}$.** The intersections of these bisectors give the **center of the CPC**.

- Bisector of $P_{12}P_{23}$: midpoint $M_{12,23} \approx (1.519,\; 4.703)$, slope of segment $\approx (5.175 - 4.231)/(3.837 + 0.800) = 0.944/4.637 \approx 0.2036$, so the bisector slope is $-1/0.2036 \approx -4.91$.  
- Bisector of $P_{23}P_{13}$: midpoint $M_{23,13} \approx (2.468,\; 4.639)$, slope of segment $\approx (5.175 - 4.103)/(3.837 - 1.099) = 1.072/2.738 \approx 0.3915$, so the bisector slope is $-1/0.3915 \approx -2.55$.

**Step 2 — Intersect the two bisectors** to get the CPC center. Solving the two linear equations:

$$y - 4.703 = -4.91(x - 1.519) \quad \text{and} \quad y - 4.639 = -2.55(x - 2.468)$$

gives the CPC center at approximately $(C_{CPC},\, y_{CPC}) \approx (-1.20,\; 11.36)$ (numerical answer; the *method* is what earns marks).

**Step 3 — Radius of CPC** is the distance from $C_{CPC}$ to any pole:  

$$r_{CPC} = \sqrt{(-0.800 - (-1.20))^2 + (4.231 - 11.36)^2} \approx \sqrt{0.16 + 50.83} \approx 7.14$$

**Step 4 — Repeat using image poles** (reflect each pole across the line connecting the displacement pivot) to construct the **CenP**.

**Step 5 — Intersect CPC and CenP.** Each intersection point is a valid *moving pivot* $B$ or $C$. Pick the pairing that gives a *physically realizable* 4-bar (positive link lengths, no interference).

**Step 6 — Final step.** Once $B$ and $C$ are selected on the coupler in position 1, the fixed pivots $A$ and $D$ are the corresponding CenP intersection points. The four link lengths are:

$$r_1 = \vert A - D \vert, \quad r_2 = \vert A - B \vert, \quad r_3 = \vert B - C \vert, \quad r_4 = \vert C - D \vert$$

*(2 marks: procedure to construct CPC. 2 marks: procedure for CenP. 2 marks: pivot selection. 1 mark: link-length formula.)*

---

### 🔹 Solution to Question B-B

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Complex-plane displacement operator.** With $z_{P_1} = 5 + 0i$ and $z_{P_2} = 3 + 4i$, the displacement operator mapping $P_1$ to $P_2$ must satisfy:

$$z_{P_2} = e^{\,i\theta}\,z_{P_1} + w, \quad \text{where} \quad \theta = 45^{\circ}, \;\; e^{\,i 45^{\circ}} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}(1 + i)$$

Compute $e^{\,i 45^{\circ}}\,z_{P_1}$:

$$e^{\,i 45^{\circ}}(5) = \frac{5\sqrt{2}}{2} + i\,\frac{5\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 3.536 + 3.536\,i$$

Therefore:

$$w = z_{P_2} - e^{\,i 45^{\circ}}\,z_{P_1} = (3 - 3.536) + i(4 - 3.536) = -0.536 + 0.464\,i$$

**Step 2 — Displacement operator:** $D(\theta, w) : z \mapsto e^{\,i 45^{\circ}}\,z + (-0.536 + 0.464\,i)$.

**Step 3 — Fixed pivot $A$ at origin;** choose $D$ at $(L, 0)$ on the $x$-axis with $L$ as a free parameter (the *ground link*).

**Step 4 — Circle equations for moving pivot $B$.** The point $B$ is a *rigid* attachment of the coupler, so $B_1$ and $B_2$ are related by the same displacement. The *fixed* pivot $A$ satisfies $\vert B_1 - A \vert = \vert B_2 - A \vert = r_2$. Substituting $B_2 = e^{\,i 45^{\circ}}\,B_1 + w$ and $A = 0$:

$$\vert B_1 \vert = \vert e^{\,i 45^{\circ}}\,B_1 + w \vert = r_2$$

Squaring and expanding:

$$\vert B_1 \vert^2 = \vert e^{\,i 45^{\circ}}\,B_1 + w \vert^2 = \vert B_1 \vert^2 + 2\,\text{Re}\bigl(\overline{B_1}\,e^{\,i 45^{\circ}}\,w\bigr) + \vert w \vert^2$$

This simplifies to the **circle equation** (a line in the special case of pure rotation, or a true circle for general displacement):

$$2\,\text{Re}\bigl(\overline{B_1}\,e^{\,i 45^{\circ}}\,w\bigr) + \vert w \vert^2 = 0$$

This is a *line* (perpendicular bisector property) — geometrically the locus of $B$ equidistant from $A$ in both positions. A similar equation holds for $C$ around pivot $D$. The two **intersections** of these loci give the candidate positions of $B$ and $C$.

*(3 marks: complex displacement operator. 2 marks: the two circle/line equations. 2 marks: interpretation of intersection.)*

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Determine the moving pivots $B$ and $C$ in position 1.** For a 4-bar with $r_1 = 6$ (ground, $A$ at origin, $D$ at $(6, 0)$), and the tracer point $P$ at polar coordinates $(\rho = 2.5, \mu = 60^{\circ})$ from $B$ on the coupler.

The location of $B$ on the coupler relative to a *local* coordinate frame: choose $B$ as the local origin, so $P_1^{(\text{local})} = 2.5(\cos 60^{\circ}, \sin 60^{\circ}) = (1.25, 2.165)$. Similarly, $C$ is located on the coupler at some polar coordinate $(\rho_{BC}, \mu_{BC})$ — to be determined.

**Step 2 — Two-position constraints.** Apply the displacement operator derived in part (a) to $B$ and $C$. The Burmester pivot points for *fixed pivots* $A$ and $D$ are:

$$A = \frac{B_1 + B_2}{2} + \frac{B_2 - B_1}{2i}\cot(\theta/2) \quad \text{(one form)}$$

For position 1, $B_1 = (B_{1x}, B_{1y})$; for position 2, $B_2 = R(45^{\circ})B_1 + w$.

**Step 3 — Numerical solution** (assumes initial guess $B_1$):

Take $B_1 = (1, 0)$ on the coupler (local coupler frame). Then:

$$B_2 = R(45^{\circ})(1, 0) + (-0.536, 0.464) = (0.707 - 0.536,\; 0.707 + 0.464) = (0.171, 1.171)$$

Fixed pivot $A$ (which must lie on the perpendicular bisector of $B_1B_2$ at distance satisfying the geometric constraints):

$$A = \frac{(1, 0) + (0.171, 1.171)}{2} + \frac{(0.171 - 1, 1.171 - 0)}{2i}\cot(22.5^{\circ})$$

$$A = (0.586, 0.586) + \frac{(-0.829, 1.171)}{2i} \cdot 2.414$$

Using $\dfrac{1}{i} = -i$:

$$A = (0.586, 0.586) + 2.414 \cdot (-i)(0.829 - 1.171\,i) = (0.586, 0.586) + 2.414\,(-1.171 + 0.829\,i)$$

Wait — the complex arithmetic requires careful bookkeeping. In real 2-D form:

$$A_x = \frac{B_{1x} + B_{2x}}{2} + \frac{B_{2y} - B_{1y}}{2}\cot(\theta/2)$$

$$A_y = \frac{B_{1y} + B_{2y}}{2} - \frac{B_{2x} - B_{1x}}{2}\cot(\theta/2)$$

Substituting with $\cot(22.5^{\circ}) \approx 2.414$:

$$A_x = \frac{1 + 0.171}{2} + \frac{1.171 - 0}{2} \cdot 2.414 = 0.586 + 1.413 = 1.999 \approx 2.0$$

$$A_y = \frac{0 + 1.171}{2} - \frac{0.171 - 1}{2} \cdot 2.414 = 0.586 + 1.000 \approx 1.586$$

So **$A \approx (2.0, 1.6)$**. By symmetry of the choice, $D$ is determined from the analogous computation on $C$.

**Step 4 — Final link lengths.** The candidate values of $A$ and $D$ are *not* on the $x$-axis in this case — the mechanism is non-grounded to the $x$-axis. The KTU-acceptable final answer is the *coordinates* of $A$ and $D$ and the *computed* $r_1, r_2, r_3, r_4$.

*(2 marks: applying rotation matrix. 2 marks: half-angle pole formula. 2 marks: numerical evaluation. 1 mark: link-length computation.)*

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in KTU exams on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the three synthesis tasks** (function / path / body). Many students mix up *path generation* with *body guidance* — remember: path generation specifies a *curve*, body guidance specifies *poses*.
> 2. **Sign errors in the rotation matrix** — $\sin\theta$ is **negative in the upper-right** entry. Writing it as $+\sin\theta$ loses 1 mark per occurrence.
> 3. **Forgetting the half-angle identity** in the pole formula — students who compute $P_{12}$ using the full angle $\theta$ instead of $\theta/2$ get the wrong pole coordinates.
> 4. **Confusing *image poles* and *real poles*** in Burmester theory — image poles are the *reflections* of real poles across the line of displacement and are used to construct the *Center-Point Circle*, not the *Circle-Point Circle*.
> 5. **Not validating the Grashof condition** — a synthesized 4-bar that violates Grashof's inequality is *not* a continuously rotatable mechanism, even if mathematically valid. KTU examiners check this.
> 6. **Mixing up the coupler angle conventions** — $\theta$ measured counter-clockwise positive (CCW) is the KTU 2024 Scheme convention. CW angles must be entered as *negative*.
> 7. **Insufficient precision in numerical answers** — KTU expects 4 significant figures minimum. Intermediate rounding loses method marks.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Kinematic synthesis** is the *design* of mechanisms from desired motion. It has three stages: **type**, **number**, **dimensional**.
- ✅ **Dimensional synthesis** has three canonical tasks: **function generation**, **path generation**, **rigid body guidance**.
- ✅ **Path generation** specifies a *curve* traced by a single *tracer point*; orientation of the link is **not** specified.
- ✅ **Rigid body guidance** specifies *complete postures* (translation + rotation) of a rigid body; typically the *coupler* is the rigid body.
- ✅ The **2-D rotation matrix** is $R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$, with $\theta$ CCW-positive.
- ✅ The **complex-plane displacement** is $z_{P_2} = e^{i\theta}\,z_{P_1} + w$.
- ✅ The **pole** of a displacement is the unique point with zero velocity under that displacement — given by the half-angle formula.
- ✅ **Burmester Theory** is the classical tool for 3-precision-point *rigid body guidance*: it constructs the **Circle-Point Circle (CPC)** and the **Center-Point Circle (CenP)**; their intersections yield 4-bar solutions.
- ✅ A 4-bar mechanism can match up to **3 precision points** for rigid body guidance, **5 for function generation** (Freudenstein), and **9 in special cases for path generation**.
- ✅ **Freudenstein's equation** is the workhorse for function generation — always state it in its **standard form** with $A, B, C, D$ link lengths and the angles $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$ before plugging numbers.
- ✅ **Grashof's condition** ($s + l \leq p + q$) must be checked *after* synthesis to ensure the mechanism moves as intended.
- ✅ For more than 3 precision points of body guidance, you need a **5-bar** mechanism or **optimization-based approximate synthesis**.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Scheme** expects answers in three clear sections: *Diagram, Formula, Numerical* — mark allocation is roughly 2 (diagram) + 3 (formula/derivation) + 2 (numerical) for 7-mark sub-questions.
- ✅ **Practice drawing Burmester circles** — they are the *single most tested* topic in Module 2 ESE. Be fluent with pole computation and the perpendicular-bisector construction.
- ✅ **Real-world design case studies** to remember: windshield wipers (body guidance), walking toys (path generation), analog-computer function generators (function generation).

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Wisdom:** A clean diagram of the *prescribed positions* + the *poles* + the *Burmester circles* + the *final 4-bar sketch* will fetch full marks. The KTU examiner does not penalize for "extra" clarity — only for missing steps. Always draw the figure first, then derive, then compute. This is the universal KTU 2024 Scheme problem-solving pattern.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dead Center Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT412 - KINEMATICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dead Center Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

In the **inversion of the single slider-crank mechanism**, the position of the slider at which the line of stroke passes through the crank centre is known as the **Dead Centre (DC)**. At this position, the **effective crank arm becomes zero**, and hence the slider cannot drive the crank, nor can the crank drive the slider. The mechanism effectively "loses" its kinematic transmissibility at this extreme angular position.

The slider-crank mechanism possesses **two dead centres**:

1. **Inner Dead Centre (IDC):** Occurs when the slider is at its position of minimum distance from the crank centre. Here, the crank and connecting rod overlap (are collinear and folded).
2. **Outer Dead Centre (ODC):** Occurs when the slider is at its position of maximum distance from the crank centre. Here, the crank and connecting rod are collinear and extended (stretched).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** "Dead centre problems arise only when the mechanism is used with a single cylinder. The conditions for the crank to revolve without being turned by the slider are crucial in designing IC engines and reciprocating compressors."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a see-saw board (acting as a connecting rod) attached to a rotating wheel (the crank) with your hand (the slider). When the wheel rotates so that the rod lies exactly along the radius pointing away from your hand, no matter how hard you push or pull along the line of stroke, the wheel refuses to budge — the push is purely along the axis of the rod and produces **zero torque** on the crank pin. This "locked" position is a **Dead Centre**.

A more relatable example is the **gates of a fortress**: at exactly midday, the shadow of the pole (analogous to the connecting rod) points directly along the ground line. The sun's rays (the slider force) strike perpendicular to nothing — they cannot rotate the pole any further. The pole is at its "dead point."

### 1.3 Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics

- **Crank Radius ($r$):** Distance from the crank centre $O$ to the crank pin $A$ — typically **50 mm to 300 mm** in IC engines.
- **Connecting Rod Length ($l$):** Distance from the crank pin $A$ to the slider pin (wrist pin) $B$ — always **$l > r$** (typically $l/r \approx 3.3$ to $4$).
- **Ratio $n = r/l$:** A dimensionless number, usually between **0.20 and 0.33** for IC engines.
- **Inertia Force ($m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$):** The dominant force at high RPM — this is the force that can cause the crank to be flung past the dead centre.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Callout — Effective Crank Arm**
> The perpendicular distance from the crank centre to the line of action of the thrust on the connecting rod is called the **effective crank arm** ($r_{\text{eff}}$). At dead centres, $r_{\text{eff}} = 0$, which is the root cause of the entire problem.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — The "Why" Behind Dead Centres

The slider-crank mechanism transmits force along the connecting rod. For the crank to rotate, this force must possess a **moment arm** about the crank centre $O$. The moment arm equals $r \cdot \sin(\theta \pm \phi)$, where $\theta$ is the crank angle and $\phi$ is the obliquity of the connecting rod.

**The "Why":**
- When the crank is at IDC ($\theta = 0°$): the connecting rod is folded over the crank. The force is directed along the crank itself. $\sin\theta = 0$ and $\sin\phi = 0$ → **zero moment arm**.
- When the crank is at ODC ($\theta = 180°$): the connecting rod is collinear with the crank but on the opposite side. The same condition arises → **zero moment arm**.

**The "How":**
The slider is reciprocating, and its velocity becomes **zero** at the two extreme ends of the stroke. By Newton's second law, the force required to instantaneously accelerate the slider from rest is non-zero. However, this force cannot generate a torque on the crank to push it past the dead centre — leading to **stalling**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

- **Step 1 — Position Analysis:** Define the crank angle $\theta$ measured from the line of stroke. Identify the limiting positions $\theta = 0°$ (IDC) and $\theta = 180°$ (ODC).
- **Step 2 — Velocity Analysis:** The slider velocity $v = r\omega\left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{n\sin 2\theta}{2}\right)$ is zero at $\theta = 0°$ and $\theta = 180°$.
- **Step 3 — Force Transmission:** The thrust $P$ in the connecting rod acts along its length. Its moment about $O$ is $P \cdot r \cdot \sin(\theta \pm \phi)$.
- **Step 4 — Crisis Point:** At $\theta = 0°$ or $180°$, $\sin(\theta \pm \phi) = 0$, so **moment = 0**.
- **Step 5 — Consequence:** Without an external torque (inertia of flywheel, or another piston), the crank will jam at the dead centre.

### 2.3 The Crank Effort Equation — Derivation Setup

Consider a horizontal slider-crank with the slider on the right of the crank centre. The thrust $P$ on the slider (from the piston, say) is transmitted along the connecting rod. Resolving $P$ at the crank pin:

- Tangential component (perpendicular to crank): $P_T = P \cdot \sin(\theta + \phi)$ → for the outstroke
- Radial component: $P_R = P \cdot \cos(\theta + \phi)$ → no torque contribution

The **turning moment** (crank effort) on the crank is:
$$T = P \cdot r \cdot \sin(\theta + \phi)$$

Using the angle-relation $\sin\phi = n\sin\theta$ and expanding using compound-angle identities yields the famous **Rankine's formula**:

$$T = P \cdot r \left[\sin\theta + \dfrac{n \sin 2\theta}{2}\right] \quad \text{(for outstroke)}$$

For the instroke (slider pulling the rod), the sign changes:
$$T = P \cdot r \left[\sin\theta - \dfrac{n \sin 2\theta}{2}\right] \quad \text{(for instroke)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the $+$ sign in outstroke and $-$ in instroke?**
> During outstroke, the obliquity $\phi$ **adds** to the moment arm because the rod tilts *towards* the line of stroke. During instroke, the rod tilts *away* from the line of stroke, **reducing** the moment arm.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Symbol** | **Boundary / Range** | **Units** |
|--------------|-------------|------------|----------------------|-----------|
| Crank Angle at IDC | $\theta = 0°$ | $\theta$ | Fixed | radians / degrees |
| Crank Angle at ODC | $\theta = 180°$ | $\theta$ | Fixed | radians / degrees |
| Ratio of lengths | $n = r / l$ | $n$ | $0 < n < 1$ | dimensionless |
| Slider displacement | $x = r(1 - \cos\theta) + \dfrac{r^2}{2l}(1 - \cos 2\theta)$ | $x$ | $0 \leq x \leq 2r(1 + n)$ | metres (m) |
| Slider velocity | $v = r\omega\left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{n \sin 2\theta}{2}\right)$ | $v$ | peaks mid-stroke | m/s |
| Slider acceleration | $a = r\omega^2\left(\cos\theta + n\cos 2\theta\right)$ | $a$ | max near DC | m/s² |
| Crank effort (outstroke) | $T = P\cdot r\left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{n \sin 2\theta}{2}\right)$ | $T$ | $T = 0$ at DC | N·m |
| Crank effort (instroke) | $T = P\cdot r\left(\sin\theta - \dfrac{n \sin 2\theta}{2}\right)$ | $T$ | $T = 0$ at DC | N·m |
| Angularity of rod | $\sin\phi = n\sin\theta$ | $\phi$ | $\phi_{\max} = \sin^{-1}(n)$ | radians |
| Effective crank arm | $r_{\text{eff}} = r\sin(\theta \pm \phi)$ | $r_{\text{eff}}$ | $= 0$ at DC | m |
| Minimum force to turn crank at DC | $P_{\min} = \dfrac{I \cdot \omega^2 \cdot n}{1}$ (approx) | $P_{\min}$ | depends on $I, \omega$ | Newtons (N) |
| Inertia torque needed | $T_{\text{iner}} = I \cdot \alpha$ | $T$ | instantaneous at DC | N·m |
| Work done per cycle (2 strokes) | $W = P \cdot 4r$ (mean effective) | $W$ | ideal | Joules (J) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the backbone of **IC engine balancing, reciprocating pump design, compressor design, and crank-effort diagrams**. Without accounting for dead centres, no internal combustion engine could self-start or run smoothly.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Single-Cylinder Petrol Engine (Motorcycle):** Dead centres are crossed thanks to the **heavy flywheel**, which stores rotational kinetic energy $E_k = \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$ and releases it to push the crank past the DC.
- **Multi-Cylinder Engines (Cars, Aircraft):** Multiple pistons are arranged so that when one is at DC, another is mid-stroke and can drive the crank — **inherent balancing**.
- **Steam Engines / Reciprocating Pumps:** Historically solved by using **two cranks at 90°** (e.g., beam engines) or **gearing arrangements** (Grasshopper, Watt, Corliss).
- **Hand-Cranked Starters (Old Cars, Scooters):** The "kick" is timed to provide angular momentum precisely before the crank reaches DC.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Crank Effort at Dead Centres (Rigorous Proof)

**Given:** Slider-crank mechanism with crank $OA = r$, connecting rod $AB = l$, slider at $B$, thrust on slider $= P$ acting along the line of stroke.

**Step 1 — Geometry Setup:**

Place the crank centre at $O$, the slider moves along a line passing through $O$. Let $\theta$ be the angle the crank makes with the line of stroke (measured from IDC). Let $\phi$ be the obliquity of the connecting rod with the line of stroke.

**Step 2 — Angle Relationship (Menelaus / Sine Rule):**

Drop a perpendicular from $A$ to the line of stroke, meeting it at $M$. In triangle $OAB$, applying the sine rule:
$$\dfrac{\sin\phi}{r} = \dfrac{\sin(180° - \theta)}{l} = \dfrac{\sin\theta}{l}$$

Therefore:
$$\sin\phi = \dfrac{r}{l} \cdot \sin\theta = n \sin\theta$$

This is the **fundamental angularity relation** of the slider-crank mechanism.

**Step 3 — Force Resolution at the Crank Pin:**

The thrust $P$ on the slider is transmitted along the rod $AB$ as a compressive force (during outstroke). At the crank pin $A$, this force can be resolved into:
- A component **along the crank** $OA$: $P \cos(\theta + \phi)$ — produces **no torque** about $O$.
- A component **perpendicular to the crank** $OA$: $P \sin(\theta + \phi)$ — produces the **torque**.

**Step 4 — Turning Moment (Crank Effort):**

The torque about the crank centre $O$ is:
$$T = P \sin(\theta + \phi) \cdot r = P \cdot r \cdot \sin(\theta + \phi)$$

**Step 5 — Expansion Using Compound Angle Identity:**

$$\sin(\theta + \phi) = \sin\theta \cos\phi + \cos\theta \sin\phi$$

Substitute $\sin\phi = n\sin\theta$ and $\cos\phi = \sqrt{1 - n^2\sin^2\theta}$:

$$T = P \cdot r \left[\sin\theta \cdot \sqrt{1 - n^2\sin^2\theta} + \cos\theta \cdot n \sin\theta\right]$$

**Step 6 — Binomial Expansion (since $n < 1$):**

For small $n$ (typically $n \leq 1/3$), use the binomial expansion $\sqrt{1 - x} \approx 1 - \dfrac{x}{2}$ with $x = n^2 \sin^2\theta$:

$$\sqrt{1 - n^2 \sin^2\theta} \approx 1 - \dfrac{n^2 \sin^2\theta}{2}$$

Substitute:
$$T = P \cdot r \left[\sin\theta\left(1 - \dfrac{n^2\sin^2\theta}{2}\right) + n \sin\theta \cos\theta\right]$$

$$T = P \cdot r \left[\sin\theta - \dfrac{n^2 \sin^3\theta}{2} + \dfrac{n \sin 2\theta}{2}\right]$$

**Step 7 — Drop the Cubic Term (Standard Approximation):**

Since $n^2 \sin^3\theta$ is very small (of order $n^2 \cdot 1 \approx 0.05$ to $0.10$), and the $n \sin 2\theta$ term dominates, we approximate:

$$\boxed{T_{\text{outstroke}} = P \cdot r \left[\sin\theta + \dfrac{n \sin 2\theta}{2}\right]}$$

For the **instroke**, the slider pulls the rod, and the geometry reverses, giving:

$$\boxed{T_{\text{instroke}} = P \cdot r \left[\sin\theta - \dfrac{n \sin 2\theta}{2}\right]}$$

**Step 8 — Verification at Dead Centres:**

At IDC ($\theta = 0°$): $\sin 0° = 0$, $\sin 0° = 0$ → $T = 0$ ✓
At ODC ($\theta = 180°$): $\sin 180° = 0$, $\sin 360° = 0$ → $T = 0$ ✓

This proves the dead centre problem analytically.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Condition for Crank to Turn at Dead Centre

**Problem Statement:** A single-cylinder engine is at the inner dead centre. The piston is exerting a thrust $P$ on the slider. Determine the **minimum value of $P$** (or equivalently, the **minimum inertia torque**) required to push the crank past the IDC.

**Step 1 — Forces on the Crank at IDC:**

At IDC, $\theta = 0°$, so the connecting rod is collinear with the crank (folded). The thrust $P$ acts purely along the crank axis.

**Step 2 — Net Torque Required:**

For the crank to start rotating, the net torque about $O$ must be positive (i.e., in the direction of intended rotation). At the exact IDC, this net torque is zero. To get past it, we need an **inertia torque** from the rotating masses.

**Step 3 — Inertia of the Crank:**

Let $I$ = mass moment of inertia of the crank + flywheel system about $O$. The angular acceleration is $\alpha$. The inertia torque opposing motion (or aiding, depending on direction) is:
$$T_{\text{iner}} = I \cdot \alpha$$

**Step 4 — Minimum Thrust Condition:**

For the crank to just be on the verge of turning:
$$P_{\min} \cdot r = T_{\text{iner, min}}$$

The exact expression, derived from the second derivative of the crank effort, gives:
$$P_{\min} = I \cdot \omega^2 \cdot \dfrac{(1 + n)}{1} \cdot \text{(correction factor)}$$

**Simplified Standard KTU Form:**

The minimum force required to start the crank turning at the dead centre, neglecting friction, is given by:

$$P_{\min} = \dfrac{I \cdot \omega^2}{r} \cdot (1 + n)$$

where $I$ is the moment of inertia of the flywheel-crank assembly, $\omega$ is the angular velocity at the dead centre, $r$ is the crank radius, and $n = r/l$.

**Step 5 — Physical Interpretation:**

A heavier flywheel (larger $I$) makes the engine easier to start past the DC. A longer connecting rod (smaller $n$) also helps. This is why **bigger engines have bigger flywheels and longer rods**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Solutions to Dead Centre Problems

| **Solution Method** | **Operating Principle** | **Application** | **Advantage** | **Limitation** |
|---------------------|--------------------------|------------------|---------------|----------------|
| **Flywheel** | Stores kinetic energy $E_k = \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$ to cross DC | Single-cylinder IC engines, steam engines | Smooth, simple | Adds weight; cannot start from rest |
| **Two-Crank Arrangement (90° apart)** | When one piston is at DC, the other is mid-stroke | Beam engines, two-cylinder pumps | Self-starting | Complex mechanism |
| **Four-Crank Arrangement (Whitworth, etc.)** | Multiple offsets ensure no simultaneous DC | Large steam engines, compressors | High torque, smooth | Bulky |
| **Compressed Air / Spring Starter** | External torque to push past DC | Aircraft engines, large diesels | Reliable start | Requires auxiliary system |
| **Mechanical Linkage (Slider on slotted lever)** | Adds a second constraint — **double slider-crank** | Grasshopper beam engine, return-connecting rod engines | Self-starting | Higher friction, wear |
| **Using the piston as a pump during start** | Manually cranking gives energy | Small petrol engines (kick-start) | Cheap, simple | Operator effort needed |
| **Scotch Yoke / Pantograph Mechanisms** | Different kinematics, no dead centre | Specialised machinery, simulators | Pure sinusoidal motion | Limited stroke length |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Slider-Crank Mechanism at Both Dead Centres

The following Mermaid block renders a **block-level functional architecture flow** that maps the kinematic states, force vectors, and dead-centre triggers. It is engineered to be safe from Mermaid parser errors by using purely alphanumeric node IDs and clean labels.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[START Mechanism State] --> B{Crank Angle theta}
    B -- theta equals 0 deg --> C[INNER DEAD CENTRE IDC]
    B -- theta equals 90 deg --> D[MIDSTROKE - Maximum Velocity]
    B -- theta equals 180 deg --> E[OUTER DEAD CENTRE ODC]
    B -- theta equals 270 deg --> F[MIDSTROKE - Maximum Velocity Return]
    C --> G[Effective Crank Arm equals 0]
    E --> G
    D --> H[Effective Crank Arm equals r sin theta plus phi - Maximum]
    F --> H
    G --> I[Crank Effort T equals 0]
    H --> J[Crank Effort T equals P times r times sin theta plus phi]
    I --> K{Is External Torque Available}
    K -- YES Flywheel Inertia --> L[Crank Crosses DC Smoothly]
    K -- NO Insufficient Energy --> M[ENGINE STALLS AT DC]
    J --> N[Slider Velocity V equals r omega sin theta plus n sin 2 theta over 2]
    L --> O[Continue Rotation to next DC]
    M --> P[Engine Restart Required]
    N --> Q[Slider Acceleration a equals r omega squared cos theta plus n cos 2 theta]
    Q --> R[Peak Inertia Force at DC]
    R --> S[Design Constraint for Connecting Rod and Crank Pin]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Force Resolution and Crank Effort Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Slider Thrust P along line of stroke] --> B[Force transmitted along Connecting Rod AB]
    B --> C[Resolve at Crank Pin A]
    C --> D[Radial Component P cos theta plus phi - NO TORQUE]
    C --> E[Tangential Component P sin theta plus phi - PRODUCES TORQUE]
    E --> F[T equals P times r times sin theta plus phi]
    F --> G[Expand using sin phi equals n sin theta]
    G --> H[T equals P times r times sin theta plus n sin 2 theta over 2]
    H --> I{At Dead Centre}
    I -- theta equals 0 deg --> J[T equals 0 - IDC]
    I -- theta equals 180 deg --> K[T equals 0 - ODC]
    J --> L[CRISIS: Slider cannot drive Crank]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Solution: Flywheel stores Inertia Torque I times alpha]
    M --> N[Crank Crosses DC successfully]
```

### 4.3 ASCII Schematic — Slider-Crank at Inner and Outer Dead Centres

```
      INNER DEAD CENTRE (IDC)              OUTER DEAD CENTRE (ODC)
      Theta = 0 degrees                     Theta = 180 degrees

      A========B                            O========A===========B
      Crank+rod folded                      Crank+rod extended
      |        |                            |                    |
      |        v                            |                    v
      +--O (crank centre)                   +--O (crank centre)
      |
      v slider
      [B]
      
   Force P from slider along OA:           Force P from slider along BA-extended:
   Moment arm = 0                          Moment arm = 0
   T = 0  ==>  ENGINE STALLS               T = 0  ==>  ENGINE STALLS
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Methods to Overcome Dead Centre Problem

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Dead Centre Problem Detected] --> B{Solution Strategy}
    B --> C[Method 1: Flywheel with High Inertia]
    B --> D[Method 2: Multi-Cylinder Arrangement]
    B --> E[Method 3: External Starting Torque]
    B --> F[Method 4: Modified Mechanism Whitworth Quick Return]
    C --> C1[Stores KE equals half I omega squared]
    C1 --> C2[Releases Energy to Cross DC]
    D --> D1[2 cylinders 90 deg apart]
    D1 --> D2[4 cylinders 0 90 180 270 apart]
    D2 --> D3[6 cylinders 0 60 120 180 240 300 apart]
    E --> E1[Compressed Air Start]
    E1 --> E2[Electric Starter Motor]
    E2 --> E3[Manual Crank or Kick Start]
    F --> F1[Crank driven mechanism not slider driven]
    F1 --> F2[Whitworth Quick Return uses separate drive crank]
    C2 --> G[Crank Crosses DC Successfully]
    D3 --> G
    E3 --> G
    F2 --> G
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q: Define the terms "Inner Dead Centre" and "Outer Dead Centre" as applied to a slider-crank mechanism. Why does the mechanism stall at these positions?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Inner Dead Centre (IDC)** is the position of the slider-crank mechanism at which the slider is closest to the crank centre. In this position, the crank and the connecting rod are collinear and overlap each other, and the crank angle $\theta = 0°$ (by convention).

The **Outer Dead Centre (ODC)** is the position at which the slider is farthest from the crank centre. Here, the crank and connecting rod are collinear but extended, and $\theta = 180°$.

At both dead centres, the **line of stroke passes through the crank centre**. The force $P$ acting on the slider is transmitted along the connecting rod, which is along the crank itself. Hence, the **effective crank arm becomes zero**, and the **turning moment $T = P \cdot r \cdot \sin(\theta \pm \phi) = 0$**. The slider cannot produce a torque to rotate the crank, causing the mechanism to stall. **(3 Marks)**

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample]`

**Q: State any three methods to overcome the dead centre problem in a single-cylinder reciprocating engine.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Use of a Flywheel:** A heavy flywheel mounted on the crankshaft stores kinetic energy ($E_k = \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$) during the power stroke and releases it to drive the crank past the dead centres during the idle strokes.

2. **Use of Multiple Cylinders:** In a multi-cylinder engine, the cranks are arranged at offset angles (e.g., 0°–90°–180°–270° for 4 cylinders). When one piston is at DC, another is mid-stroke, ensuring continuous torque.

3. **External Starting Mechanism:** Devices like kick-starters, electric starters, or compressed air systems provide an initial external torque to push the crank past the first DC. **(3 Marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** use $\sin\phi = n\sin\theta$ incorrectly. The sign of the $\pm$ in the crank-effort equation depends on whether the rod is in **tension or compression** (outstroke vs. instroke).
> - **Do NOT** forget to convert angles to **radians** when computing work, power, or inertia torque — KTU examiners deduct 1 full mark for this.
> - **Do NOT** state the dead-centre condition as "force is zero." It is the **torque** that is zero, not the force. The force can be very large (gas pressure at TDC in IC engines), but it still cannot produce rotation.
> - In crank-effort diagrams, **always mark the angle axis properly** (0° to 360°), and label the two dead centres at 0° and 180° — many students swap them.

---

#### **Part B Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**In a single-cylinder horizontal reciprocating engine, the crank radius is 100 mm and the connecting rod length is 400 mm. The crank rotates at 300 rpm. When the crank has turned through 45° from the inner dead centre, the thrust on the piston is 25 kN. Determine:**

**(a)** [7 Marks] The tangential force on the crank pin and the crank effort (turning moment) at this instant.

**(b)** [7 Marks] The position of the crank at which the crank effort is maximum during the outstroke, and the corresponding maximum crank effort.

---

##### **Model Solution for Part B Question A:**

**Given Data:**
- $r = 100$ mm $= 0.1$ m
- $l = 400$ mm $= 0.4$ m
- $N = 300$ rpm
- $\theta = 45°$ from IDC
- $P = 25$ kN $= 25{,}000$ N

**Step 1 — Compute the ratio $n$:**
$$n = \dfrac{r}{l} = \dfrac{100}{400} = 0.25$$

**Step 2 — Compute the obliquity $\phi$ at $\theta = 45°$:**
$$\sin\phi = n \sin\theta = 0.25 \times \sin 45° = 0.25 \times 0.7071 = 0.1768$$
$$\phi = \sin^{-1}(0.1768) = 10.18°$$

---

**(a) Tangential Force and Crank Effort at $\theta = 45°$:**

**Step 3 — Tangential force on the crank pin:**

The tangential force is the component of the connecting-rod force perpendicular to the crank:
$$P_T = P \cdot \sin(\theta + \phi) = 25{,}000 \times \sin(45° + 10.18°)$$
$$P_T = 25{,}000 \times \sin(55.18°) = 25{,}000 \times 0.8212 = 20{,}530 \text{ N} \approx 20.53 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Crank effort (turning moment):**
$$T = P_T \cdot r = 20{,}530 \times 0.1 = 2053 \text{ N·m} = 2.053 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 5 — Verification using Rankine's formula:**

For the outstroke:
$$T = P \cdot r \left[\sin\theta + \dfrac{n \sin 2\theta}{2}\right]$$
$$T = 25{,}000 \times 0.1 \left[\sin 45° + \dfrac{0.25 \times \sin 90°}{2}\right]$$
$$T = 2500 \times \left[0.7071 + \dfrac{0.25 \times 1}{2}\right] = 2500 \times [0.7071 + 0.125]$$
$$T = 2500 \times 0.8321 = 2080.3 \text{ N·m}$$

The small discrepancy (~1.3%) is due to the binomial approximation in Rankine's formula. Using the exact trigonometric value ($2.053$ kN·m) is more accurate. **Answer (a):** $P_T = 20.53$ kN, $T = 2.053$ kN·m. **(7 Marks)**

---

**(b) Position of Maximum Crank Effort During Outstroke:**

**Step 6 — Differentiate the crank-effort expression:**

For the exact crank-effort: $T = P \cdot r \cdot \sin(\theta + \phi)$, where $\sin\phi = n\sin\theta$.

To find the maximum, set $\dfrac{dT}{d\theta} = 0$:
$$\dfrac{dT}{d\theta} = P \cdot r \cdot \cos(\theta + \phi) \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{d\phi}{d\theta}\right) = 0$$

Since $P \cdot r \neq 0$:
$$\cos(\theta + \phi) = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad \dfrac{d\phi}{d\theta} \to \infty \text{ (not physical)}$$

Therefore:
$$\theta + \phi = 90°$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the critical angle:**

Substitute $\sin\phi = n\sin\theta$:
$$\sin\phi = n \sin(90° - \phi) = n \cos\phi$$
$$\tan\phi = n = 0.25$$
$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.25) = 14.04°$$
$$\theta = 90° - \phi = 90° - 14.04° = 75.96° \approx 76°$$

**Step 8 — Maximum crank effort:**
$$T_{\max} = P \cdot r \cdot \sin(\theta + \phi) = P \cdot r \cdot \sin(90°) = P \cdot r$$
$$T_{\max} = 25{,}000 \times 0.1 = 2500 \text{ N·m} = 2.5 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Answer (b):** Maximum crank effort occurs at $\theta \approx 76°$ from IDC, and $T_{\max} = 2.5$ kN·m. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Part B Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**A horizontal single-cylinder engine has a crank of length 150 mm and a connecting rod of length 600 mm. The mass of the reciprocating parts is 50 kg. The crank rotates at 240 rpm. Calculate:**

**(a)** [7 Marks] The inertia force on the piston and the inertia torque on the crankshaft when the crank has turned through 60° from IDC.

**(b)** [7 Marks] The position of the crank where the inertia torque is maximum, and the magnitude of the maximum inertia torque.

---

##### **Model Solution for Part B Question B:**

**Given Data:**
- $r = 150$ mm $= 0.15$ m
- $l = 600$ mm $= 0.6$ m
- $m = 50$ kg
- $N = 240$ rpm
- $\theta = 60°$ from IDC

**Step 1 — Compute $\omega$ and $n$:**
$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 240}{60} = 8\pi = 25.13 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$n = \dfrac{r}{l} = \dfrac{150}{600} = 0.25$$

---

**(a) Inertia Force and Inertia Torque at $\theta = 60°$:**

**Step 2 — Inertia force on the piston:**

The piston (slider) acceleration is:
$$a = r\omega^2 \left(\cos\theta + n\cos 2\theta\right)$$

Substitute:
$$a = 0.15 \times (25.13)^2 \times \left[\cos 60° + 0.25 \cos 120°\right]$$
$$a = 0.15 \times 631.65 \times \left[0.5 + 0.25 \times (-0.5)\right]$$
$$a = 94.75 \times \left[0.5 - 0.125\right] = 94.75 \times 0.375$$
$$a = 35.53 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 3 — Inertia force:**
$$F_I = m \cdot a = 50 \times 35.53 = 1776.5 \text{ N} \approx 1.78 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Inertia torque on the crankshaft:**

The inertia force acts along the line of stroke, equivalent to a slider thrust $P = F_I$. The inertia torque equals the crank effort with $P = F_I$:
$$T_I = F_I \cdot r \cdot \sin(\theta + \phi)$$

Compute $\phi$ at $\theta = 60°$:
$$\sin\phi = 0.25 \times \sin 60° = 0.25 \times 0.866 = 0.2165$$
$$\phi = 12.52°$$
$$\theta + \phi = 60° + 12.52° = 72.52°$$
$$\sin(72.52°) = 0.9540$$

Therefore:
$$T_I = 1776.5 \times 0.15 \times 0.9540 = 254.2 \text{ N·m}$$

**Answer (a):** Inertia force $F_I = 1.78$ kN; Inertia torque $T_I = 254.2$ N·m. **(7 Marks)**

---

**(b) Position and Magnitude of Maximum Inertia Torque:**

**Step 5 — Acceleration is maximum at the dead centre, but torque depends on geometry:**

Inertia force is maximum when $\vert \cos\theta + n\cos 2\theta \vert$ is maximum. This occurs at the **dead centres** ($\theta = 0°$ or $180°$), where $a = r\omega^2(1 + n)$.

**Step 6 — Compute maximum inertia force at IDC:**
$$a_{\max} = r\omega^2(1 + n) = 0.15 \times 631.65 \times 1.25 = 118.43 \text{ m/s}^2$$
$$F_{I,\max} = 50 \times 118.43 = 5921.5 \text{ N} \approx 5.92 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 7 — But the inertia torque at the DC is zero (dead centre crisis!):**

At IDC, $\theta = 0°$, so $\sin(0° + \phi) = \sin(0° + 0°) = 0$. Hence $T_I = 0$ at the very point where force is maximum.

**Step 8 — Maximum inertia torque actually occurs slightly past the DC:**

For maximum torque with inertia force, differentiate $T_I = m \cdot r\omega^2 (\cos\theta + n\cos 2\theta) \cdot r \cdot \sin(\theta + \phi)$ w.r.t. $\theta$ and equate to zero. This is solved numerically.

Using the standard result for the maximum torque angle (with $n = 0.25$):

**Iterative method — try $\theta = 76°$ (same as in Question A):**
$$\cos 76° = 0.2419, \cos 152° = -0.882$$
$$a = 0.15 \times 631.65 \times (0.2419 + 0.25 \times -0.882) = 94.75 \times 0.0214 = 2.03 \text{ m/s}^2$$

Try $\theta = 80°$:
$$\cos 80° = 0.1736, \cos 160° = -0.9397$$
$$a = 94.75 \times (0.1736 - 0.235) = 94.75 \times (-0.0614) = -5.82 \text{ m/s}^2$$

The peak $|a|$ for torque is around the value where $F_I \cdot \sin(\theta + \phi)$ is maximum, which is at $\theta \approx 76°$ from the previous result, but for inertia-torque, the sign of $F_I$ also matters.

**Simplified standard KTU answer:**
- Position of maximum inertia torque: **$\theta = 90° - \phi$ where $\tan\phi = n$**, i.e., at $\theta = 75.96°$ from IDC.
- Magnitude: $T_{I,\max} \approx m \cdot r^2 \cdot \omega^2 \cdot \sin 90° \cdot (\cos\theta + n\cos 2\theta)$ evaluated at this angle.

Substituting $\theta = 76°$ (and noting that the inertia force changes sign as the crank passes mid-stroke):
$$F_I(\theta = 76°) = 50 \times 94.75 \times (0.2419 + 0.25 \times -0.882) = 50 \times 94.75 \times 0.0214 = 101.4 \text{ N}$$
$$T_{I,\max} = 101.4 \times 0.15 \times \sin(90°) = 15.21 \text{ N·m}$$

This small value reflects that the acceleration itself passes through zero near $76°$. The actual maximum **magnitude** of inertia torque occurs at the angle where $|F_I \cdot \sin(\theta + \phi)|$ is maximum, which is between $50°$ and $60°$ — typically solved graphically or via numerical methods in KTU exams.

**Answer (b):** Maximum inertia torque occurs near $\theta \approx 76°$, with a value depending on the instantaneous $F_I$. For a more conservative answer, students may state that **maximum inertia force is at DC** but the corresponding **torque is zero**, illustrating the dead-centre problem itself. **(7 Marks)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Dead Centre Problems**

- ☐ **Dead Centre** = Position where the line of stroke passes through the crank centre; effective crank arm $= 0$; crank effort $T = 0$.
- ☐ **IDC** = $\theta = 0°$, slider closest to crank centre, crank and rod overlap.
- ☐ **ODC** = $\theta = 180°$, slider farthest from crank centre, crank and rod extended.
- ☐ **Root cause of stalling:** The slider thrust produces no torque on the crank at DC.
- ☐ **Crank Effort (Outstroke):** $T = P \cdot r \left[\sin\theta + \dfrac{n \sin 2\theta}{2}\right]$ — **Rankine's formula**.
- ☐ **Crank Effort (Instroke):** $T = P \cdot r \left[\sin\theta - \dfrac{n \sin 2\theta}{2}\right]$.
- ☐ **Angularity:** $\sin\phi = n\sin\theta$, with $n = r/l$ and $0 < n < 1$ (always).
- ☐ **Maximum crank effort angle:** $\theta + \phi = 90°$ → $\tan\phi = n$ → $\theta = 90° - \tan^{-1}(n)$.
- ☐ **Maximum crank effort value:** $T_{\max} = P \cdot r$ (occurs at the angle above).
- ☐ **Inertia force** $= m \cdot a = m \cdot r\omega^2(\cos\theta + n\cos 2\theta)$; **maximum at DC** (crisis point).
- ☐ **Solutions to dead centre problem:** (i) Flywheel ($E_k = \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$), (ii) Multi-cylinder, (iii) External starter, (iv) Whitworth quick-return mechanism.
- ☐ **Flywheel sizing:** Heavier flywheel = easier to cross DC; rule of thumb: $I \geq \dfrac{P \cdot r}{\omega^2 \cdot (1+n)}$.
- ☐ **Engine classification:** Single-cylinder engines **always** have a dead-centre issue; multi-cylinder engines overcome it by offset crank arrangements.
- ☐ **Work done per cycle** (ideal, neglecting rod angularity): $W = P_{\text{mean}} \times 4r$ over two strokes.
- ☐ **SI units always:** crank length in metres, force in Newtons, torque in N·m, angular velocity in rad/s.
- ☐ **Common examiner traps:** forgetting the $\pm$ sign in the crank-effort formula, using degrees instead of radians in $\omega$, mixing up IDC and ODC angles.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Branch and Order Defects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT412 - KINEMATICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Branch and Order Defects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Branch and Order Defects** are the two primary categories of *contact-ratio-related failures* observed in involute gear meshing. They arise when the **Contact Ratio (n_c)** deviates from its design sweet-spot window of approximately **1.4 to 1.75**.

| Defect | Trigger Condition | Engineering Symptom |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Order Defect** | $n_c < 1$ | Impact, knocking, noise, vibration |
| **Branch Defect** | $n_c > 2$ | Lubricant starvation, power loss, scoring |
| **Healthy Mesh** | $1 \le n_c \le 2$ | Smooth, continuous, conjugate action |

### What is a "Branch of Contact"?

A **branch of contact** is the *continuous segment* of the **line of action** (path of contact) over which a *single pair of teeth* remains in mutual contact. Since the path of contact is divided at the **pitch point (P)** into an *approach* and a *recess*, every engaging pair contributes exactly one branch spanning these two sub-segments.

### What is the "Order of Contact"?

The **order of contact** is the *number of tooth pairs simultaneously in contact* at any given instant during the mesh cycle. For an involute pair with contact ratio $n_c$, the order varies between the integer parts of $n_c$ — i.e., it alternates between $\lfloor n_c \rfloor$ and $\lceil n_c \rceil$ as the meshing progresses.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of gear meshing like walking up a spiral staircase in a clock tower.**

- **Order of Contact** is analogous to the number of *feet on the stairs* at any instant. A safe staircase always has at least one foot on a step ($n_c \ge 1$). If the steps are too far apart ($n_c < 1$), your foot lands in mid-air — this is the *Order Defect*, producing a sharp impact every step.

- **Branch of Contact** is the *trail of footprints* left by one foot. If the steps are too shallow and closely spaced ($n_c > 2$), so many feet pile onto one step that the staircase becomes **crowded** — lubricant (grease) gets squeezed out, and the wood (gear teeth) starts to **score**. This is the *Branch Defect*.

**The Engineering Sweet Spot** is the staircase where steps are spaced such that exactly 1 or 2 feet (1.4 – 1.75 pairs average) rest on the steps at any time — enough to never lose contact, but few enough to leave room for the oil film.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Pressure Angle (Full-Depth Teeth):** $\phi = 20°$ *(also 14.5° legacy / 25° for high-load applications)*
- **Standard Addendum Coefficient:** $a^* = 1$ module *(for full-depth teeth)*
- **Recommended Contact Ratio Range:** $1.4 \le n_c \le 1.75$ *(per AGMA / IS 4460 standards)*
- **Minimum Theoretical Contact Ratio:** $n_c = 1$ *(absolute limit for continuous action)*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Building Blocks: The Five Contact Parameters

Before diagnosing defects, we must precisely define the five geometric parameters that govern gear meshing quality. They are arranged in a logical derivation cascade:

### Step 1 — The Path of Contact (Line of Action)
For involute gears, the path traced by the instantaneous point of contact is a *straight line* — the **common internal tangent to the two base circles**. It is inclined at the pressure angle $\phi$ to the common tangent at the pitch point.

### Step 2 — The Length of Path of Contact ($L$)
This is the *straight-line distance* along the line of action between the two intersection points of the **addendum circles** with the line of action.

- **T₁** = start of contact (intersection with pinion addendum circle)
- **P** = pitch point (intersection with common tangent to pitch circles)
- **T₂** = end of contact (intersection with gear addendum circle)

### Step 3 — The Arc of Contact ($\theta_s$)
This is the *arc length traced on the pitch circle* by a point during one full engagement cycle.

- **Arc of Approach** = path length on the pinion pitch circle from T₁ to P
- **Arc of Recess** = path length on the pinion pitch circle from P to T₂

### Step 4 — Contact Ratio ($n_c$)
The dimensionless ratio of *total length of path of contact* to *circular pitch*. It is the **single most important diagnostic number** for branch and order defect analysis.

### Step 5 — Order of Contact
The instantaneous count of tooth pairs in contact — directly tied to the fractional part of $n_c$.

---

## 2.2 Why and How Each Step Works

- **Why a straight line of action?** → Because the involute profile is generated by unwinding a string from the base circle, every point of contact is on the common tangent to both base circles.
- **Why divide at the pitch point?** → The pitch point is the unique location where the gears roll without slipping; engagement begins or ends symmetrically around it.
- **Why does the contact ratio matter?** → It is a *direct integer-indicator* of how many pairs share the load at any instant. A value below 1 is mathematically equivalent to "gaps in coverage".

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use the symbols: $R$ = pinion pitch radius, $r$ = gear pitch radius, $R_a$ = pinion addendum radius, $r_a$ = gear addendum radius, $R_b$ = pinion base radius, $r_b$ = gear base radius, $a$ = addendum, $m$ = module, $p_c$ = circular pitch, $T_1, T_2$ = number of teeth.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Pinion pitch radius | $R = m \cdot T_1 / 2$ | mm |
| 2 | Gear pitch radius | $r = m \cdot T_2 / 2$ | mm |
| 3 | Addendum radius (pinion) | $R_a = R + a$ | Typically $a = m$ |
| 4 | Addendum radius (gear) | $r_a = r + a$ | — |
| 5 | Base radius (pinion) | $R_b = R \cos\phi$ | $\cos 20° \approx 0.9397$ |
| 6 | Base radius (gear) | $r_b = r \cos\phi$ | — |
| 7 | **Length of path of approach** | $L_a = \sqrt{R_a^{2} - R_b^{2}} \;-\; R \sin\phi$ | mm |
| 8 | **Length of path of recess** | $L_r = \sqrt{r_a^{2} - r_b^{2}} \;-\; r \sin\phi$ | mm |
| 9 | **Length of path of contact** | $L = L_a + L_r$ | mm |
| 10 | **Arc of contact (on pitch circle)** | $\theta_{s} = L / \cos\phi$ | mm of arc length |
| 11 | Circular pitch | $p_c = \pi m$ | mm |
| 12 | **Contact Ratio** | $n_c = L / p_c$ | **Dimensionless** |
| 13 | Angular contact (pinion) | $\theta_{1} = L / (R \cos\phi)$ | radians |
| 14 | Angular contact (gear) | $\theta_{2} = L / (r \cos\phi)$ | radians |
| 15 | Branch defect condition | $n_c > 2$ | Crowding, starvation |
| 16 | Order defect condition | $n_c < 1$ | Impact, noise |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Transmissions:** Passenger cars require $n_c \approx 1.6$ to absorb torque fluctuations and damp gear whine.
- **Aerospace Gears:** Helicopter main-rotor gearboxes are designed with $n_c$ approaching 2 to ensure *zero-impact* meshing under all load conditions.
- **Industrial Gearboxes:** High-contact-ratio profile-shifted gears ($x > 0$) deliberately push $n_c$ upward to eliminate order defects at the cost of accepting mild branch crowding — managed via high-pressure lubrication systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Length of Path of Approach ($L_a$)

We wish to find the distance $T_1 P$ measured along the line of action.

**Given Geometry:**

- The pinion has pitch radius $R$, base radius $R_b = R\cos\phi$, and addendum radius $R_a = R + a$.
- The point $T_1$ is where the *addendum circle* of the pinion meets the *line of action*.
- The point $P$ is the *pitch point* — where the common tangent to both pitch circles touches them.

**Step 1:** Drop a perpendicular from the pinion center $O_1$ to the line of action. This perpendicular equals the base radius $R_b$ (since the line of action is the common tangent to the base circles).

**Step 2:** The distance from $O_1$ to the point $T_1$ on the line of action, by simple right-triangle geometry, is $\sqrt{R_a^{2} - R_b^{2}}$.

**Step 3:** Similarly, the distance from $O_1$ to the point $P$ on the line of action is $R \sin\phi$ (the perpendicular distance is $R\cos\phi$, so the hypotenuse to the line of action is $R\sin\phi$).

**Step 4:** Subtract the inner segment from the outer segment to get the path of approach:

$$L_a = T_1 P = \sqrt{R_a^{2} - R_b^{2}} \;-\; R \sin\phi$$

**Why this works:** The two segments $T_1 P$ and the perpendicular projection share the *same perpendicular foot* on the line of action — the line of action is straight, so the difference of the two oblique distances along the line equals the difference of the squared hypotenuses under the right-triangle relation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Length of Path of Recess ($L_r$)

By identical reasoning, applied to the *gear* (center $O_2$, pitch radius $r$, base radius $r_b = r\cos\phi$, addendum radius $r_a = r + a$):

**Step 1:** The point $T_2$ lies on the addendum circle of the gear, on the line of action. Its distance from $O_2$ along the line of action is $\sqrt{r_a^{2} - r_b^{2}}$.

**Step 2:** The distance from $O_2$ to the pitch point $P$ along the line of action is $r \sin\phi$.

**Step 3:** The path of recess is the difference:

$$L_r = P T_2 = \sqrt{r_a^{2} - r_b^{2}} \;-\; r \sin\phi$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Total Length of Path of Contact ($L$)

The total length is simply the sum of the two segments, since $T_1$, $P$, and $T_2$ are *collinear* on the line of action:

$$L = T_1 T_2 = T_1 P + P T_2 = L_a + L_r$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \sqrt{R_a^{2} - R_b^{2}} \;-\; R \sin\phi \;+\; \sqrt{r_a^{2} - r_b^{2}} \;-\; r \sin\phi \\
  &= \sqrt{R_a^{2} - R_b^{2}} \;+\; \sqrt{r_a^{2} - r_b^{2}} \;-\; (R + r) \sin\phi
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **master formula** for all subsequent defect analysis.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Contact Ratio ($n_c$)

The contact ratio is the *number of circular pitches* that fit into the path of contact length. This represents the *average number of pairs* simultaneously sharing the load.

**Step 1:** By definition,

$$n_c = \frac{\text{Length of path of contact}}{\text{Circular pitch}} = \frac{L}{p_c}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $p_c = \pi m$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_c = \frac{L}{\pi m} = \frac{\sqrt{R_a^{2} - R_b^{2}} \;+\; \sqrt{r_a^{2} - r_b^{2}} \;-\; (R + r) \sin\phi}{\pi m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** For a *full-depth* tooth system where $R_a = R + m$ and $R_b = R\cos\phi$, the term $\sqrt{R_a^{2} - R_b^{2}}$ can be simplified:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{R_a^{2} - R_b^{2}} &= \sqrt{(R+m)^{2} - R^{2}\cos^{2}\phi} \\
&= \sqrt{R^{2} + 2Rm + m^{2} - R^{2}\cos^{2}\phi} \\
&= \sqrt{R^{2}\sin^{2}\phi + 2Rm + m^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This compact form is the engineer's *quick calculation* expression.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem Statement:**
> Two involute gears in external mesh have $T_1 = 20$ teeth (pinion) and $T_2 = 40$ teeth (gear), with module $m = 5$ mm and pressure angle $\phi = 20°$. Find the length of path of contact, arc of contact, contact ratio, and determine whether branch or order defects are present.

**Given Data:**

$$R = \frac{m T_1}{2} = \frac{5 \times 20}{2} = 50 \text{ mm}$$

$$r = \frac{m T_2}{2} = \frac{5 \times 40}{2} = 100 \text{ mm}$$

$$R_a = R + m = 55 \text{ mm}, \qquad r_a = r + m = 105 \text{ mm}$$

$$R_b = R \cos 20° = 50 \times 0.9397 = 46.985 \text{ mm}$$

$$r_b = r \cos 20° = 100 \times 0.9397 = 93.97 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 1: Path of Approach**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_a &= \sqrt{R_a^{2} - R_b^{2}} \;-\; R \sin\phi \\
    &= \sqrt{55^{2} - 46.985^{2}} \;-\; 50 \times \sin 20° \\
    &= \sqrt{3025 - 2207.6} \;-\; 50 \times 0.3420 \\
    &= \sqrt{817.4} \;-\; 17.10 \\
    &= 28.59 \;-\; 17.10 \\
    &= 11.49 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Path of Recess**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_r &= \sqrt{r_a^{2} - r_b^{2}} \;-\; r \sin\phi \\
    &= \sqrt{105^{2} - 93.97^{2}} \;-\; 100 \times \sin 20° \\
    &= \sqrt{11025 - 8830.4} \;-\; 100 \times 0.3420 \\
    &= \sqrt{2194.6} \;-\; 34.20 \\
    &= 46.85 \;-\; 34.20 \\
    &= 12.65 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Total Length of Path of Contact**

$$L = L_a + L_r = 11.49 + 12.65 = 24.14 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Arc of Contact**

$$\theta_{s} = \frac{L}{\cos\phi} = \frac{24.14}{\cos 20°} = \frac{24.14}{0.9397} = 25.69 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Contact Ratio**

$$n_c = \frac{L}{p_c} = \frac{L}{\pi m} = \frac{24.14}{\pi \times 5} = \frac{24.14}{15.708} = 1.537$$

**Step 6: Defect Diagnosis**

> [!NOTE]
> Since $1.0 < n_c = 1.537 < 2.0$, the mesh is **healthy** — neither order defect nor branch defect exists. The order of contact alternates between 1 and 2 pairs, which is the ideal condition for smooth power transmission.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Defect Decision Tree

| Computed $n_c$ | Order of Contact Variation | Diagnosis | Required Action |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $n_c < 1.0$ | Periodically drops to **0** | **ORDER DEFECT** — impact at every tooth entry | Increase addendum (profile shift +ve), or reduce circular pitch |
| $1.0 \le n_c < 1.4$ | Always 1, occasionally 2 | Borderline — minor noise expected | Slight profile shift recommended |
| $1.4 \le n_c \le 1.75$ | 1 to 2 pairs | **IDEAL OPERATION** | No change needed |
| $1.75 < n_c \le 2.0$ | Mostly 2 pairs | Slight crowding possible | Verify lubrication regime |
| $n_c > 2.0$ | 2 pairs, sometimes 3 | **BRANCH DEFECT** — over-crowding | Reduce addendum, increase module, or use thinner face width |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: The Defect Analysis Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Gear Mesh Initiated]) --> Geo[Define Geometry: R, r, m, phi]
    Geo --> Rad[Compute Pitch, Addendum, Base Radii]
    Rad --> Approach[Calculate Path of Approach La]
    Rad --> Recess[Calculate Path of Recess Lr]
    Approach --> Sum[Total Path of Contact L]
    Recess --> Sum
    Sum --> CR[Compute Contact Ratio nc = L over pi m]
    CR --> Decision{nc Value?}
    Decision -->|nc less than 1| OrderDef[Order Defect]
    Decision -->|nc between 1 and 2| Healthy[Smooth Mesh]
    Decision -->|nc greater than 2| BranchDef[Branch Defect]
    OrderDef --> Rx1[Remedy: Profile Shift Positive]
    BranchDef --> Rx2[Remedy: Increase Module or Reduce Addendum]
    Healthy --> Conf[Design Confirmed]
    Rx1 --> Validate[Recheck nc]
    Rx2 --> Validate
    Conf --> Validate
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Path of Contact — Branches & Order Zones

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph lineA[Line of Action]
        T1[Start Point T1] -->|Approach Branch| P[Pitch Point P]
        P -->|Recess Branch| T2[End Point T2]
    end
    subgraph pair1[Pair 1 Active Window]
        P1A[T1 contact begin] --> P1Z[T2 contact end]
    end
    subgraph pair2[Pair 2 Active Window]
        P2A[T1 contact begin] --> P2Z[T2 contact end]
    end
    P1A --> T1
    P1Z --> P2A
    P2Z --> T2
    Overlap[Overlap Zone n c greater than 1] -.covers.-> pair1
    Overlap -.covers.-> pair2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Engineering Implications of Defects

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OD[Order Defect nc less than 1] --> OD1[Impact Loading]
    OD --> OD2[Acoustic Emission Noise]
    OD --> OD3[Pitch Error Sensitivity]
    BD[Branch Defect nc greater than 2] --> BD1[Oil Film Squeeze Out]
    BD --> BD2[Scoring and Pitting]
    BD --> BD3[Efficiency Loss]
    OD1 --> Fix1[Increase Face Width]
    OD2 --> Fix1
    BD1 --> Fix2[EP Lubricant or Profile Shift Negative]
    BD2 --> Fix2
    Fix1 --> R[Re compute Contact Ratio]
    Fix2 --> R
    R --> OK[Confirm Healthy Mesh]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid schematics intentionally use **functional block architecture** rather than literal gear-tooth sketches. This is a robust engineering representation that clearly traces how each geometric input flows through the analysis to a definitive defect diagnosis, while remaining 100% syntactically valid for Mermaid rendering.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

### Q.A1. Define the term "contact ratio" of gear teeth. What is the recommended value of contact ratio for a smooth and quiet operation?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **contact ratio ($n_c$)** is defined as the *ratio of the length of the path of contact to the circular pitch*. It indicates the *average number of pairs of teeth in contact* at any instant during the mesh cycle.

$$n_c = \frac{L}{p_c} = \frac{\sqrt{R_a^{2} - R_b^{2}} + \sqrt{r_a^{2} - r_b^{2}} - (R+r)\sin\phi}{\pi m}$$

For a **smooth, quiet, and continuous transmission** of motion, the contact ratio must be **greater than 1** (to prevent order defect). The *recommended range* per AGMA / IS standards is **$1.4 \le n_c \le 1.75$**, which ensures that 1 to 2 pairs of teeth are always in contact without excessive crowding.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

### Q.A2. Differentiate between **branch defect** and **order defect** in gear meshing.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Branch Defect | Order Defect |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cause** | Contact ratio $n_c > 2$ | Contact ratio $n_c < 1$ |
