> 3. **Why involute? — generic vs. specific reason:** A generic answer like "it is easy to manufacture" earns **partial credit** only. The *specific* reason — insensitivity to centre-distance variation, automatic satisfaction of the fundamental law, interchangeability of gears — must be stated. **Lose 1 mark** for the generic answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3 (Cam Followers & Fundamental Law of Gearing)**

### **Translating Roller Follower Cam**
- Pitch curve = path of the **roller centre**; cam profile = path of the **roller rim**; separated by $r_r$ along the common normal.
- For a radial in-line follower: $r_p = r_b + r_r$. For an offset follower: $r_{p,n} = \sqrt{r_b^2 - e^2} - r_r$, $r_{p,f} = \sqrt{r_b^2 - e^2} + r_r$.
- The cam profile is the **inner offset** of the pitch curve (towards the cam centre for convex profiles).
- The offset follower reduces the **maximum pressure angle** compared to the in-line follower for the same lift and base circle.

### **Oscillating Roller Follower**
- Follower displacement is **angular** ($\psi$ in radians) rather than linear.
- Linear rise $s = L \cdot \psi$, where $L$ is the arm length from fulcrum to roller centre.
- Construction uses **reversal of motion** — the cam is held fixed and the follower arm is rotated in the opposite sense.

### **Fundamental Law of Gearing**
- **Statement:** The common normal at the contact point must pass through a **fixed point (pitch point) on the line of centres**.
- **Derivation cornerstone:** $v_{1n} = v_{2n}$ on the common normal, leading to $\omega_1 / \omega_2 = \overline{O_2P} / \overline{O_1P}$ = constant.
- **Conjugate profiles** (involute, cycloid) are those that satisfy this law.
- **Involute** is the industry standard because it **automatically** satisfies the law and is **insensitive to small centre-distance errors**.
- **Pressure angle $\phi$** is the angle between the line of action (common normal) and the common tangent to the pitch circles; standard full-depth values are $14.5°$ and $20°$.

### **Pressure Angle — Design Limits**
- Translating follower: $\alpha_{\max} \le 30°$
- Oscillating follower: $\alpha_{\max} \le 45°$
- Exceeding these limits risks **jamming, side-thrust overload, and follower tilt**.

### **Common KTU Pitfalls**
- Forgetting to convert angles to radians in cam-motion formulas.
- Confusing pitch circle with base circle in gear diagrams.
- Stating the involute is used "because it is easy to manufacture" — must mention **centre-distance insensitivity**.
- Treating the roller radius $r_r$ as a part of the base circle (it is *not*; the base circle is purely a property of the cam profile, $r_r$ is added to obtain the prime circle).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics of Involutive Action
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT412 - KINEMATICS OF MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Characteristics of Involutive Action # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Involute Action** is defined as the geometric and kinematic behavior exhibited by two meshing gear teeth (or cam-follower pairs) whose working profiles are generated as **involutes** of their respective base circles. The fundamental characteristic is that the **common normal** drawn at every point of contact between the two conjugate profiles always passes through a single, fixed point on the line of centers — the **pitch point (P)**. When this condition is satisfied, the **angular velocity ratio** between the driving and driven members remains **constant** throughout the entire period of engagement. This fundamental property is known in KTU 2024 Scheme terminology as the **"Fundamental Law of Gearing"** or the **"Law of Conjugate Action."** Mathematically, for a pair of meshing involute gears rotating about fixed centers $O_1$ and $O_2$ with angular velocities $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$, the law demands: $$\frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \frac{O_2N}{O_1N} = \text{constant}$$ where $N$ is the instantaneous point of contact and $O_1N$, $O_2N$ are the normals drawn from the respective centers to the common tangent at $N$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The "Involute Action" is not merely the tooth shape — it is a *kinematic condition* that must be satisfied for **smooth, vibration-free, constant-velocity power transmission**. Any deviation from involute action causes fluctuating velocity, noise, wear, and dynamic loading. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine two cyclists riding parallel rails toward each other, and at the moment they cross, they hand off a baton. For the handoff to be smooth and not jarring, the baton must be passed along a line that always points to the *same spot on the ground* between them — the "handshake line." If that line wandered, the baton would jerk sideways and one cyclist would be pushed off balance. **That "fixed handshake line" is the common normal in involute action.** | Mechanical Analogy | Gear Equivalent | |---|---| | Two cyclists on parallel rails | Two gear centers $O_1$ and $O_2$ | | The baton | The point of tooth contact $N$ | | The fixed handshake line on ground | The **common normal** through pitch point $P$ | | Smooth velocity ratio | Constant $\omega_1 / \omega_2$ | > [!NOTE] > **Why "Involute"?** An involute is the curve traced by the end of a taut string being unwound from a cylinder. This particular curve has the magical property that the string (the normal) is *always tangent to the base circle*, meaning the line of action is invariant — guaranteeing the constant-velocity ratio. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics The following are universally fixed / standard values in KTU board problems: - **Standard Full Depth System (135° system, NOT used in KTU):** Addendum = $1 \text{ module}$, Dedendum = $1.25 \text{ module}$ - **Standard Stub Depth System:** Addendum = $0.8 \text{ module}$, Dedendum = $1.0 \text{ module}$ - **Standard Pressure Angle (KTU 2024):** $\phi = 20°$ (modern standard, replacing the older $14.5°$) - **Minimum number of teeth** (to avoid interference, $20°$ pressure angle, full depth): **17 teeth** on the pinion - **Module (m):** ratio of pitch circle diameter to number of teeth, in **mm** - **Circular Pitch (Pc):** arc length along the pitch circle between corresponding points on adjacent teeth # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Step-by-Step Logic of Involute Action The involute action is built upon a layered hierarchy of geometric and kinematic principles. Below is the rigorous KTU-grade logical sequence: **Step 1 — Generation of the Involute Profile** - A string is wrapped around a **base circle** of radius $r_b$. - As it is unwound, the free end traces a curve in space. - This curve is the **involute** of the base circle. - Mathematically: $r = \dfrac{r_b}{\cos \psi}$ where $\psi$ is the **pressure angle at any point** and $r$ is the radius of curvature at that point on the involute. **Step 2 — Establishing the Conjugate Pair** - Two involute profiles (one on each gear) mesh such that at every instant of contact, the **common tangent** to both profiles coincides with the **common normal** at the point of contact. - By construction of the involute, this common normal is **tangent to both base circles simultaneously**. **Step 3 — The Pitch Point Theorem (Core of Involute Action)** - The tangent point of the two base circles lies on the **line of centers** $O_1O_2$. - This point is called the **pitch point (P)**. - The common normal at any point of contact $N$ must, by geometry, pass through $P$. **Step 4 — Velocity Ratio Invariance** - Since the common normal always passes through the fixed point $P$, the velocity component of the contact point along the common normal must be identical for both gears. - This forces $\omega_1 \cdot O_1P = \omega_2 \cdot O_2P$ ⇒ constant ratio. **Step 5 — The Pressure Angle Stays Constant** - The angle between the common normal and the common tangent at the pitch point is the **pressure angle $\phi$**. - It can be shown that this angle is *constant* throughout the engagement for involute gears (unlike cycloidal gears where it varies). **Step 6 — The Path of Contact** - The locus traced by the point of contact $N$ on a fixed reference frame is a **straight line**, called the **line of action** or **path of contact**. - This line is tangent to both base circles and is inclined at angle $\phi$ to the common tangent at the pitch point. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** Every formula below has appeared in at least one KTU University Examination. Memorize the boundary conditions (length of path of contact limits) and the unit conventions. | # | Quantity | Formula | Standard Symbol / Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Base Circle Radius** | $r_b = \dfrac{D \cos \phi}{2} = \dfrac{m \cdot T \cdot \cos \phi}{2}$ | $r_b$ in mm | | 2 | **Addendum Circle Radius** | $r_a = \dfrac{D}{2} + a = \dfrac{m \cdot T}{2} + m$ (full depth) | $r_a$ in mm | | 3 | **Dedendum Circle Radius** | $r_f = \dfrac{D}{2} - d = \dfrac{m \cdot T}{2} - 1.25\,m$ (full depth) | $r_f$ in mm | | 4 | **Length of Path of Contact** | $L_{pc} = \sqrt{r_{a1}^{\,2} - r_{b1}^{\,2}} + \sqrt{r_{a2}^{\,2} - r_{b2}^{\,2}}$ | $L_{pc}$ in mm | | 5 | **Length of Arc of Contact** | $L_{ac} = \dfrac{L_{pc}}{\cos \phi}$ | $L_{ac}$ in mm | | 6 | **Contact Ratio** | $n_c = \dfrac{L_{ac}}{P_c}$ (must be $\geq 1$ for continuous motion) | dimensionless | | 7 | **Circular Pitch** | $P_c = \pi m$ | $P_c$ in mm | | 8 | **Angular Velocity Ratio** | $\dfrac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \dfrac{D_2}{D_1}$ | dimensionless | | 9 | **Sliding Velocity at $N$** | $V_s = \omega_1 \cdot O_1N - \omega_2 \cdot O_2N$ (along common tangent) | m/s | | 10 | **Angle turned by pinion (engagement)** | $\theta_1 = \dfrac{L_{ac}}{r_1}$ | radians | > [!NOTE] > **Path Notation Convention:** $r_{a1}, r_{b1}$ refer to the addendum and base circle radii of the **driving (pinion)** gear; $r_{a2}, r_{b2}$ refer to the **driven gear**. Subscripts $1$ and $2$ are interchangeable with "pinion" and "gear" in KTU problems. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The involute action is not just academic — it is the *backbone* of nearly every power transmission system in modern engineering: - **Automotive Gearboxes:** Spur, helical, and double-helical gears all use involute profiles. The constant pressure angle ensures quiet, high-speed operation. - **Industrial Machinery:** Conveyor drives, mixers, mills, and pumps rely on involute action to transmit power without pulsations. - **Robotics and CNC:** Precision motion control requires the predictable, backlash-free engagement only involute geometry provides. - **Wind Turbines and Aerospace:** High-ratio planetary gearboxes use involute action for the same reasons at extreme torque and RPM. > [!TIP] > The reason involute gears replaced cycloidal gears in the 1900s is precisely the **manufacturing simplicity**: an involute rack has straight, flat sides. A gear-cutting hob (a rack-shaped cutter) can generate any involute gear by simple rolling motion — something impossible with cycloidal profiles. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Law of Conjugate Action (Fundamental Law of Gearing) **Statement to Prove:** The common normal at the point of contact between two meshing teeth must always pass through the pitch point $P$ on the line of centers, for the velocity ratio to remain constant. **Given Setup:** - Two gear centers at $O_1$ and $O_2$ with angular velocities $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$. - $N$ = instantaneous point of contact on the common tangent. - Common normal drawn at $N$ meets the line of centers $O_1O_2$ at point $K$. **Step 1 — Velocity of point $N$ on gear 1** The point $N$ on gear 1 has a velocity $v_{N1}$ perpendicular to $O_1N$: $$v_{N1} = \omega_1 \cdot O_1N$$ **Step 2 — Velocity of point $N$ on gear 2** Similarly, the velocity of the coincident point on gear 2 is: $$v_{N2} = \omega_2 \cdot O_2N$$ **Step 3 — Condition for No Separation (Conjugate Action)** Since the two teeth are in physical contact and must not interpenetrate or separate, the components of velocity along the **common normal** $NK$ must be equal for both gears. The component of $v_{N1}$ along $NK$ is $v_{N1} \cos \angle (v_{N1}, NK) = v_{N1} \sin(\angle O_1NK)$ — but using the right-geometry trick, the projection along the normal is: $$v_{N1} \sin \alpha_1 = v_{N2} \sin \alpha_2$$ where $\alpha_1 = \angle O_1NK$ and $\alpha_2 = \angle O_2NK$. In triangle $O_1NK$, the angle at $K$ is $90°$ (since $O_1N$ is the radius and $NK$ is perpendicular to the tangent at $N$). Therefore $\sin \alpha_1 = \dfrac{O_1K}{O_1N}$. Similarly $\sin \alpha_2 = \dfrac{O_2K}{O_2N}$. **Step 4 — Substituting the projections** $$\omega_1 \cdot O_1N \cdot \dfrac{O_1K}{O_1N} = \omega_2 \cdot O_2N \cdot \dfrac{O_2K}{O_2N}$$ This simplifies beautifully to: $$\omega_1 \cdot O_1K = \omega_2 \cdot O_2K$$ **Step 5 — The Velocity Ratio** $$\dfrac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \dfrac{O_2K}{O_1K}$$ **Step 6 — Invariance Condition** For the ratio to be **constant** for *all* positions of $N$ during the engagement, the point $K$ where the common normal meets the line of centers must remain **fixed**. The only point on the line of centers that is stationary for both gears is their intersection with the common internal tangent of the base circles — namely, the **pitch point $P$**. Hence, $K \equiv P$, and we obtain the celebrated law: $$\boxed{\dfrac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \dfrac{O_2P}{O_1P} = \text{constant}}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation of the Length of Path of Contact **Given:** - Gear 1 (pinion): addendum radius $r_{a1}$, base radius $r_{b1}$. - Gear 2 (gear): addendum radius $r_{a2}$, base radius $r_{b2}$. - Pressure angle $\phi$. - Engagement begins at $A$ (on addendum circle of gear 1) and ends at $B$ (on addendum circle of gear 2). **Step 1 — Geometric identification of $A$ and $B$** - Point $A$: intersection of the line of action with the addendum circle of the **pinion** ($r_{a1}$). - Point $B$: intersection of the line of action with the addendum circle of the **gear** ($r_{a2}$). **Step 2 — Path from $A$ to pitch point $P$** In right triangle $O_1AP$, the hypotenuse is $O_1A = r_{a1}$ and the angle at $P$ is $\phi$ (pressure angle). Therefore: $$AP = \sqrt{r_{a1}^{\,2} - r_{b1}^{\,2}}$$ This is because $O_1P \cos \phi = r_{b1}$ (since $P$ is on the base-circle tangent construction), and using the Pythagorean theorem on $\triangle O_1AP$: $$ \begin{aligned} AP &= \sqrt{(O_1A)^2 - (O_1P)^2} \\ &= \sqrt{r_{a1}^{\,2} - (r_{b1}/\cos\phi)^2 \cdot \cos^2\phi} \quad \text{[after geometry simplification]}\\ &= \sqrt{r_{a1}^{\,2} - r_{b1}^{\,2}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Path from $P$ to $B$** Similarly for the driven gear: $$PB = \sqrt{r_{a2}^{\,2} - r_{b2}^{\,2}}$$ **Step 4 — Total Path of Contact** Adding both segments: $$\boxed{L_{pc} = AP + PB = \sqrt{r_{a1}^{\,2} - r_{b1}^{\,2}} + \sqrt{r_{a2}^{\,2} - r_{b2}^{\,2}}}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation of Arc of Contact from Path of Contact **Step 1 — Geometric relation** The arc of contact $L_{ac}$ is the path of contact projected onto the pitch circle. The relationship between them is governed by the pressure angle $\phi$: $$ \begin{aligned} L_{ac} &= \dfrac{L_{pc}}{\cos \phi} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Justification** Consider the pinion pitch circle. The contact point $N$ traverses the line of action while the pinion rotates through angle $\theta_1$. The arc traced on the pitch circle is $r_1 \theta_1$. By projecting the line-of-action motion onto the pitch circle using the pressure angle, we get the factor $1/\cos \phi$. **Step 3 — Contact Ratio Final Form** $$\boxed{n_c = \dfrac{L_{ac}}{P_c} = \dfrac{\sqrt{r_{a1}^{\,2} - r_{b1}^{\,2}} + \sqrt{r_{a2}^{\,2} - r_{b2}^{\,2}}}{\pi \, m \, \cos \phi}}$$ For smooth continuous transmission, **$n_c \geq 1$**. In practice, KTU board problems often check whether $n_c \geq 1.4$ (recommended) for industrial applications. ## 3.4 Sample Numerical Pattern (For Concept Anchoring) > **Example Pattern (KTU-typical):** A pinion has 18 teeth, gear has 36 teeth, module = 4 mm, pressure angle = 20°. Compute the length of path of contact. **Step 1 — Pitch diameters** $D_1 = mT_1 = 4 \times 18 = 72 \text{ mm}$, $D_2 = 4 \times 36 = 144 \text{ mm}$ **Step 2 — Pitch radii** $r_1 = 36 \text{ mm}$, $r_2 = 72 \text{ mm}$ **Step 3 — Base radii** $r_{b1} = r_1 \cos 20° = 36 \times 0.9397 = 33.83 \text{ mm}$ $r_{b2} = r_2 \cos 20° = 72 \times 0.9397 = 67.66 \text{ mm}$ **Step 4 — Addendum radii (full depth)** $r_{a1} = r_1 + m = 40 \text{ mm}$, $r_{a2} = 72 + 4 = 76 \text{ mm}$ **Step 5 — Length of Path of Contact** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{pc} &= \sqrt{40^2 - 33.83^2} + \sqrt{76^2 - 67.66^2} \\ &= \sqrt{1600 - 1144.47} + \sqrt{5776 - 4577.88} \\ &= \sqrt{455.53} + \sqrt{1198.12} \\ &= 21.34 + 34.62 \\ &= 55.96 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Geometry of Involute Action ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Gear 1 Center O1
Pinion] --> B[Base Circle Radius rb1] A --> C[Addendum Circle Radius ra1] A --> D[Pitch Circle Radius r1] E[Gear 2 Center O2
Driven] --> F[Base Circle Radius rb2] E --> G[Addendum Circle Radius ra2] E --> H[Pitch Circle Radius r2] B -.Tangent to.-> I[Common Internal Tangent] F -.Tangent to.-> I I --> J[Pitch Point P
on line of centers] J --> K[Common Normal at Contact N
passes through P] K --> L[Contact Begins at A
on addendum of pinion] K --> M[Contact Ends at B
on addendum of gear] C --> L G --> M L --> N[Path of Contact Lpc] M --> N N --> O[Arc of Contact Lac = Lpc sec phi] O --> P[Contact Ratio nc = Lac / Pc] P --> Q{nc greater than 1?} Q -->|Yes| R[Smooth Continuous Transmission] Q -->|No| S[Intermittent Contact - Failure] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence: Velocity Ratio Invariance Mechanism ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant O1 as Pinion Center participant P as Pitch Point (Fixed) participant N as Contact Point participant O2 as Gear Center O1->>N: Tooth surface rotates N->>O2: Common normal drawn N->>P: Normal MUST pass through P Note over N,P: Common normal invariant O1->>O1: omega1 constant O2->>O2: omega2 constant O1->>O2: Ratio omega1/omega2 = O2P/O1P O2-->>O1: Constant velocity ratio maintained ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Involute Action Properties ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph GEOM[Geometric Foundation] G1[Base Circle Construction] G2[Involute Curve Generation] G3[Common Normal Invariance] end subgraph KINE[Kinematic Consequences] K1[Constant Velocity Ratio] K2[Constant Pressure Angle phi] K3[Zero Transmission Pulsation] end subgraph MECH[Mechanical Benefits] M1[Smooth Power Transfer] M2[Low Vibration and Noise] M3[High Load Capacity] end subgraph MFG[Manufacturing Advantages] F1[Rack-Cutter Compatibility] F2[Center Distance Tolerance] F3[Interchangeable Gears] end GEOM --> KINE KINE --> MECH KINE --> MFG ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The involute curve (geometric layer) directly *forces* the kinematic invariance (middle layer), which in turn *enables* both the mechanical benefits (right side) and the manufacturing flexibility (bottom). This 4-layer hierarchy is the standard KTU examiner's mental model for evaluating "characteristic" type questions. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (2-Mark Conceptual / 3-Mark Definitions) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question Paper]` **Q: State the "Law of Gearing" and explain its significance in the design of gear teeth.** **Model Answer:** The **Law of Gearing** (also called the **Fundamental Law of Conjugate Action**) states that: > *For a constant angular velocity ratio between two meshing gear teeth, the common normal at the point of contact must always pass through a fixed point (the pitch point) on the line of centers.* Mathematically: $\dfrac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \dfrac{O_2P}{O_1P} = \text{constant}$ **Significance:** 1. It is the **theoretical basis** for all involute and cycloidal tooth profiles. 2. It **eliminates pulsations** in velocity during engagement. 3. It ensures **uniform power transmission** without shocks. 4. It allows **interchangeability** of gears of the same module. 5. It forms the foundation for **standardized design** of gear teeth across industries. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **Q: List any four important characteristics of involute action as applied to gear teeth.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Constant pressure angle:** The pressure angle $\phi$ remains invariant throughout the period of engagement, ensuring uniform force transmission. 2. **Constant angular velocity ratio:** Satisfies the law of gearing — the common normal always passes through the fixed pitch point $P$. 3. **Straight line path of contact:** The path of contact is a straight line (line of action), tangent to both base circles — geometrically simple to construct. 4. **Insensitivity to center distance variation:** Small changes in center distance do not disturb the conjugate action; only the pressure angle changes slightly. 5. **Ease of manufacture:** Involute profiles can be cut by simple rack-type cutters and hobs. 6. **Interchangeability:** Gears of the same module but different tooth numbers can be paired. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 – Model Paper]` **(a)** Derive the expression for the **length of the path of contact** of a pair of meshing involute gear teeth. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Two involute spur gears in mesh have a module of 5 mm and a pressure angle of 20°. The pinion has 20 teeth and the gear has 40 teeth. Calculate: **(i)** Length of path of contact, **(ii)** Length of arc of contact, **(iii)** Contact ratio. Use full-depth teeth. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Assumptions:** 1. Teeth are of standard proportions (full-depth system, $a = m$, $d = 1.25m$). 2. The gears are mounted on fixed centers. 3. There is no interference at the addendum circles. 4. The profile is a true involute generated from the base circle. **Derivation:** Let the line of action intersect the addendum circle of the pinion at $A$ and the addendum circle of the gear at $B$. The pitch point $P$ lies between $A$ and $B$. In the right-angled triangle $O_1AP$ (with $O_1A = r_{a1}$ as hypotenuse and $O_1P = r_{b1}/\cos\phi$ as the projection along the line of centers): $$ \begin{aligned} AP &= \sqrt{O_1A^2 - O_1P^2} \\ &= \sqrt{r_{a1}^{\,2} - \left(\dfrac{r_{b1}}{\cos\phi}\right)^2 \cdot \cos^2\phi} \\ &= \sqrt{r_{a1}^{\,2} - r_{b1}^{\,2}} \end{aligned} $$ Similarly, in $\triangle O_2BP$: $$PB = \sqrt{r_{a2}^{\,2} - r_{b2}^{\,2}}$$ Therefore, the total length of path of contact is: $$\boxed{L_{pc} = AP + PB = \sqrt{r_{a1}^{\,2} - r_{b1}^{\,2}} + \sqrt{r_{a2}^{\,2} - r_{b2}^{\,2}}}$$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** $m = 5 \text{ mm}$, $\phi = 20°$, $T_1 = 20$, $T_2 = 40$. **Step 1 — Pitch radii** $$r_1 = \dfrac{mT_1}{2} = \dfrac{5 \times 20}{2} = 50 \text{ mm}$$ $$r_2 = \dfrac{mT_2}{2} = \dfrac{5 \times 40}{2} = 100 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2 — Base radii** ($r_b = r \cos\phi$) $$r_{b1} = 50 \cos 20° = 50 \times 0.9397 = 46.985 \text{ mm}$$ $$r_{b2} = 100 \cos 20° = 100 \times 0.9397 = 93.970 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 3 — Addendum radii (full depth, $a = m = 5 \text{ mm}$)** $$r_{a1} = 50 + 5 = 55 \text{ mm}, \quad r_{a2} = 100 + 5 = 105 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 4 — Length of Path of Contact** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{pc} &= \sqrt{55^2 - 46.985^2} + \sqrt{105^2 - 93.970^2} \\ &= \sqrt{3025 - 2207.6} + \sqrt{11025 - 8830.4} \\ &= \sqrt{817.4} + \sqrt{2194.6} \\ &= 28.59 + 46.85 \\ &= 75.44 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Length of Arc of Contact** $$L_{ac} = \dfrac{L_{pc}}{\cos 20°} = \dfrac{75.44}{0.9397} = 80.28 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 6 — Contact Ratio** $$P_c = \pi m = \pi \times 5 = 15.708 \text{ mm}$$ $$n_c = \dfrac{L_{ac}}{P_c} = \dfrac{80.28}{15.708} = 5.11$$ **Conclusion:** Since $n_c = 5.11 \gg 1$, the gears provide excellent continuous tooth contact. --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative to Question A) **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **path of contact** and **arc of contact** for a pair of meshing involute spur gears. Define **contact ratio** and state its physical significance. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A pinion with 24 teeth drives a gear with 56 teeth. The module is 3 mm and the pressure angle is 20°. The addenda on both gears are equal to one module. Determine: **(i)** the length of path of contact, **(ii)** the contact ratio, and **(iii)** whether the gears will interfere. Justify your answer. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Diagram (described in words):** Draw two circles with centers $O_1$ and $O_2$ — the pitch circles, touching at the pitch point $P$ on the line of centers. Inside each, draw the base circle ($r_b = r \cos\phi$). Draw the common internal tangent to both base circles; this tangent line is the **line of action (path of contact)**. It cuts the addendum circle of the pinion at $A$ and the addendum circle of the gear at $B$. The line of action is inclined at angle $\phi$ to the common tangent at $P$. **Definitions:** - **Path of Contact ($L_{pc}$):** The locus of the point of contact between two meshing teeth, measured as a straight line on the line of action from $A$ to $B$. Formula: $L_{pc} = \sqrt{r_{a1}^2 - r_{b1}^2} + \sqrt{r_{a2}^2 - r_{b2}^2}$. - **Arc of Contact ($L_{ac}$):** The arc traced by a point on the pitch circle from the start of engagement to the end of engagement. Formula: $L_{ac} = L_{pc} / \cos\phi$. - **Contact Ratio ($n_c$):** The ratio of the arc of contact to the circular pitch. Formula: $n_c = L_{ac} / P_c$. **Physical Significance:** The contact ratio represents the **average number of tooth pairs in contact** at any instant. If $n_c \geq 1$, at least one pair is always in contact, ensuring smooth, continuous transmission. For quiet operation, $n_c \geq 1.4$ is recommended. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** $T_1 = 24$, $T_2 = 56$, $m = 3 \text{ mm}$, $\phi = 20°$, $a = 1 \cdot m = 3 \text{ mm}$. **Step 1 — Pitch radii** $$r_1 = \dfrac{3 \times 24}{2} = 36 \text{ mm}, \quad r_2 = \dfrac{3 \times 56}{2} = 84 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2 — Base radii** $$r_{b1} = 36 \cos 20° = 33.83 \text{ mm}, \quad r_{b2} = 84 \cos 20° = 78.94 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 3 — Addendum radii** $$r_{a1} = 36 + 3 = 39 \text{ mm}, \quad r_{a2} = 84 + 3 = 87 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 4 — Length of Path of Contact** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{pc} &= \sqrt{39^2 - 33.83^2} + \sqrt{87^2 - 78.94^2} \\ &= \sqrt{1521 - 1144.47} + \sqrt{7569 - 6231.5} \\ &= \sqrt{376.53} + \sqrt{1337.5} \\ &= 19.40 + 36.57 \\ &= 55.97 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Contact Ratio** $$L_{ac} = \dfrac{55.97}{\cos 20°} = \dfrac{55.97}{0.9397} = 59.56 \text{ mm}$$ $$P_c = \pi \times 3 = 9.425 \text{ mm}$$ $$n_c = \dfrac{59.56}{9.425} = 6.32$$ **Step 6 — Interference Check** For a $20°$ pressure angle and full-depth teeth, the **minimum number of teeth to avoid interference on the pinion is 17**. Here $T_1 = 24 > 17$, so **no interference** will occur. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert $\phi$ to radians** when computing $L_{ac}$ — the formula $L_{ac} = L_{pc}/\cos\phi$ uses $\phi$ in *degrees* when $\cos$ is called; do NOT use $\tan$ or $\sin$ by mistake. > 2. **Confusing addendum circle with pitch circle** when computing $L_{pc}$ — the addendum circle (not the pitch circle) is where contact begins/ends. > 3. **Mixing up pinion and gear values** — the path of contact has two segments: $AP$ (pinion side) and $PB$ (gear side); both must be computed and summed. > 4. **Dropping units in final answer** — KTU strictly deducts 0.5 marks if units (mm) are missing. > 5. **Forgetting the contact ratio interpretation** — many students compute $n_c$ but fail to state whether it is $\geq 1$ (which the examiner explicitly asks in nearly every board paper). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Print this section and paste it on your study wall — these are the KTU 2024 examiner's favourite triggers:** - [ ] **Involute curve** is generated by unwinding a string from the base circle. The string is always tangent to the base circle, and its length equals the arc unwound. - [ ] **Law of Gearing** = common normal at $N$ passes through fixed pitch point $P$ ⇒ $\omega_1/\omega_2 = O_2P/O_1P = $ constant. - [ ] **Path of Contact** is **straight** (line of action) — tangent to *both* base circles, inclined at $\phi$ to the common tangent at $P$. - [ ] **Arc of Contact** is on the pitch circle and is *longer* than the path of contact by a factor of $1/\cos\phi$. - [ ] **Base circle radius**: $r_b = r\cos\phi$ — this is the *only* circle from which the involute is generated. - [ ] **Pressure angle is constant** for involute gears (the killer advantage over cycloidal). - [ ] **Center distance variation** is *tolerated* — small errors do not break conjugate action; they merely change $\phi$ slightly. - [ ] **Standard pressure angle** is **$20°$** (KTU 2024 default; older textbooks use $14.5°$). - [ ] **Standard module series** (preferred): 1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20 mm. - [ ] **Circular pitch** $P_c = \pi m$. - [ ] **Contact ratio $n_c \geq 1$** is the *minimum* for continuous transmission; $n_c \geq 1.4$ is *recommended* for industrial use. - [ ] **Minimum teeth to avoid interference** with $20°$ full depth: $T_{\min} = 17$ on the pinion. - [ ] **Involute action applies to spur, helical, and bevel gears** — *not* directly to worm gears (which use a different geometry). - [ ] The **arc of contact consists of two parts**: arc of approach (before $P$) and arc of recess (after $P$). - [ ] **Sliding velocity is maximum** at the pitch point $P$ and zero at the tips of the addendum circles. - [ ] The **rolling velocity is zero** at $P$ and maximum at $A$ and $B$ — so contact is *pure rolling* at $P$ and *pure sliding* at $A$ and $B$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Mnemonic for the Board Exam:** **"I-C-L-A-P"** — > **I**nvolute curve, **C**onjugate law (path of contact), **L**aw of gearing (constant $\omega$ ratio), **A**rc of contact (longer, on pitch circle), **P**ressure angle constant.

topics.title: Analysis of Gear Trains
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT412 - KINEMATICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Gear Trains

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Gear Train** is a mechanical system formed by the meshing of two or more gears to transmit rotary motion and power from one shaft to another, where the axes of rotation may be stationary (ordinary gear train) or moving (epicyclic gear train).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, gear train analysis is the study of the **kinematic relationships**—speed, direction of rotation, and mechanical advantage—between the input and output members of such a coupled gear system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> Gear trains are classified into four primary categories that form the foundation of every numerical and conceptual question in this module:
> 1. **Simple Gear Train** — Each shaft carries only one gear.
> 2. ****Compound Gear Train** — One or more intermediate shafts carry two gears.
> 3. **Reverted Gear Train** — Two shafts are coaxial; driven and driver gears on the same shaft.
> 4. **Epicyclic (Planetary) Gear Train** — At least one gear axis moves relative to a fixed frame.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine riding a **bicycle**. When you shift to a lower gear at the pedals, the rear wheel rotates many times for every single turn of the pedals — this *reduction* is achieved by a **simple gear train** at work (chainring → sprocket). 

Now imagine an **automatic car gearbox**: it must produce multiple forward speeds, reverse, and neutral, all in a compact housing. It uses a **compound gear train** with multiple intermediate shafts. 

Finally, imagine a **planetary gearbox** in a helicopter or a rear-wheel-drive car differential. Here, the small pinion gears are not fixed in space — they "orbit" around a central sun gear while spinning. This is an **epicyclic gear train**, offering high torque density in a very small package.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Gear Trains Matter in Engineering:**
> Gear trains are the backbone of nearly every power transmission system — from wind turbines and CNC machines to automotive differentials and robotics. Mastering gear train analysis is essential because **the speed ratio of an entire machine is governed by this single kinematic chain**.

## 1.3 Terminology & Standard Metrics

- **Driver Gear (Pinion):** The gear that receives power from the source (smaller in a reduction setup).
- **Driven Gear (Wheel/Gear):** The gear that delivers power to the next stage.
- **Train Value (TV):** The ratio of output speed to input speed, equal to the product of driven teeth divided by the product of driver teeth.
- **Module (m):** A standard metric size defined as $m = \frac{D}{T}$, where $D$ is the pitch circle diameter and $T$ is the number of teeth.
- **Pitch Circle:** The imaginary circle on which two meshing gears roll without slipping.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Gear Trains — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Simple Gear Train
- **Configuration:** Each shaft carries only **one gear**.
- **Step-up / Step-down:** Determined by whether the driven gear is larger or smaller than the driver.
- **Direction:** Two external gears in mesh rotate in **opposite directions**. With an idler, the driver and final driven rotate in the **same direction**.

### 2.1.2 Compound Gear Train
- **Configuration:** Intermediate shafts carry **two gears** rigidly fixed together, each meshing with another gear on adjacent shafts.
- **Purpose:** Achieves **large speed reductions** in compact space by cascading two simple reductions.
- **Direction:** Same logic as simple trains, but with an extra gear pair added.

### 2.1.3 Reverted Gear Train
- **Configuration:** Input and output shafts are **coaxial** (lie on the same axis). Two gear pairs are used, with gears mounted on the same shaft.
- **Purpose:** The classic **automotive manual gearbox** layout; allows multiple ratios without lengthening the gearbox.
- **Special feature:** The center-distance of the two gear pairs must match for the gears to mesh properly.

### 2.1.4 Epicyclic (Planetary) Gear Train
- **Configuration:** At least one gear (the **planet**) revolves around another gear (the **sun**) while simultaneously rotating about its own axis. A third member, called the **arm (carrier)**, holds the planet axes.
- **Purpose:** Provides multiple speed ratios, torque multiplication, and reversal using a single compact unit.
- **Key members:** Sun gear, Planet gear, Ring (annulus) gear, and Arm (carrier).
- **Why it's special:** It has **three degrees of freedom**; any two members can be fixed or driven, producing different outputs.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Gear Train Type | Key Formula | Symbol Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Simple Gear Train** | $i = \frac{N_1}{N_{last}} = \frac{T_2 \cdot T_4 \cdots T_n}{T_1 \cdot T_3 \cdots T_{n-1}}$ | $N$ = speed (rpm), $T$ = teeth |
| 2 | **Compound Gear Train** | Same form as simple, with intermediate shaft products | Each shaft's gears rotate together |
| 3 | **Reverted Gear Train** | $i = \frac{N_{in}}{N_{out}} = \frac{\text{Product of driven teeth}}{\text{Product of driver teeth}}$ | Plus center-distance constraint: $m_1(T_a+T_b) = m_2(T_c+T_d)$ |
| 4 | **Train Value (TV)** | $TV = \frac{N_{driver}}{N_{driven}} = \frac{T_{driven}}{T_{driver}}$ | Output rpm / Input rpm |
| 5 | **Epicyclic Fundamental** | $\frac{N_L - N_{arm}}{N_F - N_{arm}} = r$ | $N_L$ = last gear, $N_F$ = first gear, $r$ = ratio when arm is fixed |
| 6 | **Sign Convention (Epicyclic)** | Same direction: $+$; Opposite direction: $-$ | Applied to the fixed-arm ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** For epicyclic gear trains, KTU examiners expect students to clearly state the **sign convention** before solving. Always write: *"Assume clockwise rotation as positive."*

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive differentials** use epicyclic trains for splitting torque between two wheels while allowing them to rotate at different speeds during cornering.
- **Wind turbine gearboxes** use compound gear trains to step up rotor speed (15–20 rpm) to generator speed (1500 rpm).
- **Robotic joints** use harmonic drives (a special type of epicyclic train) for zero-backlash, high-ratio motion control.
- **Helicopter transmissions** use reverted/epicyclic trains to redirect power by 90° from the engine to the rotor.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Speed Ratio of a Simple Gear Train

Consider gears 1 and 2 in mesh on parallel shafts. The pitch line velocity must be equal at the point of contact (no slipping):

$$
\pi D_1 N_1 = \pi D_2 N_2
$$

Since module $m$ is the same for meshing gears, $D_1 = m T_1$ and $D_2 = m T_2$:

$$
m T_1 N_1 = m T_2 N_2
$$

$$
\frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{T_2}{T_1}
$$

**Generalizing** to $n$ gears in series, where odd-numbered gears are drivers and even-numbered are driven:

$$
\frac{N_1}{N_n} = \frac{T_2 \cdot T_4 \cdot T_6 \cdots T_n}{T_1 \cdot T_3 \cdot T_5 \cdots T_{n-1}}
$$

> **Logic:** Each successive gear pair multiplies the ratio, so the overall ratio is the product of all individual ratios.

## 3.2 Derivation: Reverted Gear Train — Center Distance Constraint

In a reverted gear train, gear **A** (driver on input shaft) meshes with gear **B** on an intermediate shaft. Gear **C** (on the same intermediate shaft, rigidly fixed to B) meshes with gear **D** on the output shaft. The output shaft is coaxial with the input shaft.

Center distance between input and intermediate shafts:

$$
C_1 = \frac{D_A + D_B}{2} = \frac{m_1(T_A + T_B)}{2}
$$

Center distance between intermediate and output shafts (same physical distance as $C_1$ because shafts are coaxial):

$$
C_2 = \frac{D_C + D_D}{2} = \frac{m_2(T_C + T_D)}{2}
$$

For the gears to mesh correctly without interference, $C_1 = C_2$:

$$
m_1(T_A + T_B) = m_2(T_C + T_D)
$$

**This is a frequently asked KTU question** — students often forget this constraint when solving for tooth numbers.

## 3.3 Derivation: Fundamental Equation of an Epicyclic Gear Train

Consider a simple epicyclic train with sun gear $S$, planet gear $P$, and arm $A$. The planet gear rotates about its own axis AND revolves around the sun.

**Step 1 — Fix the arm:** Imagine the arm is locked. Then it behaves as an ordinary gear train, and we can compute a fixed-arm ratio:

$$
r = \frac{N_{last}}{N_{first}} \bigg|_{arm \ fixed}
$$

For a sun-planet mesh: $r = -\frac{T_P}{T_S}$ (negative because external mesh reverses direction).

**Step 2 — Add arm motion:** Let the arm rotate at $N_A$. Now give the *entire gear train* a rotation of $+N_A$ (i.e., superimpose arm motion on every member). Each member's new speed is:

$$
N_{member,\ new} = N_{member,\ old} + N_A
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the kinematic relationship:** The relative velocities between members must still satisfy the fixed-arm ratio $r$. Expressing this in terms of the new (absolute) speeds:

$$
\frac{N_{last} - N_A}{N_{first} - N_A} = r
$$

This is the **fundamental equation of an epicyclic gear train**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention:**
> - If two gears mesh externally, their rotational senses are **opposite** → use **negative** sign.
> - If they mesh internally (ring gear), the senses are **same** → use **positive** sign.

## 3.4 Analytical Solution Matrix — Worked Examples

### Example 1: Simple Gear Train
Given: $T_1 = 20$, $T_2 = 40$, $T_3 = 30$, $T_4 = 60$, $N_1 = 100$ rpm. Find $N_4$.

$$
i = \frac{N_1}{N_4} = \frac{T_2 \cdot T_4}{T_1 \cdot T_3} = \frac{40 \times 60}{20 \times 30} = \frac{2400}{600} = 4
$$

$$
N_4 = \frac{N_1}{i} = \frac{100}{4} = 25 \text{ rpm}
$$

### Example 2: Epicyclic Gear Train (Automotive Differential Type)
Given: Sun gear S has 20 teeth, Planet P has 30 teeth, Ring R has 80 teeth. Arm is driven at 100 rpm CW. Sun is fixed. Find the ring gear speed.

**Step 1:** Compute fixed-arm ratio (arm locked, sun fixed, ring is output):

$$
r = \frac{N_R - N_S}{N_S - N_S}\bigg|_{arm\ fixed}
$$

Wait — when arm is fixed and sun is the driver:

$$
r = \frac{N_R}{N_S}\bigg|_{arm\ fixed} = \frac{T_S}{T_R} \times (-1) \times \frac{T_P}{T_P}
$$

Actually, for sun→planet→ring via the same planet gear meshing both externally and internally:

$$
\frac{N_R}{N_S} = +\frac{T_S \cdot T_P}{T_P \cdot T_R} = \frac{T_S}{T_R}
$$

(positive because one mesh is internal — ring is internal teeth)

$$
r = \frac{20}{80} = 0.25
$$

**Step 2:** Apply epicyclic fundamental equation. Let $N_S = 0$ (fixed), $N_A = 100$ rpm CW, find $N_R$:

$$
\frac{N_R - N_A}{N_S - N_A} = r
$$

$$
\frac{N_R - 100}{0 - 100} = 0.25
$$

$$
N_R - 100 = -25
$$

$$
N_R = 75 \text{ rpm (CW)}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Classification of Gear Trains

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the hierarchical classification of gear trains as per the KTU 2024 Module 3 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootNodeA["Gear Trains"] --> rootNodeB["Ordinary Gear Train"]
    rootNodeA --> rootNodeC["Epicyclic Gear Train"]

    rootNodeB --> rootNodeD["Simple Gear Train"]
    rootNodeB --> rootNodeE["Compound Gear Train"]
    rootNodeB --> rootNodeF["Reverted Gear Train"]

    rootNodeD --> rootNodeD1["One gear per shaft"]
    rootNodeD --> rootNodeD2["Direction reverses per mesh"]

    rootNodeE --> rootNodeE1["Two gears on intermediate shaft"]
    rootNodeE --> rootNodeE2["Used for large reductions"]

    rootNodeF --> rootNodeF1["Input and output coaxial"]
    rootNodeF --> rootNodeF2["Used in automotive gearboxes"]

    rootNodeC --> rootNodeG["Sun Planet Ring Arm"]
    rootNodeC --> rootNodeH["Differential Gearbox"]
    rootNodeC --> rootNodeI["Planetary Speed Reducer"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Epicyclic Analysis Methods

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically lists three analytical techniques. The diagram below shows the decision logic a student should follow when solving an epicyclic problem.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    methodA["Epicyclic Problem Given"] --> methodB["Identify first gear last gear and arm"]
    methodB --> methodC["Compute fixed arm ratio r"]
    methodC --> methodD["Apply N_L minus N_arm over N_F minus N_arm equals r"]
    methodD --> methodE["Substitute known speeds and sign convention"]
    methodE --> methodF["Solve for the unknown member speed"]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Power Flow in a Compound Gear Train

The block diagram below illustrates the energy and motion flow in a compound gear train used in industrial reducers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    block1["Input Shaft RPM N1"] --> block2["Driver Gear A on Input Shaft"]
    block2 --> block3["Driven Gear B on Intermediate Shaft 1"]
    block3 --> block4["Driver Gear C on Intermediate Shaft 1"]
    block4 --> block5["Driven Gear D on Output Shaft"]
    block5 --> block6["Output RPM N Last"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** In a physical textbook, this would be drawn as two meshing gear pairs with two parallel shafts. The Mermaid block diagram above conveys the same functional signal flow clearly.
